experiments.title: Does Matching Landing Page Imagery to Meta Ad Creative Increase Ebook Signups and Donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: If the landing page includes imagery that matches the creative used in Meta ads, the resulting visual continuity may reinforce message relevance and reduce cognitive friction for visitors arriving from those ads. This alignment could increase the likelihood that visitors complete the ebook signup and potentially make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2026 - 02/28/2026
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization dedicated to advancing scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal of this test was to evaluate whether including an image on the ebook landing page that matched the creative used in Meta ads would increase email acquisitions and donations. All other elements of the landing page remained unchanged. As with the previous test, traffic to the page came from multiple channels, including Meta, Performance Max, email, and email prospecting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Matching Image)
Visitors: 20,209Signups: 2,367Signup Rate: 11.71%Gifts: 118Revenue: $5,977.40Response Rate: 0.58%RPM: $295.80
Treatment (Matching Image Added)
Visitors: 20,319Signups: 1,481Signup Rate: 7.29%Gifts: 58Revenue: $2,708.00Response Rate: 0.29%RPM: $133.30
What We Observed
The control significantly outperformed the treatment across all key metrics.
Compared to the treatment, the control generated:
+886 signups+60 gifts+$3,269.40 in revenue
The treatment saw substantial declines in:
Signup rate (↓ ~38%)Response rate (↓ ~50%)Revenue per thousand visitors (↓ ~55%)
Why We Think This Happened
The added image likely disrupted the landing page’s visual clarity and diverted attention away from the primary conversion action.
Because traffic came from multiple channels, many visitors likely did not have the context of the Meta ad the image was intended to match. Without that context, the image functioned more as a distraction than a reinforcement.
In contrast, the control page maintained:
A simpler, cleaner layoutStronger visual hierarchyClear focus on the primary action
Next Steps
Test minimal landing page designs vs. image-heavy versions to confirm whether simpler layouts consistently drive stronger conversions.Evaluate placing images below the signup form so they support the offer without distracting from the primary action.Develop channel-specific landing pages so Meta visitors see matched creative while other channels see a simplified version optimized for conversion.

experiments.title: How removing a form disclaimer from a content offer landing page impacts sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the form disclaimer for 2026 Calendar landing page visitors will increase sign-up rates by eliminating unnecessary friction and reducing distractions, thereby preventing users from being drawn away from the form and allowing them to focus solely on completing their request.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2026 - 02/03/2026
Each year, IMB runs a Calendar acquisition offer that consistently performs well. The campaign follows a standard funnel: users land on a page to request the calendar, then are redirected to a donation page to complete their gift.
This year, we noticed a new addition to the landing page—a small form disclaimer that was not present last year. Although subtle, the change raised concerns about whether it might be creating friction and negatively impacting conversion.
To evaluate its effect, we ran an A/B test over a two-week period, splitting traffic evenly between the version with the disclaimer and the version without it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the form disclaimer from the landing page for the 2026 Calendar acquisition offer led to a 10.1% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. This change was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating a clear impact on user behavior. Moving forward, it's important to remember that reducing unnecessary friction and distractions can improve conversion rates. By simplifying the user experience and allowing visitors to focus solely on completing their request, we can potentially see higher engagement and sign-up rates. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different elements on the landing page to further optimize the conversion process. This could include testing different forms of messaging, images, layouts, or additional features to see what resonates best with users. By consistently running experiments and analyzing results, we can continue to improve the effectiveness of our campaigns and drive better outcomes for our nonprofit.

experiments.title: Testing Form Friction: Will Requiring a Phone Number on a Content Offer Landing Page Hurt Sign-Ups from Facebook Traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that requiring a mobile phone number on the sign-up form for a Facebook video series acquisition offer will result in a small drop in conversions but more valuable contacts because motivated users will tolerate the added friction in exchange for high-value content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2025 - 11/11/2025
Focus on the Family has been working to grow their SMS program and wanted to test the impact of requiring a mobile phone number on a content sign-up form.
We decided to test this on a free video series titled Contagious Joy, which was being promoted on Facebook with acquisition ads. Visitors who clicked the ad would land on a sign-up page with a form asking for First Name, Last Name, and Email Address.
In the Treatment version of the page, a fourth required field—Mobile Phone Number—was added. All other elements of the page remained the same.
The test was set up as a split URL experiment, with 50% of traffic directed to the control version and 50% to the treatment.
This test aimed to answer a key question: How much friction can we introduce in the name of better data collection—without meaningfully hurting conversion rates?
We did expect a potential drop in sign-ups due to the added field but hypothesized that the value of acquiring phone numbers would outweigh the slight loss in volume.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results we saw were surprising. We had anticipated a slight drop in email conversions, but didn’t expect it to be as significant as it was. Requiring a phone number led to a 26.4% decrease in email sign-ups, with a 99.9% level of confidence—clear evidence that this added field created too much friction for users coming from Facebook ads.
We also saw an almost statistically significant 40% drop in transactions on the post-sign-up donation page (92.2% LOC), suggesting the impact extended beyond the initial form. While average gift amount and recurring donor conversion didn’t change, the decrease in overall donor volume is still meaningful.
For this acquisition audience, the added requirement of a phone number appeared to outweigh the perceived value of the free video series. This test shows that asking for too much, too soon—especially from new visitors—can hurt both lead generation and potential revenue.
Going forward, it may be more effective to collect phone numbers later in the journey such as on the donation page, once trust has been built or value has been delivered. Or test adding the Phone Number field on the sign-up page but do not make it required to try and reduce friction.

experiments.title: How a more detailed and benefit-focused landing page headline impacts the sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline for Talking Sense e-book landing page visitors will achieve an increase in the sign up rate because it clearly communicates both the value of the ebook and the immediacy of access, making it more appealing and actionable to the visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2025 - 02/21/2025
In an effort to increase sign-up rates on MPR’s Talking Sense e-book landing page, we conducted a headline test designed to more effectively communicate the value of the offer to visitors. Our goal was to determine whether a more explicit, benefit-driven headline would lead to higher engagement and conversions.
Control: Free E-book: Have Hard Political Conversations, Better
Treatment: Get instant access to this free e-book “Talking Sense” today to have better political conversations
*This test ran on both Talking Sense funnels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline for the Talking Sense e-book landing page resulted in a 5.9% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This increase was statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.5%, indicating that the treatment version outperformed the control. Based on these results, we recommend rolling out the improved headline to all traffic.

These findings reinforce that MPR’s audience engages more when the benefits are clearly articulated.

Furthermore, the treatment also led to a 12.9% increase in donations at a 67.1% LOC. While this metric did not meet our standard of 95% validity, it does suggest that the treatment motivated more visitors to make donations over the control.

Building on this success, our next tests will explore how refining the CTA and button clarity can further enhance conversions.

experiments.title: Can a Streamlined Donation Path Improve Results for a Content Offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: By removing the initial email capture step and directing high-intent visitors straight to the donation page via a slide-up prompt, we believe we can reduce conversion friction, shorten the donor journey, and increase overall donation revenue without significantly sacrificing lead generation for the digital magazine.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/25/2025
This experiment was conducted for Catholic Extension Society (CES), a nonprofit organization that supports Catholic faith communities in under-resourced areas. The campaign centered around a special content offer tied to Extension, CES’s quarterly magazine. This particular edition was a commemorative issue celebrating the newly appointed pope.
The primary offer allowed supporters to sign up to receive the digital edition when it becomes available. Additionally, anyone who donated $35 or more would receive a physical copy of the magazine as a thank-you gift.
The experiment aimed to test whether adding a slide-up that bypassed the digital sign-up step—and instead directed visitors straight to the donation page—would reduce friction and result in more donations.
This test was split 50/50, and everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment compared two user pathways to drive donations in exchange for a commemorative edition of Extension magazine:
Control: A landing page that promoted sign-up for the digital magazine, with an optional donation ask (gift array started below $35).
 	Variant (Direct Ask): A slide-up that bypassed the digital sign-up and directed users straight to a donation page (gift array starting at $35).
Key Takeaways
The Control significantly outperformed the Direct Ask in donor conversion rate—5.4% vs. 1.3%, representing a 75.4% drop in performance for the variant.
 	While the Direct Ask page aimed to remove friction by sending visitors straight to a donation form, it appears to have disrupted the user motivation flow. The digital magazine offer likely served as a stronger value proposition and entry point, especially for cold or less-committed visitors.
 	Despite a slightly higher average gift on the Direct Ask page, the sharp drop in conversion volume led to dramatically lower total revenue and engagement.
Insights & Interpretation
Donation Volume: The control page drove nearly 4x more qualified gifts (177 vs. 45) than the direct ask page. This suggests that leading with the magazine offer and allowing users to choose whether to donate afterward may generate more overall engagement and gift volume.
 	Average Gift Size: While the direct ask page had a higher average gift size ($48 vs. $42.71), the lift was not enough to offset the substantial decrease in total conversions.
 	Total Revenue: The control page significantly outperformed in total revenue from $35+ gifts, generating $7,560 vs. $1,680—a 350%+ increase over the direct ask version.
 	Friction Tradeoff: Although the direct ask aimed to reduce friction by shortening the donor journey, it may have missed the motivational hook provided by the digital content offer, resulting in lower engagement and fewer conversions overall.
Conclusion
substantially more revenue and donor conversions
leading with a high-value content offer

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins

rows.name: Remove Form Frame + Condensed Opt-In language/Preselect

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Today in the Word acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 07/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Today in the Word’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
Control: Stay grounded in your faith through God’s Word! Get our Daily Devotional and Bible-centered resources from Today in the Word! Sign Me Up!
Treatment: “I want to stay grounded in faith through God’s Word! Send me the Daily Devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the opt-in copy and removing the form frame on the Today in the Word acquisition landing page led to a significant decrease in opt-ins for all traffic, with a 46.8% decrease at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence. This indicates that the treatment did not resonate well with visitors and may have caused confusion or hesitation in opting in for Bible-centric content and resources from Moody Radio.
Furthermore, the experiment also showed a considerable decrease in One-Time Donations, Transactions, and Revenue, with decreases ranging from 758% to 764% at statistical confidence levels of 96% to 100%. This suggests that the changes made to the opt-in process not only impacted the opt-ins but also other key metrics related to donations and transactions.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of simplifying messaging and form layouts on user behavior and conversion rates. While streamlining the opt-in process may work in some cases, it is crucial to test and validate these changes thoroughly before implementation. Additionally, it is essential to prioritize user experience and ensure that any modifications to the acquisition funnels are user-friendly and clear to visitors to avoid any potential drop in engagement or conversions.

experiments.title: How an exit intent on single post pages affects comments
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent popup for single post page visitors will achieve an increase in comments on a post because the copy in the exit intent encouraged the user to leave a comment, highlighting the importance of leaving a comment for the author.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2025 - 08/01/2025
For CaringBridge, you can go to a page that has a single post on it if you want to go to a specific post. In an effort to encourage users to leave comments on posts, we wanted to test having an exit intent popup with copy that would encourage a user to leave a comment for the author on their post. This experiment targeted desktop users. The exit intent was shown on pages with posts where a user hadn’t commented on the post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:
1. The addition of an exit intent popup for single post page visitors led to a significant increase in comments on a post by 9.6% with a high level of statistical confidence (99.9%). This indicates that the copy in the exit intent, which encouraged users to leave a comment for the author, was effective in driving user engagement.
2. Revenue - Tribute (JEN) also experienced a substantial increase of 17.2% with a 100% level of confidence. This shows that not only did the exit intent popup result in more user engagement in the form of comments, but it also positively impacted revenue generation for single post page tribute widget.
3. The results suggest that prompting users to leave comments through an exit intent popup can be a successful strategy for increasing user engagement and potentially driving revenue for Caringbridge.
We also saw a positive increase in tribute donations, which would indicate that the presence of the exit intent did not discourage users to make a tribute gift, but rather encouraged users to make a gift.
Future Experimentation Recommendations:
1. Implement similar exit intent popups on other pages of the website to test if this strategy can be scaled across different sections for further increasing user engagement and revenue.
2. Test different variations of the exit intent copy to optimize the messaging and further enhance its effectiveness in encouraging users to leave comments and contribute towards the nonprofit's objectives.
3. Explore the possibility of personalizing the exit intent popups based on user behavior or demographics to make the messaging more relevant and compelling to different audience segments.
4. Consider conducting qualitative research, such as user feedback surveys or interviews, to gain deeper insights into user preferences and attitudes towards the exit intent popups, helping to refine the strategy for future implementations.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image on a petition landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad will increase the email acquisition rate because the image provides continuity for people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2025 - 05/29/2025
Mountain States Legal Foundation protects property rights and economic liberty, defends the right to keep and bear arms, champions free speech and association, and upholds the principle of equal protection.
We ran this test on an instant donation page from a petition. Audience: People from social media ads.
We wanted to know if more people will donate because people see an image on the ad and then the same image on the landing page. We thought this would provide continuity between the channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions and recurring donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of image continuity between different channels. While the hypothesis was based on the idea that continuity could enhance the user experience and engagement, the results indicate that this may not always be the case.

Moving forward, it is crucial to test hypotheses thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. This can help avoid potential negative impacts on donation rates or other key metrics. Additionally, it is important to analyze the results objectively and be prepared to pivot strategies based on data-driven insights, even if they contradict initial assumptions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of experimentation and data analysis in optimizing donation pages and marketing strategies. It is essential to continuously learn from both successful and unsuccessful experiments to improve future campaigns and drive better outcomes for the nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How redesigning Thinkific landing pages impacts course sign ups (Bible Study Methods)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the updated landing page design for landing page visitors will achieve more course sign ups because it reduces friction on the page. because  it reduces friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2025 - 07/09/2025
DTS uses Thinkific for their online course software. Thinkific has a native course sign up that requires a user to “check out” to sign up for a course, even though the courses are free. Our contact at DTS built a custom landing page that we believed would reduce friction on the page. It reduced the number of steps a course taker would have to take to sign up and moved the sign up form to be directly on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
The updated landing page design, which featured the sign up form directly on the landing page, led to an 11.4% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. This result indicates that reducing friction on the page by simplifying the sign-up process can positively impact user engagement and conversions.To further optimize the sign-up process, future experiments could explore different variations of the landing page design, such as testing the placement of the sign-up form, the design elements, or the messaging to find the ideal balance between user experience and business outcomes.Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is the importance of balancing user experience improvements with the impact on key business metrics, and using data-driven insights to inform decision-making in future experimentation.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the updated landing page design for landing page visitors will achieve more course sign ups because it reduces friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2025 - 07/09/2025
DTS built a custom landing page that we believed would reduce friction on the page. It reduced the number of steps a course taker would have to take to sign up and moved the sign up form to be directly on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that the updated landing page design with the sign-up form directly on the page was successful in reducing friction and ultimately led to a significant increase in course sign-ups and revenue for Dallas Theological Seminary. This experiment demonstrates the importance of minimizing steps and reducing friction in the sign-up process to improve conversion rates. The success of this approach suggests that future experimentation should focus on streamlining the user experience and making it easier for visitors to take the desired action. Additionally, it highlights the significance of testing different designs and layouts to identify what resonates best with the target audience.

Moving forward, Dallas Theological Seminary should continue to prioritize user-friendly design elements and conduct further A/B testing to optimize their online course sign-up process. By leveraging the insights gained from this experiment, the organization can continue to enhance conversions, increase revenue, and ultimately provide a better experience for their audience.

experiments.title: How anonymity on a landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the option to send a note anonymously for Note of Encouragement landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it reduces the perceived pressure and lowers the barrier for participation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2025 - 06/30/2025
In an effort to increase the conversion rate on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested allowing users to mark their message as anonymous, allowing them to send their message without their name attached.
Our goal was that this treatment would allow visitors to feel more comfortable and confident submitting their encouraging notes, therefore leading to an increase in emails acquired on the page.
The treatment ran for about 2 weeks with half the traffic seeing the anonymous note check box.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the option to send a note anonymously on the Note of Encouragement landing page did not lead to an increase in email acquisition as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 19.3% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group, with a high statistical level of confidence at 98.1%.

Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changes to the user experience on conversion rates. While the idea of reducing perceived pressure and lowering barriers for participation seemed promising, the results show that anonymity may not always lead to increased engagement. These results indicate that the treatment added too much additional friction and not enough value to the visitors.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to thoroughly test and evaluate any proposed changes before fully implementing them. Additionally, conducting user testing and gathering feedback prior to launching an experiment can help uncover potential pitfalls and ensure that the changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to continually test and iterate on strategies to optimize conversion rates and user engagement.

experiments.title: How does adding an exclusive offers slide-up to a landing page impact email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting users with additional video series options in a slide-up, they will be more likely to find content that resonates with them, which will lead to a higher email acquisition rate. By offering more choices, we expect to reduce friction in the decision-making process and increase the likelihood that users will opt in with their email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/24/2025
We hypothesized by providing visitors with alternative video series options, visitors would more likely to find content that resonates with them, thereby increasing the likelihood of signing up. The test was not about determining which series resonated more with people but rather whether offering additional options would lead to more email sign-ups.
To test this, we implemented an A/B test in VWO, splitting traffic evenly (50/50) between the Control and Treatment. The Control version of the landing page remained unchanged, while the Treatment featured a slide-up at the bottom of the page displaying three alternative video series options. We tested this on “Proof You Need,” their most successful video series we are running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 18 days, during which the control acquired 1,249 emails and the treatment acquired 1,155 emails. This resulted in a 6.8% decrease in email acquisition for the Treatment at a 95.6% level of confidence. While we initially hypothesized that offering more options would increase conversions, the results suggest that the slide-up may have unintentionally introduced decision friction. Users who arrived on the landing page were likely already primed to sign up based on the ad they clicked, but the presence of additional choices may have disrupted that intent, leading to hesitation rather than action. Instead of reinforcing their decision, the added options could have created uncertainty, ultimately reducing the overall conversion rate.

Based on these results, we recommend maintaining the current landing page design without the slide-up to avoid introducing additional friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity around where an email is being used on a landing page form affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity around where an email is being used for quiz takers will achieve an increase in conversion rate because users will feel more informed and confident about how their personal information is being used, reducing hesitation and fostering trust in the process, which leads to higher engagement and willingness to complete the download.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2024 - 10/01/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a quiz on Marxism through paid social and email prospecting. The control landing page didn’t address on the form where the email address the user submitted would be used, so Heritage wanted to test adding an enrollment option button to the form that allows users to opt in to communication from Heritage and HAFA.
They originally wanted to run this experiment to be able to capture emails who opted in to HAFA communications as well as Heritage.
This test ran for two weeks to 50% of the traffic to this landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the clarity around where an email is being used for survey takers did not achieve an increase in conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version, which included additional language about agreeing to terms and conditions and privacy policies, saw a significant 33.1% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control version with a 100% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of adding extra language or steps to the conversion process. While transparency and clarity are important, it is crucial to balance this with the potential for increasing friction and reducing conversion rates. It may be beneficial to test different variations of messaging or form options to find the right balance between providing information and maintaining a smooth user experience.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing messaging and form elements to ensure the highest conversion rates possible.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the radical redesign applied to the landing page for ebook offers led to a significant decrease in email acquisition for all traffic. The treatment group experienced an 86.2% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group, and this result was statistically significant at a level of 100.0%.
These results are specific to desktop only, when we looked at mobile we saw a similar situation with a 14% decrease in emails acquired at a 90% LoC
Based on these findings, it is clear that the radical redesign did not achieve the intended outcome of increasing email conversations. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of significant design changes on user behavior and conversion rates. It may be beneficial to conduct user testing or smaller-scale experiments before implementing radical redesigns across all offers.

experiments.title: How form location on a survey landing page impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the form below the survey for MPR Listener Survey landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because participants will focus solely on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront, creating a more seamless and user-friendly experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/20/2025
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on MPR’s Listener Survey landing page, we opted to test into relocating the form to the bottom of the page.
Our hypothesis was that this change would reduce the friction happening at the top of the page therefore increasing the number of people who would complete the survey.
Control: form above the survey
Treatment: form below the survey
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the form below the survey on the MPR's Listener Survey landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition. The treatment version, where the form was placed at the bottom of the page, saw a 39.2% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic with a 99.1% statistical level of confidence, indicating that this change was highly effective in improving the user experience and increasing conversions and should be rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of page layout on user behavior. Placing the form below the survey reduced the friction at the top of the page, allowing participants to focus on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront. This created a more seamless and user-friendly experience ultimately leading to a higher conversion rate.

Moving forward, we should continue to test different page layout elements, such as form placement, language, and design, to optimize the user experience and improve conversion rates. It is also important to analyze the results of each experiment carefully and draw actionable insights to inform future optimization strategies.

experiments.title: How should we present an ask on the homepage for Spring Share?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a lowe third slide up for the homepage during the Spring Share campagin will achieve more transactions because the ask is a newer element that can be more visible to users on the page and the lower third sticky bar allows the user to start their gift on the page rather than going straight to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2025
In previous catalytic campaigns for Moody we have found that adding a specific ask on the homepage will convert donors better than relying on the donate buttons that are in the navigation.
During CYE, we tested a sticky bar vs. a lower third slide up and saw that having the lower third increased transactions and revenue. We wanted to see if this same result would hold true outside of CYE, where the motivation is vastly different.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a lower third slide up on the homepage during the Spring Share campaign significantly increased donations compared to the control group. The treatment group saw a 33.1% increase in donations for all traffic with a 95.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the lower third slide up was a more effective way to encourage users to donate.
Moving forward, we should consider implementing lower third slide ups or similar prominent calls-to-action on our homepage during fundraising campaigns. This can help increase visibility of the donation ask and prompt users to start their gift directly on the page, potentially leading to higher conversion rates and donation amounts. Additionally, we should continue to conduct A/B testing and experiments to optimize our donation strategy and improve the overall effectiveness of our fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: Will a personalized ask on the homepage convert more donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized touch to the lower third for the Spring Share campagin will achieve more conversions because having the ask come from a well known on-air host will lend to more credibility.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2025
In this series of homepage testing for Spring Share we found that having a lower third slide up is better to have to convert users to donors on the homepage.
For this test, we wanted to test the elements on the lower third to see if we could further optimize the experience for the user.
In this test, we wanted to see if having the lower third ask come from a human had the potential to increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized touch to the lower third for the Spring Share campaign by having the ask come from the President actually led to a decrease in donations. This result was unexpected as the hypothesis suggested that a personal touch would increase credibility and drive more conversions.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of humanizing elements on the website. While personalization can often be effective in building trust and engagement, it is crucial to test these elements to ensure they are positively impacting the desired outcomes. It is also essential to remain open to unexpected results and to use them as learning opportunities for future experiments. Additionally, continuous testing and optimization are key to understanding what resonates best with users and drives the most positive outcomes.

experiments.title: How underlining post titles increases clicks to single-post page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that underlining post titles for users on an author profile will achieve an increase in clicks to the single-post page and by extension Tribute Gifts because it directs more traffic to a widget that, otherwise, users are not aware how to get to.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/10/2025
We hypothesized that in the current experience, users are not aware that they can click the titles of an Author’s Post—leading them to the single-post page view. To test this hypothesis, we wanted to observe the impact of underlining this title in the treatment, more clearly denoting that it was clickable. This would have two potential impacts. First, it could help improve the user experience by allowing them to read longer posts in a designated window versus in-feed. Second, it would increase traffic to the single-page view. On this page, they are presented with a Tribute Widget (16003). We hypothesized that directing more traffic to this widget would also increase donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that underlining post titles on an author profile significantly increased the number of clicks to the single-post page. This successfully led to a substantial increase in traffic to the single-post page. Confirming that in the current experience, users are not aware that the titles can be clicked. However, this did not by extension lead to an increase in Tribute widget gifts as hypothesized.

When considering the user journey, these results can be understood better. While conversion rate on this widget is typically high for JEN traffic (those clicking from an email and going to the post) it may not convert as highly for traffic who is coming to the single-post page from the user's profile. In the latter, they are already shown tribute widgets and asks on the author's profile, seeing another on the single-post page doesn't necessarily inspire them to give on its own. Unlike JEN traffic, which is clicking from an email where no ask is present and arriving on a page with an ask.

However, what these results underscore is the potential importance of increasing traffic while also optimizing the widget. If more users arrived on this page AND were presented with a unique ask, it could potentially convert that traffic into donors.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security statement at the bottom of the form for 2025 Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because it alleviates nerves about giving up personal information through our form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2025 - 02/24/2025
In an effort to boost our sign-up rate on IMB’s 2025 Calendar Landing Page, we decided to test into a security statement on the landing page.
The security statement read: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your calendar.”
Our hypothesis was that this statement would ease any nervousness of giving up personal information through our landing page form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. The addition of a security statement at the bottom of the form on the 2025 Calendar landing page led to a significant decrease in email acquisition rates.

2. The decrease in email acquisition suggests that the security statement may have inadvertently raised concerns or doubts among visitors, leading them to reconsider providing their personal information.

Future Experimentation:

1. When testing security statements or trust signals on landing pages, it is crucial to carefully consider the phrasing and placement of such statements to avoid creating unintended negative effects.

2. In future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different variations of security statements or phrasing to determine what resonates best with visitors and effectively boosts email acquisition rates.

3. It is essential to continuously monitor and analyze the impact of any changes made to landing pages to ensure that they are having the intended positive effect on visitor behavior.

4. Collecting feedback from visitors through surveys or interviews can provide valuable insights into their perceptions of security statements and help in optimizing their effectiveness in future experiments.

experiments.title: How an aligned, value-driven image of an ebook on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using imagery that shows the content of an ebook for users visiting an ebook landing page will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because the imagery will showcase the value and ebook content more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/31/2024
During the Christmas season, Mission Aviation Fellowship (MAF) was running a devotional ebook acquisition offer. When running an offer like this, the landing page will typically feature a 3D mockup image of the ebook cover to help users on the page understand what they are receiving.
For this devotional offer, the ads on Facebook used a “scattered pages” design to showcase some of the unique offerings inside the ebook (devotions, Bible verses, crossword puzzles, etc.). We wondered if using this imagery on the devotional ebook landing page would help to enhance the value proposition and increase conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test for 29 days, with 50% of landing page traffic seeing the image of the 3D ebook, and the other 50% of traffic seeing the “scattered pages” design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing imagery that shows the content of an ebook on a landing page can significantly increase email sign-ups. In this case, the treatment version, which featured an image showcasing some of the unique offerings inside the ebook, led to a 35.5% increase in emails acquired compared to the control version that only showed the 3D ebook cover. This lift was at a 99.2% LOC, which means that the result is considered statistically valid.

In future experiments, it is important to continue testing imagery on the landing page that effectively showcases the value and content of the content offer. This can help capture the attention of users and communicate the benefits of the ebook more clearly, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates.

Further testing should also be done to determine the impact of aligning the imagery on the landing page with any imagery that users are seeing at the front of the funnel through ads, emails, MMS, etc.

experiments.title: How adding content offers to your website increases overall email and donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding content offers  for website visitors will achieve an increase in email and donor conversion rate  because we’re offering something of value to the people who are already interested in what Heritage has to offer. because  we’re offering something of value to the people who are already interested in what Heritage has to offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2024 - 08/27/2024
The Heritage Foundation wanted to find an efficient way to add their existing content offers to their website to capture additional emails and donations. They decided to add an interstitial on their content pages offering three ebooks – 5 Shocking Cases of Election Fraud, The Guide to the Constitution, and The Real Cost of America’s Border Crisis.
We ran this experiment for almost a month and showed the treatment to 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment conducted by The Heritage Foundation are as follows:
1. **Value of Adding Content Offers:** The experiment demonstrated that adding content offers to the website can significantly impact email acquisition and donor conversion rates. By offering something of value to website visitors, in this case, three ebooks, the organization was able to attract more email sign-ups and increase one-time transactions.
2. **Significant Increase in Email Acquisition:** The treatment group, which received the content offers, saw a remarkable 46.0% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This indicates that providing valuable content can incentivize visitors to share their contact information.
3. **Substantial Increase in Donations:** The experiment also showed a substantial 485% increase in one-time transactions, indicating that the content offers not only attracted more email sign-ups but also encouraged visitors to make donations.
4. **Statistical Significance:** Both the increase in email acquisition and one-time transactions were statistically significant, with confidence levels of 100.0% and 99.0%, respectively. This reinforces the reliability of the results obtained from the experiment.
Moving forward, The Heritage Foundation should leverage the learnings from this experiment in future experimentation by:
- Continuing to test the impact of different content offers on email acquisition and donation rates.
- Experimenting with the placement and design of content offers on the website to optimize conversion rates.
- Segmenting the audience to tailor content offers based on visitor preferences and behaviors.
- Monitoring the long-term impact of content offers on donor retention and engagement metrics.
Overall, this experiment highlights the effectiveness of providing valuable content to website visitors as a strategy to increase email acquisition and donor conversion rates. By incorporating these learnings into future experimentation, The Heritage Foundation can continue to optimize its digital marketing efforts and achieve its organizational goals.

experiments.title: How an affirming CTA on tribute widget impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an affirming CTA for users viewing a tribute widget will achieve an increase in donor conversino l because it empowers the reader with the autonomy to answer a question in the affirmative, reinforcing their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/04/2024
During CaringBridge’s 2-day match appeal, the Tribute widget copy would be updated to include mention of the match opportunity in addition to highlighting the usual $25 callout. In the control, the CTA button was “Donate to CaringBridge today.” We hypothesized that by turning this CTA into an affirmation of the question “Will you give $25?” would improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Affirming CTAs can positively impact donor conversion rates: The treatment, which featured an affirming CTA "Yes, I can give $25" resulted in a significant 32.5% increase in donations compared to the control. This indicates that empowering the reader with an affirmative question can reinforce their decision to give.
2. Statistical significance: The results showed a high level of statistical confidence at 98.9%, indicating that the observed increase in conversions is likely not due to random chance.
It's worth noting that this impact was seen during the match opportunity, however, it performed flat when run as an evergreen experiment. However, it's possible that the affirmative language benefits most from a sense of urgency.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown timer to an “update your membership information” landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 10-minute countdown timer for membership update page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because there is an increased sense of urgency to complete the form when given a set amount of time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign targeting donors who had not yet given a gift in 2024 to update their membership information, including name, email address, phone number, and address on a landing page. They were then redirected to a donation page where they could give a gift in order to renew their Heritage membership.
In an effort to increase email acquisition rate on the landing page, Heritage wanted to test whether adding a 10-minute countdown timer would increase the viewer’s sense of urgency and encourage them to fill out the form more often.
The control page did not have a countdown timer, and the treatment page had a 10-minute countdown timer that started when the viewer started the page session and ended when it reached 0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a 10-minute countdown timer to the membership update page did not achieve the desired increase in email acquisitions. In fact, the treatment page saw a significant and valid 11.5% decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control page. This indicates that the sense of urgency created by the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on the viewers, leading to a lower conversion rate. Viewers may have felt put off or pressured by the timer, or there may have been a disconnect in understanding why there was a limited timeframe to update their membership information.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of elements like countdown timers on user behavior. While urgency can sometimes be effective in driving action, it is crucial to test such elements to ensure they are positively influencing the desired outcome. Additionally, it is essential to analyze the results of experiments thoroughly before implementing changes across all marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How removing imagery on a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the images for Avila’s landing page viewer will achieve an increase in email acquisition because less images will reduce friction and draw attention to the sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/25/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
They are currently running an offer, Spiritual Direction and the Discernment of Spirits. We decided to test removing all the images from their landing page to see if this increases conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing images from Avila's landing page led to a 20% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 100.0%. This means the control group, which had images on the landing page, performed better in terms of email sign-ups. This led to the treatment showing a 20% decrease in overall revenue. There was also a decrease in the number of transactions from 30 in the control group to 28 in the treatment group. However, this was not statistically valid, so this cannot be a learning but something to test further.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of visual elements such as images on user engagement and conversion rates. While the hypothesis that removing images would reduce friction and draw attention to the sign-up form seemed plausible, the results indicate the opposite effect.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different types of images, their placement, or the overall design of the landing page to optimize for conversions. Additionally, conducting user surveys or gathering qualitative feedback can provide insights into the preferences and behaviors of the target audience when interacting with the website.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and being open to unexpected results in order to make informed decisions for optimizing conversion rates in the future.

It is essential to test different strategies and elements to see what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, analyzing the impact of each change on user behavior is crucial before implementing it across the board. By continuing to test and iterate based on the results obtained, the Hoover Institution can optimize their campaigns and strategies for better performance in the future.

rows.name: Form Adjustments + New Layout + Image Left

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side for Prayer Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because there will be less friction on the page and the visitor will perceive the form as less work since all content will be above the fold..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2024 - 08/20/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run an A/B/C split test with traffic equally split between each version.
Treatment 1 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout
Treatment 2 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout + making the image left aligned
Our hypothesis that the form relocation would make the landing page appear like a short experience for the user and the image adjustment seems small but the image is angled to face outward which we thought may have been adding unnecessary friction to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side of the Prayer Calendar landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition rate. The treatment led to a 15.4% increase in email acquisition for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 95.2% meaning that these results are valid and should be rolled out to all traffic.
This result indicates that the hypothesis was correct in assuming that these adjustments would reduce friction on the page and make it appear as a shorter and easier experience for the user. Moving forward, it is important to consider the impact of design elements on user experience and to continue testing and iterating on these factors to optimize conversion rates.
It is important to note that this was technically a multivariate test as we changed both the form location and used a condensed version between the control and treatment 1. This was unintentional, but it is clear that the combination of the form location, condensed fields, and left alignment of the image increased the number of emails acquired which was the final goal.
Future experimentation should focus on further refining the layout and design of the landing page to continue improving the user experience and email acquisition rates. It is also beneficial to involve user feedback and conduct usability testing to gain insights into how visitors interact with the page. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can optimize our conversion funnels and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to enhance authority impacted course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal, clarity, and authority through a testimonial for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
In a previous experiment, we had seen that adding a personal quote from the professor on the course registration page increased sign-ups. We wanted to test this same hypothesis on additional courses to see if this is something that was specific to the previous course or specific quote or if this was a global concept that could be rolled out on all courses taught by the seminary’s president. For this course, we added a brief testimonial and a photo of the professor so potential course registrants can get a glimpse of additional clarity about who is teaching the course and how having social proof from a figure of authority would motivate visitors to give away their email address on the page. We split the traffic 50/50 to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered after running the experiment for one month was that the treatment lifted the conversion rate on the acquisition page by 7.4% with 97.2% confidence. Drilling down into specific segments, we did notice a directional decrease in conversion from paid social, which will need to be addressed. However, on its face we believe this treatment layout makes the course more appealing by humanizing the offer. Since we have now seen similar treatments provide lifts on various courses regardless of the course-specific testimonial content, we will add this feature to the existing layout of the course registration pages for those that are taught by the seminary president.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a daily devotional subscription impacts email conversion on the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe strengthening the appeal and exclusivity of a daily devotional subscription will increase the email conversion on its landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2023 - 08/07/2023
For Bible League Canada’s Words from the Heart daily devotional, we felt the Value Proposition was weaker than it should be. Particularly in the exclusivity and appeal it conveyed. The control page seemed to assume that visitors would already believe that they should be reading their Bibles each day, instead of reminding them why daily Bible reading may be important.
We hypothesized that the landing page’s value proposition could be strengthened by first articulating why a daily devotional is important and then explaining how this short, exclusive devotional would enable subscribers to experience the peace and presence of Christ. To do this, we added a scripture reference that encouraged believers to daily meditate on God’s Word. Then, we emphasized the negative things that can happen when believers don’t take time to follow this verse’s instruction and meditate on Scripture. Finally, we emphasized three separate times in the copy that this was an “exclusive 3-minute daily devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After just one full week, we were able to validate the impact of this strengthened value proposition. This test was a reminder that we shouldn't assume that our audience already understands the full value of an offer. Instead, we should take time to reinforce the need the offer meets as well as the exclusive ways it will benefit users. We will continue to test the value proposition on our other landing pages to ensure that we are providing greater appeal and exclusivity to visitors.

experiments.title: How changing a Landing Page headline to a question impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the headline for Hoover’s audience will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because it poses a question that targets the audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2024 - 06/12/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. They have many eBook content offers, this specific one, Property Rights. This offer had a low email acquisition rate and a low percentage of people who signed up. We wanted to test a new headline on the offer’s landing page. Headlines are a quick way to grab your viewer’s attention. We specifically wanted to ask question targeted toward the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the headline for Hoover Institution's Property Rights offer did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 20.1% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changing key elements such as headlines before implementing them. It's also crucial to continuously test and optimize different elements of the offer to find the best combination for maximizing email acquisition. Additionally, using statistical significance to determine the effectiveness of changes is essential in making informed decisions.

experiments.title: How a landing page redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a landing page redesign for content offer viewers from acquisition will achieve the same email conversion rate because the landing page messaging and experience will mostly stay the same.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 08/05/2024
With the launch of a new website, we considered moving landing page platforms but wanted to get the data before deciding to transition in case it impacted results. We built a treatment in the new platform and compared how visitors responded in comparison to the old platform. The treatment kept all of the same copy from the control, but had some differences in formatting which may have impacted results. We ran the two versions side by side and split traffic for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesign of the landing page for content offer viewers from acquisition led to a significant decrease in email acquisition. The treatment performed 32.3% worse than the control, with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the original landing page outperformed the redesigned version. We believe this is due to a few distinct differences in the user experience on the page, including checkboxes, width of form fields, presentation of headline, formatting of page copy, and color and copy on the CTA button. While on the surface the pages were essentially the same, these slight changes in design made a significant impact on results and reduced email conversion.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to landing pages, even if the messaging and content remain the same. Small differences in formatting and design can have significant effects on user behavior and conversion rates. It is also essential to thoroughly test any new designs before fully implementing them, to avoid negative impacts on conversion rates. Additionally, considering running qualitative research such as user testing and surveys in addition to A/B testing to gather more insights into how users are interacting with the landing page and to understand their preferences and behavior better.

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat widget CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a chat message widget to the podcast pages for visitors of FamilyLife Today® will create a more engaging and persuasive experience due to the interactive nature of the widget, therefore increasing donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
FamilyLife helps families bridge the gap between their intentions and reality, fostering closer relationships with God, each other, and their communities. Their FamilyLife Podcast Network, including the FamilyLife Today® podcast, offers engaging conversations to help families grow together with Jesus and strengthen their most important relationships.
Traditionally, FamilyLife has relied on an end-of-article “dear listener” message to promote their campaigns and offer a premium item to donors. We hypothesized that adding a chat message widget to the podcast pages for listeners and visitors of FamilyLife Today® will increase donor conversion rates because the interactive and personal nature of the widget creates a more engaging and persuasive experience through social presence and personal connection.
For this test, we selected their most popular premium offer, the book “Seeing Beautiful Again.” The Control was the traditional “dear listener” message, while the Treatment was a chat message widget. Traffic was split evenly between the two versions, with identical language used in both. The Control message appeared at the end of the article, while the widget popped up in the bottom right corner and expanded when clicked. The Treatment also included images of the FamilyLife Today podcast hosts, Dave and Ann, to simulate a text messaging experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 29 days, we found that the chat message widget outperformed the traditional "dear listener" approach, achieving a conversion rate of 0.37% compared to 0.13% for the control. This represents a 187.9% increase in conversions with a high level of confidence at 98.8%.The results demonstrate that the interactive nature of the widget significantly enhanced donor engagement and conversion rates.
For future experimentation, we plan to test this approach during the Fiscal Year End (FYE) Campaign using a premium book offer, "Unoffendable" by Brant Hansen. We also aim to explore different design variations of the chat widget, evaluate other premium offers for their effectiveness with interactive features, and assess the widget’s performance across various sections of the website.
Overall, the key learnings from this experiment emphasize the importance of personalization, interactivity, and social presence in driving donor conversions. By leveraging these insights in future experimentation, FamilyLife can continue to optimize its donation strategies and create more impactful and engaging experiences for its audience.

experiments.title: How adding a clearer headline to a content offer page impacted email sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to an offer landing page for offer downloaders will achieve a change in sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 07/25/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, and was tested on educational articles on their website.
For one of their most popular offers–Things to Do in North Carolina: The Great Outdoors–we wanted to test whether a headline that talked about what people would get when they signed up for the ebook would have an impact on sign up rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding clarity to an offer landing page for offer downloaders can significantly increase sign up rates. The treatment version, which clearly stated what people would get when they signed up for the ebook, resulted in a 20.5% increase in email acquisition with a 96.9% level of confidence.
2. Revenue experienced a substantial 3078% increase with a 98% level of confidence as a result of the treatment.
3. The treatment version, which explicitly mentioned the content of the ebook, outperformed the control version, which provided a more generic description of activities in North Carolina.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue to prioritize clarity and specificity in offer descriptions. Clearly communicating the value proposition to potential customers can lead to higher conversion rates and increased revenue. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies to further optimize conversion rates and user engagement.

WUNC is a local NPR station in North Carolina.

The treatment group that saw the landing page with the testimonial had a 14.7% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group.
This suggests that including a testimonial adds validity and trust to the offer, making it more appealing to potential donors. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of testimonials or social proof elements on landing pages for different content offers or donation campaigns. Additionally, it would be worth exploring other methods to build trust and credibility, such as featuring testimonials from well-known individuals or partnering with influencers in the community.
Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of social proof in increasing conversions and can guide future optimization strategies for acquiring new names and donors for the nonprofit.

experiments.title: How using simplified, invitational ad creative for a video series ad increased clicks to the landing page

rows.name: NA | Kingdom Mindset, normal ads - PO (March 2024)
NA | Kingdom Mindset, simple ads - PO (March 2024)

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified style of ad creative for video series ads on Facebook will achieve an increase in clicks to the landing page because the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more. because  the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2024 - 04/27/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promote these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if using a simplified, “invitation” style of ad creative would lead to more clicks to the video series landing page.
For this experiment, we tested their standard style of ad creative against a version that had fewer design elements and was written to sound more like an invitation from the speaker in the video series, rather than an informal ad from an organization.
Our hypothesis is that the simple invitation ad design will increase clicks because it is more personal and engaging.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. Both ad variants used the same text and CTA button, the only variable was the ad creative, and we ran this test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for one month, we found that the simple, invitational ad creative increased in clicks by 46.2%, with a level of confidence (LOC) of 100%. This indicates that this result is valid and that we'd get a similar result if we tested this again.
Aside from clicks, the treatment ads also resulted in a 26% increase in email sign-ups for the video series, with a 99.9% LOC. This shows us that the simple, invitation style of ad creative didn't just motivate people to click on the ads. It also made them more motivated to sign up for the series!
Our key learnings from this experiment are:
The simplified style of ad creative, designed to sound like a personal invitation, led to a significant increase in clicks and email sign-ups. This indicates that the treatment ad was more effective in engaging and attracting viewers to take action.Testing this kind of simple invitation ad with Focus on the Family's other video series offers may lead to similar results, but each series offer is different and more testing is needed to have a more definitive answer.

experiments.title: How including specific examples of what a person will learn from an eBook on a landing page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including specific examples of what a person will learn from an eBook on a landing page will increase the conversion rate because it provides more context and they have more information about whether they are interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2024 - 06/17/2024
In an effort to increase the email sign-up rate on an ebook acquisition page, The Heritage Foundation ran an experiment that highlighted specific examples of what a person will learn from this ebook on the landing page.
They believed that it will provide more context and give the user more information to the contents of the ebook which will make them more interested in the offer.
This experiment was split 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including specific examples of what a person will learn from an ebook on a landing page resulted in a 19.1% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic. This decrease was statistically significant at a 98.4% confidence level, indicating that the control group, without the specific examples highlighted, had a higher conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of providing too much information upfront on a landing page. It is possible that overwhelming visitors with specific examples may have dissuaded them from signing up for the ebook. It could be beneficial to test different levels of detail and types of information provided on the landing page to find the right balance that entices visitors to take the desired action.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing hypotheses before implementing changes permanently and the value of continuous experimentation to optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a landing page headline and copy impacted sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to the headline and the copy for site visitors will achieve a higher sign-up rate because it will better communicate the value they will receive by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
In an effort to increase our sign-up rate on the landing page of this Christmas Cards offer, we wanted to test adding clarity to both the headline and the copy. In Variation 1, we tested the new headline by itself. In Variation 2, we tested the new headline and the new call to action above the sign-up form.
The new headline reads:  Get your free scripture-infused Christmas Cards
The additional copy reads: Simply complete this form to request your free Christmas cards
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to the headline and call to action button on the landing page resulted in a decrease in email acquisition, compared to the control group. Variation 1, only testing the headline, resulted in a 2.4 drop with 19.5% level of confidence. Variation 2, testing the new headline and the statement above the form, resulted in a 31.2 drop in email acquisition with a 99.9% level of confidence.
On the surface, this suggests that the changes made did not resonate with site visitors and may have made the offer less appealing which is why we saw the drop in email acquisition.
It is important to note that this landing page experienced issues with this form after this test was launched. As well as, this experiment only ran for four days which could have heavily impacted our results. Therefore, we plan to retest including clearer language on landing pages, in hopes that we will see an increase in emails acquired.

experiments.title: How using an iPhone Notes App style of Facebook ad creative impacted clicks to a video series landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative image to mimic a Notes App style for Facebook traffic will achieve an increase in clicks and email signups because the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2024 - 06/24/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promoted these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if we could try some radically different ad creatives to improve clicks and email signups from their ads.
For this experiment, we tested using an organic, non-branded ad that resembles a screenshot of someone’s iPhone Notes App. This ad creative featured an impact-focused headline and three bullet point statements below.
The control ads for this test used the same headline and bullet points, but they would look like the typical branded ads we usually run on Facebook.
Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. The Control campaign had the standard ads for two video series, and the Treatment campaign had the Notes App version of the ad creative for those same two video series. All other variables remained the same, and the test ran for a total of 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We expected the Notes App style of ad creative to perform better in this test, and we were surprised to see that our Control ads actually had the best results.
These are the results we saw:
The Treatment ads led to a -6.1% decrease in clicks to the video series landing page, with a 99.9% level of confidence (LOC).The Treatment ads also validated a -7.9% decrease in the number of emails acquired, with a 99.6% LOC.And the Treatment ads had a -24.0% decrease in donations, with a 98.8% LOC.The Control ads had a campaign ROI of 1.39, which was higher than the Treatment campaign's ROI of 0.94.
Our key learnings from this experiment are that the Notes App style ad creative did not perform as well as the standard branded ads in terms of clicks, email signups, and donations. Despite the hypothesis that the more authentic and eye-catching design would lead to an increase in engagement, the results showed a decrease in clicks to the landing page, emails acquired, and donations.
We have a couple of theories on why the Treatment ads performed worse in this test, and these are ideas that we can test into with future ads:
The Treatment ads lacked the Focus on the Family branding and logo, which could have decreased their credibilityThe Treatment ads did not incorporate any imagery to support the value or impact of the video series, but the Control ads used imagery to support their claims.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.The Control ads had more color to help them stand out on Facebook, whereas the white background of the Treatment ads caused them to almost "blend in" with the rest of the white space on the page.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.
