experiments.title: How a question headline on a landing page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A question headline on a landing page will increase the conversion rate because it creates curiosity and people will want to sign up to find out the answer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/04/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for an eBook on the state of the U.S. Military. We saw an opportunity to raise email conversion rate on the eBook offer’s landing page, so we wondered whether adding a headline that poses a question that the eBook answers would lead to an increase in conversion rate due to creating curiosity in the mind of the reader.
For this experiment, the control’s headline read: “Learn About the Current State of the U.S. Military — And What That Could Mean for Our Nation’s Future”
The treatment’s headline read: “War Is Raging—But How Prepared Is the U.S. Military if They Are Needed?”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split redirect test and ultimately ran for 20 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control, which had a headline stating the topic directly without posing a question, outperformed the treatment, which featured a question headline. The treatment received a 23.7% decrease in email conversion rate at a valid 95.7% level of confidence compared to the control. This result indicates that in this particular case, the straightforward approach was more effective in capturing the audience's attention and prompting them to sign up for the eBook offer.

Downstream, the treatment saw a 9.5% increase in donor conversion rate, but at a 17% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember that different tactics may work better depending on the context and the audience. While posing a question can create curiosity, it is essential to ensure that the question resonates with the target audience and clearly communicates the value proposition. It may be beneficial to test an even more straightforward and clear headline that states the eBook's title.

experiments.title: How a benefit-focused landing page headline affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a headline on a landing page that focuses on the benefits a supporter receives will increase the conversion rate because they will quickly understand the value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 05/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for their Election Fraud eBook content offer. In an attempt to raise email conversion for visitors to the landing page, we wanted to test whether a headline that was more focused on the benefit viewers would get from reading the eBook would achieve this outcome.
The control headline read, “Get your free “5 Shocking Cases of Election Fraud” eBook from The Heritage Foundation.”
The treatment headline read, “Uncover the Hidden World of Election Fraud and Protect Your American Freedom.”
We set this up as a 50/50 split A/B test and ran for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment headline, which focused on the benefits of reading the eBook, received a lower amount of conversions than the control headline. The treatment resulted in a 34.3% decrease in email acquisitions for All Traffic with a 99.9% level of statistical confidence. This also resulted in a valid lower donation rate downstream for the treatment, with a 57.3% decrease in transactions at 99.9% level of confidence.

Reasons for this result could include the treatment headline sacrificing clarity in order to illustrate the benefits the viewer could receive from this content. The headline "Uncover the Hidden World of Election Fraud and Protect Your American Freedom" doesn't clearly illustrate what the content offer is upon reading it, which may dissuade viewers from reading the page further and making a decision to download the resource.

Moving forward, it is essential to consider that a headline focused on the specific offer may be more effective in driving conversions compared to a headline that emphasizes the benefits. It is crucial to clearly communicate the value proposition of the offer to the audience to encourage them to take action.

experiments.title: How removing the navigation from a premium landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing the navigation for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is less distractions, frictions and CTAs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2024 - 05/21/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas
We have seen with other organizations that removing navigation from pages that lead to donation pages or for fundraising efforts, tend to see an increase in conversions because there are less distractions and CTAs.

In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in transactions by 32.4% compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence at 97.0%.

For future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing different strategies and elements on the website to optimize conversion rates. It is important to carefully monitor the results of each experiment and make data-driven decisions based on the outcomes to continually improve the user experience and achieve the desired goals of Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How including examples from the eBook on a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including examples of cases from the eBook on the sign-up landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will have more context about the E-book to decide if they want to download it
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 06/03/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for an eBook on election fraud. In an attempt to raise conversion rate on the eBook’s landing page, we wondered if adding body copy with examples of cases from the eBook would persuade more people to download the eBook if they had more context as to what the offer contained.
We tested the control against the treatment which added the following copy:
“You’ll learn about a Judge of Elections who accepted bribes, another individual who devised a ballot harvesting scheme, and more. In this eBook, you’ll also learn about the consequences of these crimes against American liberty.”
The control and treatment each saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding examples of cases from the eBook on the landing page did not increase the conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the added body copy resulted in a 21.9% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control version. This suggests that the additional information provided in the treatment did not persuade more people to download the eBook.

Viewers may have thought the additional copy in the treatment "spoiled" two of the advertised five cases of Election Fraud or took the mystery out of the eBook and dissuaded them from following through to download the offer.

Future experimentation could include rewriting the page's copy to be more accessible to a wider audience through a lower reading level.

experiments.title: How Conversion-Centered Design Affects Click Rates On A Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning the NextAfter Institute membership page using conversion-centered design will increase click-through rates by using proven persuasion levers to hold attention and compel action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/12/2024
Conversion-centered design (CCD) aims to apply multiple (a) persuasive design techniques and (b) psychological triggers to get more people to take a desired action. This tactic can be helpful when you feel a page “needs a lot of work” and/or when a page does not receive enough traffic to validate smaller changes.
As we began to drive more traffic to a landing page promoting the NextAfter Institute Membership, we wanted to test how using CCD to make multiple changes would impact conversion rates. For this test, we developed a treatment that tested:
Structuring page to improve “skim-ability” – We used color to create on-page contrast and make it easier for the user to find the information they needed; we also narrowed the copy width to make it more readable
Making social proof more prominent – We embedded a widget that made it easy for users to read and scroll through our many positive reviews, giving them a more prominent placement on the page and placing them in two locations: near the top and near the bottom of the page
Using an outcome-driven headline – The headline is the most important copy on the page, so we shifted from a feature-focused headline to one that reinforced what those features will allow the reader to achieve, reinforcing the “how” with a subhead
Using long-form copy to make the case – At the top of the page, we used outcome-driven copy in big bold font to prompt immediate action but we reinforced that messaging with an in-depth sales letter that uses compelling language to illustrate the value propsotion concisely yet thoroughly
Drawing attention to benefits and outcomes – We used color contrast, huge fonts, bold type, testimonials, highlighted text, short paragraphs, and crossheads to draw attention to the benefits and outcomes of membership
We split traffic between the control and the treatment and ran the A/B test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing conversion-centered design techniques can significantly impact click-through rates.
By making multiple changes to improve the page design from the perspective of conversion rate optimization, we were able to increase click-through rates by 106.6% with a 100% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to utilize conversion-centered design principles to optimize our landing pages and drive conversions.
We can now improve on this design by experimenting with different creative combinations to refine our approach and improve our overall conversion rates. Additionally, we should consider applying these learnings to other areas of our website and marketing materials to enhance the effectiveness of our messaging and calls to action.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Reader’s Favorite Message to a Content Offer Landing Page Help with Sign Up Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Reader’s Favorite flag for visitors on the Romans in Action landing page will achieve higher sign up conversion rates because visitors will see that this is a popular eBook through a visual queue. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2024 - 03/28/2024
We saw that the sign-up rates on the KCBI content offer sign-up pages could be higher so we could ultimately present more opportunities for visitors to share their email addresses and become donors. We were interested in trying a couple of visual queues based on the content offer performance to let our landing page visitors know, this is a a “fan favorite” of the KCBI audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of a "Reader's Favorite" flag on the landing page for the Romans in Action content offer did not result in higher sign-up conversion rates. In fact, the treatment actually saw a decrease of 21.3% in email acquisition compared to the control group. This indicates that the visual queue did not have the intended effect on visitors.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual cues and ensure that they align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience. It may be beneficial to conduct surveys or gather feedback from visitors to understand what factors would make them more likely to sign up for content offers.

Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different strategies and elements on landing pages to identify what resonates best with visitors. By continuously experimenting and analyzing the results, KCBI can optimize their campaigns and improve sign-up conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: How moving a headline with value proposition on a landing page impacts course sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the headline with value proposition up for the landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases clarity of what the person will learn when they sign up for the course and reduces friction. because it increases clarity of what the person will learn when they sign up for the course and reduces friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/19/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary has multiple free online courses that they use to acquire new names for their email list.
Evangelism has been a popular course that they have been advertising for years. When looking at all of the landing pages separately, we noticed that some landing pages had headlines “above the fold” (visible before someone has to scroll on the screen) while others did not. Evangelism had no headline above the fold.
In a follow up to this test, we decided to test again moving the headline with value proposition up higher on the landing page. One thing we did do differently this time is that the only thing we changed was the placement. We did not add any additional language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the headline with the value proposition up on the landing page led to a significant increase in email conversions for Dallas Theological Seminary's Evangelism course. This change increased clarity for visitors about what they would learn from the course, ultimately reducing friction and prompting more sign-ups.

In the future, Dallas Theological Seminary should consider the importance of positioning critical information, such as a value proposition, prominently on their landing pages to improve conversion rates. This experiment underscores the impact that small changes in layout can have on user behavior and reinforces the significance of clarity and reducing friction in driving conversions. Building on this learning, future experimentation should continue to focus on optimizing the placement of key elements on landing pages to maximize engagement and drive desired actions from visitors.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a landing page headline impacts course sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the value proposition up higher for the course landing page will achieve an increase in course sign ups because it increases the clarity of what the reader will get out of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 01/26/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary has a number of online courses that they use to acquire new names for their email list. Their latest course on 1 & 2 Peter was experiencing a low sign-up rate.
After looking at all of their most popular courses, we uncovered inconsistencies in the headlines. 1 & 2 Peter named the course at the top of the page, but after scrolling, there is a secondary headline featuring copy that appeared to have a much stronger value proposition, explaining what the reader will get if they sign up for the course.
So for this test, we decided to switch the headlines to see if it would impact overall course sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the value proposition higher on the course landing page resulted in a significant increase in course sign-ups. The treatment group, which had the value proposition moved up, saw a 32.2% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group. This increase was statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.6%.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the placement of the value proposition plays a crucial role in attracting potential learners and convincing them to sign up for the course. Moving forward, it is recommended to prioritize highlighting the benefits and value of the course at the top of the page to increase clarity and remove any potential friction that may prevent sign-ups.
Further experimentation can be done to optimize the messaging and design of the value proposition. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the value proposition to determine the most effective wording or format that resonates with the target audience. Continuous A/B testing can help refine and improve the course landing page to maximize email acquisition and overall conversions.

experiments.title: How a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition impacts clicks to purchase
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified product landing page that leads with value proposition will increase clicks to purchase by increasing the perceived value of the product
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/05/2023
An organization was launching a brand new streaming series called RVL Discipleship: The Study. Unlike their traditional video series’ acquisition offers that are free with an instant donation opportunity, this would be a product sold through the Focus on the Family store.
The funnel at launch included a product landing page that pointed to the store page that pointed to the cart and checkout pages. The primary purpose of the product landing page was to increase interest in the streaming series and get visitors to click the button to purchase the series.
In reviewing the page, our team noticed that it led with a promo trailer and then offered a free episode. While there was some value proposition copy on the page, it was further down the page and overall, the user experience was very different than the experience of someone on one of their video series’ landing pages that we’ve refined through testing during the last three years. Instead of focusing on the product, there seemed to be almost as much time focused on explaining who the Ray Vander Laan was. We hypothesized that a treatment page that looked more like their video series’ landing pages, with a clear value-driven headline at the top and copy explaining the impact of the series would lead to more purchase clicks than the current design because it would provide greater clarity about the product and its impact on buyers.
We designed a new version of the page and used VWO to split traffic between both versions. We measured clicks to purchase on each and ran the experiment for about 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Simplifying the product landing page and leading with a clear value proposition can increase clicks to purchase.

2. The treatment page had a 47.2% increase in visits to the RVL Store Page for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 100.0%, indicating that the results are highly reliable.

4. By emphasizing the value and impact of the product, potential buyers are more likely to be interested and motivated to make a purchase.

Our next experiment will be removing the store page and cart pages from the funnel altogether so that visitors to the new landing page who click to purchase go straight to the store checkout page. We believe this will further remove friction and lead to more purchases of the product.

We did see 13 purchases on the treatment page while only 10 on the control, but our level of confidence for this increase in purchases was only 50%. More time and data would be needed to validate the impact on purchases.

Additionally, it would be worth exploring other methods to build trust and credibility, such as featuring testimonials from well-known individuals or partnering with influencers in the community.

Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of social proof in increasing conversions and can guide future optimization strategies for acquiring new names and donors for the nonprofit.

experiments.title: How using a quote on a landing page affects email sign up rate

Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B experiments to further optimize the email sign-up rate on landing pages. experiments.title: How a video-based landing page affects email signup rates for a new YouTube fundraising show
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a text-based landing page with a video-based landing page will increase registrations by “providing a sample” of the content’s value and showing rather than telling why people should want to tune in.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2023
We recently decided to pursue a new way to share what works in fundraising by launching a YouTube show called “Decoding Generosity”.
And to promote this new ‘edutainment’ experience, we wanted to know how using a video on a landing page would affect sign-up rates for a prescreening of the show.
This test was run in the context of a broader promotional strategy testing the concept of creating a unified in-funnel experience using video as a means of “showing” rather than telling about the show.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using a video-based landing page instead of a text-based landing page produced a significant 57% increase in the registration rate.

We believe there are several reasons that the video-based treatment produced these results:
Show, don't tell. The video-based landing page matched the medium of the content we were promoting, allowing us to preview clips from the show to stoke their desire to tune in and "show" that the claim presented in our offer was genuine
 	Front and center. By reducing the supporting copy, the value-driven headline and subhead were given more visual prominence
 	Quality matters. The high production quality of the video likely played a role in the overall effectiveness of the treatment
 	Context matters. Traditionally, we have seen that text-based landing pages far outperform video-based landing pages. But it's important to consider context. This promotion was run in the context of a B2B promotion (rather than a fundraising context) and the landing page was part of a broader video-based promotional experience meant to "keep people in the story" without feeling like they were in a funnel**

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2023 - 06/23/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Bringing Biblical Truth to a Broken Culture video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the new language almost validated an increase in emails acquired on the page, it led to a significant decrease in downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page. These results conflicted with the results of a similar test we conducted over the same time period on their Winning Your Inner Battles landing page.

It is possible that the new impact language, while making the offer more appealing in general failed to increase the perceived value for the visitors most likely to donate. So, for these subscribers, the original language was better as it left them with the perceived impact they were looking for in the video series that was removed by the new bullet points. More testing is needed to identify the best impact/value language for this offer to increase donor conversion.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a content offer on a landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact the videos will have on subscribers will increase downstream donor conversion by increasing the perceived value of the videos.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 06/30/2023
Focus on the Family uses dozens of different free video series offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For most of these offers, their landing pages feature bullet points outlining the content of each episode in the series. In reviewing this standard design, we wanted to see if we could increase the appeal of the videos by using these bullet points to instead clarify what subscribers would be able to do as a result of these videos. This was inspired by Amy Harrison’s copywriting keynote at the 2022 NIO Summit.
We created a treatment version of their Winning Your Inner Battles video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the new language actually decreased signups on the page, however it significantly increased downstream donor conversion. Since we are optimizing this acquisition funnel for donations acquired, this was a clear win for the treatment. We believe that we had fewer subscribers on the treatment because in clarifying what the videos would do, we inevitably excluded some visitors from the page who weren't actually interested in the impact we were promising.  This narrowed the field of possible subscribers. But, it also increased the perceived value of the videos to this newly narrowed audience, which led to a much higher donor conversion on the subsequent donation page. Someone coming to the page wanting these results, wanting to experience daily joy in their walk with God, gave more readily when asked because they believed these videos were going to enable them to do that.
These results also remind us that some tactics will increase one conversion metric but decrease another. That is why it is important to consider the full impact of an experiment on each conversion in the funnel.

experiments.title: How a video impacted email sign ups on a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a video  for landing page visitors will achieve a higher email sign up rate because viewers receive a preview of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase email sign ups for the Into The Deep course, we tested adding a video the landing page
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a video to the landing page for the Into The Deep course resulted in a decrease in email sign-ups. This was observed across all traffic, as well as specific segments such as mobile and tablet visitors, new visitors, female visitors, and visitors in the age groups of 35-44 and 55-64. There was also a significant decrease in email sign-ups from paid search visitors.
These findings suggest that the video may not have resonated well with the target audience or may have led to a distraction or disinterest in signing up for the course. It is important to note that these results are statistically significant, indicating a high level of confidence in the findings.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully consider the impact of any changes or additions to the landing page. A thorough analysis of the target audience's preferences, interests, and behavior should be conducted to ensure that any modifications align with their expectations and increase the likelihood of conversion.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct qualitative research, such as interviews or surveys, to gather more insights into the audience's perception of the video and their decision-making process. This can help identify any specific issues or concerns that need to be addressed in order to optimize the landing page for maximum conversion.
Overall, this experiment serves as a reminder that assumptions and hypotheses should always be tested before implementing changes. It is essential to constantly iterate and improve based on data and customer feedback to achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a clearer value proposition on an acquisition offer landing page impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clearer value proposition on a video series landing page will increase email and donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/16/2023
Focus on the Family is a prominent global ministry that provides video content resources and insights for parents and families on topics such as faith, marriage, and parenting.
In reviewing their Inner Battles Series we felt that the headline and body copy lacked clarity about the battle the series would help subscribers win. “battle with yourself” was too vague. We proposed copy that would emphasize that this series was for anyone facing a “secret struggle with sin” and struggling with “habits, regrets, and sin” holding them back from the victory that “Jesus wants for them.”
We split the traffic and ran the test. Our hypothesis was this could increase landing page donor conversion and subsequent donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The clearer value proposition did not increase email signups. However, in providing a stronger value for subscribers, it increased their likliehood of donating on the subsequent donation page. We believe that people were better able to identify with the promised benefits of the video series, especially those who were actively carrying "shame or regrets" regarding a "secret struggle with sin." This made them more likely to give as they felt seen and cared for.

Our final LOC was between 87%-95%. We had an error where another experiment was launched in the last 3 days of the campaign on the donation page for this offer that would have impacted donor conversion in this experiment. However, in looking at our results prior to that second test launching, we were seeing increased donations for the treatment experience.

We will next test bullet points outlining the impact of these videos rather than simply their content.

experiments.title: How reformatting a landing page survey impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplified formatting through a survey for survey participants will achieve an increase in email sign ups because participants will have an improved experience by being able to more easily read the survey questions and follow along with the survey.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/24/2023
IPI’s Illinois Forward survey is producing a low 27.8% sign up rate. When reviewing the page and taking into account consumer feedback, we attributed the struggling metric to heavy friction through the survey formatting. The existing page includes yellow, all caps survey text that may be difficult for users to read, plus the survey questions are not numbered as surveys customarily are.
We wonder if reformatting the survey to use standard font color and style will make it easier to read, and numbering each survey question will make it easier to follow. You can see the noticeable differences in formatting via the screenshots below.
Let’s see how these simple, user-friendly adjustments impacted survey sign ups…
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test validated in favor of the treatment after running for just 6 days! The treatment produced a 14.1% lift in email conversion rate with a 99.6% level of confidence.
This is a prime example of reducing friction for the user by making their experience more user-friendly and through simplified styling/formatting. Just by making the survey questions more legible and easy to digest, we saw an immediate lift in email sign ups.
We have rolled the treatment out to 100% of traffic. We'll want to apply these changes to future surveys as well.

experiments.title: How a more detailed landing page with longer copy affects sign-up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more detail to the copy for landing page visitors will achieve higher sign-up rates because it will create greater urgency around the issue the asset addresses and better explain what the prospect will gain from accepting the offer. because it will create greater urgency around the issue the asset addresses and better explain what the prospect will gain from accepting the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2023 - 02/01/2023
For this experiment for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how longer value proposition copy affected sign-up rates. Because sign-up rates were so low, we redesigned several elements of the landing page in an effort to convey more value and urgency around the offer—an interactive map that scores the integrity of elections in each state.
We crafted the headline to include the word free and show what someone would learn from the offer. In the body copy, we included some specific examples of alleged voter fraud to increase urgency around the problem and thus the need for the resource. And in the bullets, we removed the “and more” bullet and instead included all 12 metrics the scorecard used to assess election integrity.
We ran this experiment for 30 days with 50% of traffic seeing the control and 50% of traffic seeing the treatment
experiments.keyLearnings:
At the conclusion of this test, we observed a 12.7% decrease in sign-up rates with a 99.9% level of confidence, proving our hypothesis that longer value-proposition copy would increase sign-up rates.

Upon review of these results, we believe that the length of the copy gave the appearance of a "wall" of text the visitor would have to read, creating enough friction for the prospect to click away. The longer copy may have done more to halt the momentum of the prospect than it did encourage them to sign up.

Because the landing page visitor has already expressed interest in both the offer and the problem it addresses, we hypothesize that it may be more effective to reduce the amount of copy and create a more frictionless path to acquisition once the prospect lands on the sign-up page.

As such, we plan to follow up this experiment with a new treatment that removes the first 3 paragraphs of copy from the control (much of which is restated from the Facebook ads through which the prospect has clicked—and get straight to the point by immediately telling the reader what they will gain by accessing the scorecard, making it easier and quicker for them to sign up.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the submit button on content offer landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the submit button for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion because it will decrease anxiety about data security and increase the appeal of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 09/30/2022
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the form submission button would affect email acquisition rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of requesting a devotional eBook would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to complete the form.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your eBook. “
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.  We set this up in Unbounce and, unfortunately, didn’t reset the conversions report so the samples look skewed in the report below as “control” reflects all samples since the launch of the content offer not just the ones during the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results from this Unbounce experiment, it's clear that including a privacy statement below the form submission button had a major impact on email acquisition rates.
This shows that anticipating privacy concerns in our customers and adapting our strategies to address those concerns can have a substantive impact on our overall marketing efforts. In the future, it will be valuable to continue testing our privacy statements to determine what language is most effective in communicating security, understanding that our customers expect transparency and taking responsible steps to secure their personal data. We will be testing this approach on donation pages as well.
Unfortunately, we were not able to validate a similar increase in downstream donor conversion from this test.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook image on an offer landing page impacts email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the ebook for users who arrive on the sign up page will achieve improve email conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2022 - 01/27/2023
For Navigators free ebook offer, Journey Through The Psalms, we hypothesized that by adding an image of the ebook on the landing page would increase email acquisition. The motivation for this test was that we believed a visual of the ebook on the landing page would help create continuity between the user clicking the ad and arriving at the landing page with the same visual.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, we saw a negligible difference in results, however, the small lift of 4.3% in email conversion on mobile reached a 99% confidence level. Our hypothesis is that the change resulted in a more marked change in the mobile view, where the addition of the image just takes up more screen view. Additionally, the data shows that this addition does not have a negative effect; so a 4.3% lift in email conversion for mobile traffic will expound in significance over time.
Revenue experienced a 251.2% increase with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:

experiments.title: How adding bullets/star bullets to registration page affects sign-up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a bulleted list of value proposition language for prospective course takers will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2022 - 11/05/2022
DTS offers free online courses using recordings of actual seminary classes. This three-way split test appeared on the Exodus course landing page.
This is a multivariate test. We first wanted to test if adding a list of value proposition language in a highlighted box would better communicate the value that each free course includes and increase conversion rate.
We then wanted to test whether using star emojis as the list’s bullet point icons would attract attention and help communicate value more than normal bullet points, leading to an increase in course signups in all traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the both treatments produced a small change in conversion rates. The bullet point treatment produced a decrease of 2.2% in course registrations and the star emoji treatment produced a lift of 1.8%, but due to an inadequate sample size, these results did not validate. This makes it difficult to draw a valid conclusion from this experiment.
It's interesting to note that in the email channel, the star emoji treatment produced a valid lift of 13%.
Possible reasons for this increase could be:
the star emoji reminding users of the star emojis seen in an online product review (i.e. Amazon) and signaling the impression of valuethe color and shape of the star emoji attracting the user's attentionthe size of the emoji treatment's container attracting the user's attention moreso than the bullet point treatment's container
As a result, we'll look into rolling this out to email traffic only since the email channel is the main source of traffic for DTS. We'll also look into retesting only the emoji treatment by running it during the launch of a new course to try to get a better sample size and valid results in all traffic channels.

experiments.title: How re-directing traffic from a blog to a lead capture page affected email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redirecting traffic from the blog to bible study resource landing pages for Searchers on Google will achieve growth in discipleship.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2022
Originally, we were driving as much free traffic we could to maximize the free ad grant dollars to mission aligned pages. We then wanted to test if shifting our goals from blog traffic to capturing emails using landing pages.
Our goal is to offer transformational content for the purpose of resourcing and equipping those interested in discipleship with free resources.
Before, we would direct them to our blog.
After, we direct them to free resource landing pages.
Bible Study Resources is the subset of the traffic we focused on for this test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
A note on the Google Ad Grant:
While we had assumed this outcome (an increase in email acquisition) would be the case, it was still a worthy test as there are obstacles to work through with the Google Ad grant quality score filter that could've limited reach to the lead capture pages.
We aimed to overcome this filter with a better user experience which is indicated in a few metrics: a better conversion rate, time on page, and how many additional pages they visited. In this case, we achieved this with a better conversion rate which improved our quality scores and met the quality score threshold for the ad grant.
In this experiment,
We found that the blog page didn't serve our new goal well: Offering transformational content (via email) for the purpose of resourcing and equipping those interested in discipleship with free resources.
The blog converted at 1.7% CVR compared to the lead capture pages which did a better job of converting at 8.7% CVR (Treatment 1) and 6.5% CVR (Treatment 2).
The final benefit to seeing the conversion rate increase with the bible study resources is that we're able to convert this free traffic into an asset: a re-addressable audience via email at a later time.
Control:
No Capture
Clicks: 7,680
Emails: 132
CPA: $262
CVR: 1.7%
Treatment 1:
How to Study the Bible
Clicks: 878
Emails: 76
CPA: $78.85
CVR: 8.7%
Treatment 2:
Bible Study Bundle
Clicks: 12,297
Emails: 796
CPA: $80.73
CVR: 6.5%

experiments.title: How promoting a free ebook offer instead of a general subscription impacted email acquisition on a commentary page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free eBook at the bottom of the Commentary page for page visitors when submitting their email will achieve an increase in email acquisition because they will be more drawn to this offer after reading gospel commentary than they would be receiving email updates from TGC.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2022 - 11/18/2022
The Gospel Coalition has a Commentary page in which visitors can study and learn about different books of the Bible. Towards the bottom of the page, there is an email subscription form where visitors can sign up for email updates. We wondered if this existing email acquisition opportunity could be optimized to be more alluring and enticing. So we developed a treatment that offered a free eBook for visitors when signing up and submitting their email. And the offer isn’t just for any eBook, but for TGC’s most popular eBook. Would this opportunity acquire more emails than the original opportunity?
We tested this on the TGC home page and saw negative results for the treatment; now we’re testing it on the Commentaries page to see if it aligns better when implemented there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (eBook offer) saw an increase in email acquisitions across all segments. Overall, the treatment produced a 0.92% increase in email acquisitions at a 99.9% level of confidence. The control acquired zero emails for the duration of the test. The results tell us that page visitors were more excited to receive a free eBook than they were to sign up and receive email updates from TGC.

It's worth restating that we ran this same test on the TGC home page and the treatment saw negative results. The difference is people often go to the home page to sign up for email updates and they expect an opportunity to sign up for email updates to exist on the home page. That is not the case for TGC's Commentary page. People visit this page to acquire specific knowledge and understanding. Thus, as they keep scrolling down the page, they're intrigued by an offer that also offers them the chance to acquire knowledge and understanding. They don't care at this stage to sign up for general email updates.

This concept is worth testing on pages for which the free offer aligns with the page content. It would be worth testing on other content-heavy pages.

experiments.title: How adding a campaign goal to an acquisition landing page during year-end impacted results.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a campaign goal for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2021 - 01/28/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular eBook offers is their Lady of Lourdes Novena.
During their calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether including the year-end campaign goal amount, as well as some specific value proposition language, on the Lourdes instant donation page would help drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a large drop in conversion rates on the treatment, campaign-goal oriented donation page, with a 41% decline in conversion rates and a 99.5% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to a couple of factors. First, the Lourdes offer was not actively pushed during the campaign; most of the digital messaging on donation forms and the homepage mentioned the year-end goal. Therefore, people that landed on the Lourdes page through a generic Facebook ad might not have been aware there was a campaign going on. Moreover, the campaign goal itself, and why people should donate to it, was not very clear.

For future experiments we will test whether making the value prop clearer and more urgent will have a more positive impact on conversion rates.

experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a 5% decrease in conversion rate. Having Fr. John sign the page could have resulted in unnecessary friction for people reading the page due to the placement of the signature. Instead of looking at the call to action directly below it, they were looking at the signature, and this could have diverted their eyes and attention from what was important on the page.

experiments.title: How increasing landing page congruence affects cost per subscriber
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis was that by increasing congruence (or visual consistency) between the landing page and the ad, we could improve landing page conversion and lower the cost per subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2014 - 12/25/2014
The Hoover Institution wanted to add new subscribers to its highly-regarded military history and foreign policy newsletter Strategika. Facebook ad targeting allowed them to find new subscribers by targeting these specific interests, but after a few weeks of the campaign, cost-per-subscriber was still higher than original projections.
The landing page did a good job selling Strategika — it constructed a strong value proposition, showed plenty of credibility, and made a clear call-to-action that highlighted all of the benefits of subscription. But we hypothesized we could increase the conversion rate on the landing page by making a single simple change — making the landing page look more like the ad.
The ad had a historic picture of American soldiers storming a beachhead in World War 2. We changed the landing page to have this same background, and shortened the copy to fit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did this new page with increased congruence convert 40% more people to subscribers, because Facebook was optimizing for converisons,  it lowered the cost per subscriber by 86% — a huge savings that allowed Hoover Institution to effectively acquire nearly twice as many subscribers with their original budget.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of elements creates the greatest sense of urgency that allows for the increase in conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/24/2014 - 12/28/2014
CaringBridge was in the final week of their 2014 year-end fundraising campaign and expected traffic to dramatically increase to their donation pages. They wanted to maximize on-page conversion by adding elements to the page to increase urgency: reminding people of the proximity to December 31st.
We knew that adding elements like a countdown clock and a thermometer would increase urgency, but we tested to see which element (or combination of elements) would maximize landing page conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask which, in turn, lead to the increase in conversion rate. However, when combined with the thermometer element of the campaign, it negatively affected the conversion rate. This may be due to the large goal seeming out of reach for the average donor. Whatever the case, for large campaigns like this, the countdown clock will be deployed in the future to increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a concealing headline affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a headline that teases the central issue of an offer increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
The Hoover Institution recently launched a new eBook offer titled, ‘Make America Exceptional Again.’ As they began to advertise this offer on Facebook, they wondered: Will a headline that teases the central issue of the eBook increase email acquisition? They set up a treatment page and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Hoover Institution found that this treatment version did, in fact, increase email acquisition rate by 30%. It’s clear the second headline increase page visitor motivation. As we look at the difference between these two headlines, we can see that the first headline promises to offer a solution to a very specific problem. The second headline, however, focuses on a broader, more concealed problem–as if the reader was being let in on a secret. It also doesn’t offer an immediate solution.

For these reasons, it’s likely that the visitors who visited the second version of the page felt more motivation to ‘find out’ the answer. Further testing will be conducted on this page, as well as the second ‘Thank you’ page to help improve overall offer performance.

experiments.title: How increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing personalization and aligning motivation with user intent increase engagement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/31/2018
CaringBridge’s marketing team sought to increase the amount of users who got to their “ways to help” page, where there were several options to deepen engagement and provide more support to their friend or family member.
They thought that a slight copy change would align the “Ways to help” button on the journal page with the user’s intent. Instead of “Ways to help”, they proposed “Help [FirstName]” to encourage more people to click through. They thought this might work because they hypothesized that people who were reading this page were (consciously or unconsciously) desiring to help their friend or family member.
They were concerned about possible reactions to this test, so they only included 10% of site traffic in the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new copy produced a 19% lift in clickthrough rate for the “Ways to help” page—an impressive lift considering the small change. This points to more opportunities to align site copy with user intent throughout the site. With the volume of traffic that CaringBridge has, there is tremendous potential to increase clarity and adjust user experience accordingly.

experiments.title: How a different offer on a confirmation page affects engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: Which confirmation page offer will lead to more click-throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 12/01/2016
When people completed their registration for a NextAfter live broadcast, we wanted to give them an opportunity to engage with other resources that we offer. Our original control prompted registrants to take our free FCORM assessment, but we hypothesized that this might not be the right next step for most people.
For the treatment, we created a page that presented multiple eBook offers that addressed a variety of topics. Our primary goal with this experiment was simply to get people to click through to explore more of our content.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our treatment saw a 156.7% increase in clicks, confirming our initial hypothesis that the FCORM offer was not the right next step. Our general audience is more motivated to learn practical insights that inform their marketing and fundraising.

Additionally, Google Analytics data tells us that those who clicked through the FCORM offer abandoned the site within 1 or 2 steps. Of those who clicked through the treatment page, 51% went on to download at least one other offer, and 41% viewed at least 5 more pages before exiting the site.

experiments.title: How congruence between ad and landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing congruence affect course signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
The new ad that used the same imagery as the landing page increased conversion by 47.6%. This shows that congruence is a powerful, if often subliminal tool. While the ad image might not be something that the visitor actively thinks about, the repetition of the imagery throughout the funnel reminds them subconsciously that the elements are all linked.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their second course, Genesis, on Facebook. They wanted to emphasize the academic nature of the course, so they launched ads that showed a person taking the course while studying. But they hypothesized that they might be able to increase conversion by employing the principle known as congruency: aligning each element of a marketing funnel to increase response.
They pulled the image of the Sistine Chapel from the landing page and put it into the ad, and launched an A/B test to see if they were able to affect conversion.

experiments.title: How the right offer affects emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer in the footer of the NextAfter website will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/10/2015
When we first launched the new NextAfter.com website, we had some internal debate about the best offer to use in the footer of the site. Historically, we had offered a subscription to our weekly newsletter, This vs. That, which allowed subscribers to receive one experiment per week from the Research Library.
Since this was our main website, we hypothesized that maybe the more general offer of “Staying Informed” would work better for the visitors. We decided to set up a test that would allow us to evaluate these two different offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the “Stay Informed” offer led to an infinitely better number of emails captured (none versus some is by definition infinitely better). While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How increasing landing page congruence affects cost per subscriber
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis was that by increasing congruence (or visual consistency) between the landing page and the ad, we could improve landing page conversion and lower the cost per subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2014 - 12/25/2014
The Hoover Institution wanted to add new subscribers to its highly-regarded military history and foreign policy newsletter Strategika. Facebook ad targeting allowed them to find new subscribers by targeting these specific interests, but after a few weeks of the campaign, cost-per-subscriber was still higher than original projections.
The landing page did a good job selling Strategika — it constructed a strong value proposition, showed plenty of credibility, and made a clear call-to-action that highlighted all of the benefits of subscription. But we hypothesized we could increase the conversion rate on the landing page by making a single simple change — making the landing page look more like the ad.
The ad had a historic picture of American soldiers storming a beachhead in World War 2. We changed the landing page to have this same background, and shortened the copy to fit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did this new page with increased congruence convert 40% more people to subscribers, because Facebook was optimizing for converisons,  it lowered the cost per subscriber by 86% — a huge savings that allowed Hoover Institution to effectively acquire nearly twice as many subscribers with their original budget.

experiments.title: How removing the call-to-action at the top of a landing page impacts name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the design and navigational elements increase the visitors getting to the assessment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 10/30/2017
The treatment decreased the amount of people getting to the assessment by 28.6%! While we hypothesized that the simplified page would help decrease friction and motivate more people to click-through because the focus of the page was on the value proposition, we learned that:
People coming to this page were already highly motivated to take the assessment. The additional value proposition focus wasn’t needed to convert these people.
By removing the call-to-action at the top of the page, we made it harder for these highly motivated people to click-through to the actual assessment.
We recommend building upon these results and learnings and continue testing separate elements on this page to get a better understanding of the motivation of the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family has a Marriage Assessment on their website to help couples grow and strengthen their marriage. Before an end-user gets to the actual assessment there is a landing page with value proposition and a call-to-action. In an effort to increase conversion on this page of people getting to the assessment, they developed a treatment. They hypothesized that by simplifying the page – including removing the call-to-action at the top of the page – they might be able to motivate more people to take it by leading with the value proposition of the assessment. The goal of the treatment was to eliminate any elements on the page that would cause potential friction in the conversion process. They split the traffic coming to the assessment page and tested the control against the treatment.

experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in a 5% decrease in conversion rate. Having Fr. John sign the page could have resulted in unnecessary friction for people reading the page due to the placement of the signature. Instead of looking at the call to action directly below it, they were looking at the signature, and this could have diverted their eyes and attention from what was important on the page.

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action to the top of a simplified landing page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding the button back in to the top of the page increase acquisition on the simplified page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2017 - 11/30/2017
By adding the button back in to the top of the page, we still weren’t able to increase the traffic going to the assessment. We saw a slight decrease in conversion – not as significant as when the button was completely removed – but still a dip.
We hypothesize that this audience is so highly motivated that they don’t actually need this assessment landing page. We propose testing removing this in-between page altogether and sending people straight to the assessment to get more people to take and complete it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family offers a Marriage Assessment on their website. In a previous test on the assessment landing page, we learned that a simplified page where we removed elements of friction actually decreased conversion because we removed a button at the top of the page that would take this highly motivated audience directly to the assessment. To build upon those learnings, we hypothesized that we could increase conversion by keeping and testing the simplified version again but simply just adding back in the button to the assessment at the top of the page. We split the traffic and tested it.

experiments.title: How removing creative elements affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removal of creative elements like the graphical banner improve clarity and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2015 - 12/07/2015
Removing the banner not only decreased conversion by nearly 20%, it also directionally reduced the average gift of those who signed up and then made a donation.
This reminded us that, while clarity is an important factor in the value proposition, appeal and credibility are essential as well. In this case, the graphical elements seem to add credibility to the course.
In addition, while the use of the word “study” might convey work, it also seems to contribute to the credibility of the course, and may align with a key motivation of visitors to the page.
Though other experiment have shown that removing creative elements from the page might improve conversion, this instance proves yet again that what might be a “best practice” for one landing page must be tested on another.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course studying the Gospel of John that was enrolling students at a rapid clip. DTS wanted to see if removing creative elements would improve the clarity of the offer by allowing the eye to focus on the headline, which delivered the value proposition. They also wanted to test a different headline that used another word than “Study”, which conveyed work to the visitor.
So they launched an experiment to determine whether streamlining the page would improve clarity and increase visitor-to-student conversion.

experiments.title: How reducing friction on a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in petition signatures.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2022 - 10/09/2022
When reviewing a petition landing page for possible barriers, we noticed there was a conflicting call to action on the page standing in the way of the email acquisition form: Click here for a printable version. We were curious if the link to the PDF was distracting users from completing the online form and was interrupting the cognitive forward momentum of page visitors. We designed a simple treatment: removing the interfering call to action from the landing page. We split traffic from 2 petitions 50/50 to this experience to observe what would happen to our email conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that reducing friction by removing the printable PDF link and call to action resulted in a 4.1% increase in email conversion rate with 99.4% level of confidence. Taking out this one step in the process led us to conversion lifts among all devices and all age segments of our audience. However, we also noticed a notable shift on a downstream metric that should be considered. Donations experienced a 48.5% decrease with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to run additional tests of this same concept on other petitions to see if these results are consistent. Due to this shift in donor response, it's possible that the printed version of the PDF is provided some sense of credibility to petition signers that is leading them to be more willing to convert on the instant donation page that follows it. If this is the case, we will need to test into an alternate method of displaying the option to access a printable version that does not interrupt the flow of the page or prohibit people from signing the petition.

experiments.title: How an “us vs. them” copy angle on a petition landing page impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an “us” vs. “them” copy angle in which we isolate the opposition and unite against the opposition for potential petition signers will achieve an increase in sign up rate and conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/02/2022 - 10/01/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is designed to build momentum towards a free and independent Texas, calling out important fundamental beliefs among Texans and asking people to stand behind them by signing the petition. The petition landing page was experiencing a low sign up rate, and we wondered if the lead in copy to the petition was lacking motivation.
We tested an “us vs. them” copy angle against the control copy which was more organization-centric and focused on the importance of keeping Texas true to its values as more and more people migrate to the state. For the treatment, the “us” is loyal conservative Texans and the “them” is politicians, policymakers, and progressives working against “us”. We thought calling out the “bad guy” and stating how we’re united against the “bad guy” would enhance motivation and convince more people to sign the petition. We also thought the motivation would carry over to the instant donation page and have a positive impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Sign ups (email acqusition) showed little difference between the control and treatment; the treatment saw a 2.3% decrease in sign ups with only a 50.7% level of confidence. However, the treatment saw a valid 74.4% decrease in donations. 11 donations were made to the control compared to only 3 to the treatment. This tells us that any motivation created from the "us" vs. "them" copy angle did not carry over to the donation page.
Interestingly, mobile users made all 11 donations to the control and the treatment actually saw more donations among desktop users. Mobile also had far more samples than desktop. This indicates that mobile users may not have been turned off by the "us" vs. "them" copy angle, but that they preferred briefer copy as they were reaching the actual petition. This experiment may be worth testing again, but with a briefer version of the treatment. If we do retest and the control wins again, then it would be more clear that the control copy angle is more impactful than the treatment copy angle.

0% increase in traffic
				× 79.2% decrease in conversion rate

experiments.title: How changing Facebook ad copy for a resource offer impacted email sign ups for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding new copy and images for potential email sign-ups will achieve a change in clicks and sign-ups..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/09/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of their most popular offers is a brochure focusing on the most popular Parables of Jesus.
For this experiment, we tested both a different copy treatment with a different approach to what the brochure offers, as well as images that showed the inside of the brochure.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the copy and images so they gave a Facebook audience more insight into what they were going to be receiving, we saw a 199% increase in email sign up rates with 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the control copy was too generic to interest people and get them to fill out a form on the landing page with all their information (the resource is mailed); once they got an 'inside' view of the content, they were much more likely to sign up to receive it.
This is an experiment we ran across multiple offers like this for Pathway to Victory, with similar results. The next iteration of this test would be to focus on isolating which component of the test (copy, image, etc) really led to the experiment results.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a graphic to a Facebook ad for a Facebook audeince will achieve a change in email sign-ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2022 - 01/31/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of their most popular offers is a brochure discussing angels and demons. This brochure is offered, and mailed, for free to anyone that wants to sign up for it on the PTV website.
In this test we wanted to see if adding in a free offer graphic call out made the ad stand out a bit more on Facebook, and thus result in higher email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a 17% increase in email sign-up conversions and a 100% level of confidence, this treatment showed how just a simple graphic change on a Facebook ad, emphasizing the free nature of the offer, has a measurable impact on offers.

experiments.title: How asking for a sign up first impacts email acquisition rate on an ebook offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pushing up the name acquisition for to the top of the page for prospective ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2022 - 08/07/2022
The Navigators was running an offer on Discipleship 101/Spiritual Mentorship. In order to increase the email acquisition rate on the landing page, we wanted to redesign the landing page so that it closely resembled the landing page of another Navigators’ resource. We wanted to see if this new template would result in more names. This new template would push the name acquisition form to the top of the landing page in hopes to get the end user to act quicker in filling out the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 16% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 99%. These results indicate that the user actually needs to read through the value proposition copy laid out on the landing page before deciding to fill out the form. The new template did not allow for sufficient enough copy at the beginning of their experience to explain the value of the resource they were about to receive.

experiments.title: How focusing on the outcomes you can achieve with an offer impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the outcomes of getting an offer for our target audience will achieve an increase in email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2022 - 08/05/2022
As a part of our own optimization efforts for an existing offer to fundraisers, we decided to look critically at one of our most popular offer landing pages, and come up with a new approach to see if it would improve email signup rates.
In reviewing the “control” version of the landing page — the message was focused on “what you will get.” We theorized that our target audience would only want to get this offer to achieve something. That led us to think through what it was that the target audience would want to achieve with this offer — and that led us to derive that they would like to raise more money online, and produce better results.
Therefore, we wrote a treatment to our landing page focused on “outcome-oriented” language, as opposed to what you get right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97.2% level of confidence, we increased email signup rates by +15.4%.
This is an exciting study because it proves that focusing on what motivates your target audience to take action and download your offer should truly be the center of the messaging as to WHY you want them to have it.
There can be other motivation types beyond "raising more money" or "increasing performance" that may motivate this audience to download at a higher rate, so further experimentation may be required, however, these results are promising and should be considered when developing new offers, or trying to optimize old ones.

experiments.title: The impact of clearer and more efficient value proposition copy on email conversion for a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy length and increasing the clarity and strength of the value proposition will increase email conversion by reducing friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2022 - 08/14/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They use content offers and premium offers on Facebook to acquire new donors and have been very successful with this approach. For their content offer, Soul-Care Staycation, they wanted to increase email acquisition on the landing page. In reviewing the page, we found that the copy was long on mobile devices, the primary source of traffic to the page. It also lacked a clear headline and the value proposition could have been stronger. We hypothesized that a stronger, more effecient value proposition would increase email conversion.
For the treatment we changed the headline from simply the title of the free resource to one that conveyed more of the value of the offer, “your free guide to essential soul-care.” We also made the copy more conversational and included highlights of the eBook’s exclusive nature and content designed to help the subscriber “take a breath and focus on what’s truly important.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that this clearer, more efficient value propositional copy increased email acquisition for all traffic with a 92% Level of Confidence, but most importantly, increased it for NEW visitors which were the primary source of subscribers for this acquisition offer. In reviewing the data for other segments we didn't have concerns with rolling out the treatment for all traffic. We will use a similar approach when launching new content offer landing pages in the future.

experiments.title: How adding credibility through evidentials on a landing page increased course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing authority and evidentials for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in free online course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2022 - 07/18/2022
A new free online course was taught by the President of the Seminary. We had seen other organizations using evidentials in the form of testimonials to improve email acquisition results and we wanted to test this on the registration page to see if we could increase conversion by adding an element to enhance the visitor’s level of trust and perceived authority of the offer. We built a treatment that included a photo and quote from the online course instructor about what key learnings they would take away from the course by sharing what he personally has learned from studying the book.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the treatment for one month, we saw a 34.7% increase in email conversion rate with 99.9% level of confidence so the results are valid. We found that adding the President's name, likeness, and personal testimonial and affiliation with the course to the body of the acquisition page gave the offer more credibility by providing evidentials to why a person should sign up for the course and increased the likelihood that a visitor would give away their email address to move forward in signing up for the course. We will want to test this concept using other professors for other courses to see if the credibility holds equal weight across other faculty members or if using the Seminary President gave the offer more credibility. We'll find additional opportunities to promote trust and authority to see if this lift can be replicated for multiple course offerings.
