experiments.title: The impact on email acquisition of greater clarity, exclusivity, and credibility on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding exclusivity, clarity, and credibility to the landing page will increase email acquisition conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2022 - 05/26/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life is one of Buckner’s most important online premium offers on Facebook. It has the highest number of emails acquired, yet its landing page has one of the lowest conversion rates.
In reviewing the landing page, we believed that we could strengthen the value proposition by emphasizing the value of the devotional offer to families and reducing the frequency the copy mentioned the “Buckner” brand. We also wanted to add copy highlighting the exclusive nature of the offer and credibility from the many other families who’ve been helped by it. “Thousands of moms and dads like you have become more like Christ through Buckner International devotionals and resources. ”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for just over a month, we found a statistically valid negative impact on email conversion. While we know that greater clarity, exclusivity, and credibility improves conversion rates on landing pages, the copy we ran did not properly convey these elements to the visitor. We will revisit the page and test several new tactical elements before revisiting the value proposition and copy.

experiments.title: How minimized navigation and design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a stripped down page for birthday searchers will achieve an increase in sponsor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/16/2021
Compassion International had found “birthday search” to be a compelling offer for new donor acquisition. For many years, they had leveraged a stripped down page to present this offer, with confetti and a headline that gave the visitor a reason to give. However, they had never tested this against a similar landing page experience that lived within the general Compassion design. They wanted to understand whether the old template, which was outside of current brand standards, was worth keeping up. They decided to test this through an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the stripped down navigation (which removed the Compassion "login" link), produced a 36.9% decrease in conversion. However, it's important to understand exactly what happened here, as there were two different responses based on the device type.
First, mobile visitors had a 45.0% decrease in conversion - with a 100% level of confidence. Since most of the traffic was mobile, this was the deciding factor in the test. However, desktop traffic had a 235.8% increase in conversion - with a 94% level of confidence.
This shows that different designs can produce opposite results with the same page—which stresses the importance of responsive design. It also may hint that something was wrong with the mobile version of the treatment that might be corrected to produce a similar result.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a landing page with clarifying copy and removing navigation effects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the navigation and providing clarity in the page copy for users signing up for this online course will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/04/2022
Luther Seminary had been running their acquisition pages with navigation in the header and footer of the page. They wanted to test what would happen if we removed the navigation from the page to see if this would increase email acquisition.
We tested this concept on their Cosmic Adventure online course landing page. On top of this page having the navigation bars, the offer was not performing well for a consistent amount of time, so they also wanted to test changing the headline and body copy of the page to see if providing clarity for the user as to what they would receive in this course would help increase email acquisition.
The original page language was vague throughout the headline, sub-headline, and body copy. Within the new copy, we wanted to be very specific in the headline and subheadline by stating exactly what this course would do for them – give them answers to questions between science and faith through an informative course. Within the body copy, we wanted to focus on curiosity and clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment (treatment page 2) produced a valid increase in the email sign-up rate of 44.7% with a 96.6 level of confidence. In looking at all three variants, we believe that removing the navigation in the header and footer provided the biggest impact on our sign-up rate. On top of that, changing the page copy to be more clarifying for the user as to what this course will give them ultimately provided us with the valid increase needed.
It is recommended that this be tested on other acquisition pages separately, so we can see the true impact of removing navigation vs adding clarifying copy.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 113.8% increase with a 86% level of confidence.

				gift).

experiments.title: How focusing on the individual’s felt need affects email acquisition rate on an offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing an individual’s felt need on a landing page will result in a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 07/30/2022
IMB had an ebook called “Loving the Lost” that had a pre-existing landing page and funnel. When we relaunched this offer with an entirely new funnel we wanted to use the landing page copy of the previous funnel and test it against our new landing page. The new landing page was focused on the micro-problem, the individual’s felt need. Our hypothesis was that people would be more likely to fill out the form if they understood how this ebook is helpful to them.
The control for this experiment used the pre-existing landing page’s copy. The treatment had the new individual-focused language. Traffic to this funnel was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 17% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 98%. This means more people were motivated to download the ebook when they understood how this resource would personally help them and solve their problems: how can you love people that you do not know. The control was much more focused on the macro-problem of lostness and had a short invitation for the individual to join the IMB in prayer. This indicates that IMB's audience wants to know how they personally are affected by the larger problem at hand and focusing on the felt needs of the individual can increase the motivation to take action.

experiments.title: How focusing on content of an ebook vs the context affected email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the offer as an “ebook”  for site visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2022
RTB has been running an offer called “If God created everything, who created God?” Throughout the promotion, this offer has been couched as a free chapter download of a book. The low sign up rate on the landing page indicated that there was friction on the page that deterred people from signing up. We hypothesized that framing this offer as a free chapter of a book added confusion for the user. If a person is new to the organization and they don’t know the book or the author, including this information on the landing page may be an additional barrier.
For our experiment we decided to exclude this information and simplify the presentation of the offer as just an “ebook.” We believe that the content of the ebook is more important to the end user than the context of the resource (being a chapter from a book).
Traffic to this offer was primarily driven by pad facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 23.6% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 96.7%. Our hypothesis was proven correct: site visitors were more inclined to request this free ebook when we simplified the language and focused on the content of the ebook. While the organization may deem it significant that this is a chapter from a newly released book, someone who is not connected to the ministry may not find that piece of information valuable.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity in the body copy of a landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the body copy of a landing page will result in greater conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2022 - 05/28/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. For this experiment, they wanted to test if adding more clarity into the body copy of the landing page for a popular content offer would result in a greater number of email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was validated with a 98.8% Level of Confidence showing a decrease in conversion rate. The practice of adding more clarity into landing page copy has yielded positive results in previous experiments, but in this case we actually saw a decrease in conversion response. Several factors could be at play leading to this decrease...such as the overall layout of the image, form layout, and even too much body copy for this particular audience.
To continue to strive for improvement of conversions, we suggest trying a few tactical changes like moving first and last name form fields on the same line, and aligning the image of eBook with the body copy. In addition, we might try adding some social proof to the page.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and urgency on a landing page impacted survey submissions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and urgency toward a specific goal for survey takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2022 - 05/12/2022
ACI was running a survey with high volume but a lower than ideal conversion rate. We evaluated the page for opportunities to increase email acquisition. The first treatment we developed included the element of social proof and let people know that over 55,000 other people had already submitted the survey to increase legitimacy of the offer. We visually highlighted this with a live counter at the top of the page. For the second treatment, we added language to let visitors know we had a goal of 100,000 submissions and their survey response would count toward that goal. We also emphasized this with a progress bar at the top of the page and asked for their help in getting closer to reaching the goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the experiment for one month, when looking at overall traffic, the second treatment had a 3.4% lift in email acquisition with an 81.9% level of confidence. However, when we assessed mobile traffic specifically, we saw a 6.4% directional increase in email acquisition with 94.1% level of confidence on the first treatment and a valid 9.1% increase in email acquisition with 99.3% level of confidence. Additionally, when we isolated traffic by new visitors we also had a 4.9% directional increase in email acquisition with a 91% level of confidence. We believe this was due to the increase in urgency and motivation by highlighting the progress towards the goal on the landing page for page visitors. Because mobile visitors make up almost half of the traffic to the page and neither treatment was showing a decline for overall traffic, we will move forward with rolling out the second treatment to 100% of traffic for future survey takers. For future testing, we could also increase urgency on the landing page by adding a countdown timer or by indicating a specific deadline in the copy to reach 100K responses.

experiments.title: How removing friction from a monthly donor landing page increased donor conversions for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction  for prospective monthly donors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2022 - 04/25/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry with a donor base built on premium offers that are publicized on both offline (TV, radio, etc) and online channels. They have developed a monthly donor program called Pathway Partner, which has a variety of levels and ways to become a Partner. On the control version, there are 12 different combinations of ways you can qualify to become a Partner; we wanted to test whether reducing the amount of friction on that landing page increased monthly donor conversion.
Instead of giving donors the choice between three ‘circles’, we looked at the data and reduced the choice to a single circle level. We also tested updating the design of the landing page, including adding copy at the top that explained the benefits of the program.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By reducing the complication on the page, and providing a single path for potential monthly donors to take to become a Pathway Partner, the donor conversion rate increased by 146%.
The design of the page--less image heavy, with copy focused on why someone should become a monthly donor, the impact they could make, and not just what they will receive as part of the program--was more conducive to monthly donor recruitment. Moreover, while new visitors to the page had a 107 increase in conversions, those that were returning visitors--and therefore had already looked at the various premium offers--had a 343% increase in conversion rates. As this segment of visitors had already been exposed to the impact supporting Pathway to Victory could make, they were much more likely to become a monthly donor and have their impact go 12x as far.

experiments.title: How a visually redesigned landing page impacted email conversion for a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a visually redesigned landing page for a content offer will achieve an increase in email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2022 - 02/18/2022
When launching a new acquisition offer, we wanted to test a more highly visual layout that would be more in line with the organization’s branding and design themes against the standard stripped-down acquisition page. The designed layout is similar to past offers TGC had displayed for its visitors, but this was the first time they were using clear value proposition and an optimized call to action so we were curious which version would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the treatment produced a 7.6% increase in emails acquired. Looking deeper into the results, however, we see that this actually had a 32% decrease for visitors in the age 55-64 segment. This is important to note as this is a segment that generally has a higher propensity to give. Based on this analysis we could look at targeting visitors based on segment and leading them to a custom landing page based on their likelihood to convert. There are a few elements on the treatment page we would like to further test including moving all value proposition above the form and moving the graphic to the right-hand side of the page that we will build as another experiment and continue to improve email acquisition rates for this page.

experiments.title: How redesigning a landing page affects email sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the landing page for users will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/30/2022 - 02/19/2022
Cosmic Adventure is an online course by Luther Seminary that discusses how science and faith enhance each other.
We tested two factors on the Cosmic Adventure landing page:
Removing the navigation bar and footer
Being more descriptive in the headline and subheadline
We wanted to test removing the navigation and footer because we’ve found in previous experiments that those items on a landing page can be a distraction causing users to click out of the page.
We wanted to test the headline and subheadline with new copy to be more descriptive as to what the user is actually signing up for. On the control page, the headline says the course name but fails to immediately say what the course is about.
The user experienced the same funnel through Facebook ads and a housefile email with the test being only on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, in this experiment, the more specific headline on the landing page decreased email acquistion.
We believe that the language used in the treatment landing page was too descriptive and long for the headline and subheadline of the page. The headline and subheadline of the control were short and more eye-catching which increased the appeal of the offer rather than a user having to "learn" and "register" like the treatment page referred to.
This language can also be tested on other acquisition landing pages.
Email acquisitions experienced a 43.7% decrease with a 99.9% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How landing page form placement impacts cognitive momentum and subsequent donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the form “above the fold” on the landing page for prospective donors will achieve an increase in email signups and donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/26/2021
As a part of optimizing a new eBook offer, Americans for Prosperity was looking at ways to increase email signup rates through the landing page. On the list of ideas was simply moving the form up on the page so that it would be “above the fold” on the page.
Would this increase email signup rates? Subsequently, how would it impact subsequent donor conversion rates, as well?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97% level of confidence, we observed a decrease of 79.8% in donor conversion rate.
Moreover, we also noticed a negligible difference in email signup rates (-1.4%, 14% LoC) in the process, as well.
Our hypothesis is that by moving the form up and "above the fold" — we actually disrupted the "cognitive momentum" in the process for a donor-inclined audience, which is why we saw a decrease in donor conversion rate on the subsequent donation page.
On top of that, we didn't see any increases (but rather a small decline) in email signup rates, as well.
Further experimentation with creating donor motivation from the landing page to the subsequent donation page should be explored in this case.
Some other notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment include the following:
Revenue experienced a 89.5% decrease with a 90% level of confidence.
Finally, here are additional observations of the impact the treatment had on other visitor segments within the experiment:
Mobile visitors had an 83.0% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The impact of a standard Facebook event vs a custom event on volunteer signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using Facebook’s registration conversion event for our volunteer campaign will achieve greater conversion rates and lower cost-per-signup.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/13/2021
For more than a year, we’ve been running a campaign on Facebook to acquire volunteers for our Making Life Disciples initiative. We had an ad that pointed to a pledge signup page. Those who signed the pledge would then land on a volunteer signup page. We had been using a custom conversion that targeted the volunteer confirmation page. We knew from our donation experiments that using the standard “purchase” event in Facebook increased conversions when tested against a custom conversion targeting our donation pages. We wondered if a similar principle would work with testing Facebook’s standard “registration” conversion event against our custom conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at email acquisition, we increased signatures on the pledge (step 1) by 155% by using the standard event. Now, signatures that came through the custom event converted to signups at about a 30% increased rate but we only had 60% validity on that metric. Since we were getting so many more users through step 1 using the standard conversion, even at a lower conversion rate on step 2, we still converted more pledge signers into volunteers. We also reduced our cost per signup from $37 to $15 by using this standard event.
It is possible that Facebook's algorithm was unable to collect enough conversions on the custom event to properly optimize for it. But either way, the standard event was clearly better and led to more emails acquired and a lower volunteer signup conversion cost.

experiments.title: How presenting a micro ask before the macro, impacts email conversion from a pop-up on the website
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a micro ask before the macro ask increase email acquisition from the pop-up on the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2021 - 05/27/2021
For the past five months, Save the Children has been testing into the email acquisition pop-up on their website. The majority of these experiments have been focused around messaging and value proposition. But all of these experiments have resulted in no positive or negative impact on conversion. As we continue to test into this feature on the website and look for ways to optimize the experience, we hypothesized that we might be able to engage more website users and ultimately increase name conversion by presenting an easier engagement experience on the pop-up before we ask them to give us their email address.
To test this, we presented a “yes”/”no” question that a user would answer before the email acquisition form field would show. The “yes”/”no” question was the same sentiment as the control – “Would you like to stay informed?”. The “Yes” button was highlighted red. When a person would click on the “Yes” button, an email form would slide out and the copy read, “Great! Please provide your email address to sign up.” If a person clicked “No”, the pop-up would simply disappear.
The control presented the same kind of email acquisition opportunity, but in the form of a statement – “Sign up to stay informed”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 10 days, we found that the "Yes"/"No" micro ask increased email acquisition by 35%! This valid lift happened on both desktop and mobile devices. In fact, mobile devices had the most significant valid increase in name conversion of 42% (99% LoC). This experiment shows us the importance of engaging people through an easy entrance point results in more people likely to convert. This micro ask increases a persons likelihood and motivation to complete the macro ask.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enhancing the value proposition for visitors giving a club membership as a gift will achieve a higher conversion rate on the landing page.

We produced a treatment that was seasonally relevant and reminded visitors of the value that would be included when they gave a membership. We altered the headline and body copy to draw attention to the fact that this would be a “meaningful gift with lasting impact” and would “bring joy, imagination, and smiles year-round!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we saw was an 87.4% lift in conversion rate on the treatment. The increase held true across nearly all visitor segments. Remember this lift came from simply adding 2-3 sentences of additional value proposition in the copy on the page above the form, reminding visitors that their decision to give this Club Membership would create a transformational experience in a child's life and faith. Based on these results, we'll look for other opportunities to build a stronger value proposition for other membership acquisition forms running on the site.

experiments.title: How removing detail from an open letter impacted signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the page and removing historical context for prospective petition signers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/30/2021
As a part of the promotion of an open letter to acquire emails and donors, Americans for Prosperity was running a campaign to find people who wanted to tell Congress that they should reject the PRO Act.
As the campaign continued, the email signature rate declined over time. In an attempt to improve email signup rates, we decided to remove some of the historical context and additional detail to shorten and streamline the page so that more people would sign it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed that the email signup rate actually decreased by -11%.
Our hypothesis on why is that the ads used in the promotional campaigns present a simplified "yes" or "no" question — which has significantly increased clickthrough rates — is producing a more motivated click, but without the context for the other aspects of the bill and why it should be rejected.
Therefore, removing the additional historical context and detail likely caused these higher volume of more motivated clicks to sign the letter less when removing the additional detail and historical context they needed to become more informed on the bill itself, and therefore sign it.
Further experimentation should be considered to drive increase congruence between pre-click and post-click experiences so that there is not a moment of re-orientation after the click to see if that increases email signup rates instead.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the sign-in options for desktop visitors will achieve an increase in created users and sites.

Ended On: 02/27/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
experiments.keyLearnings: The SSO option produced a significant decrease in conversion for desktop visitors. There was a slight increase in mobile and tablet signups, which leads to a secondary hypothesis about the ease that SSO options provide. However, to avoid a significant downturn in new sites created, they decided to relaunch the experiment just for mobile and tablet users to see if the lift validated.

experiments.researchQuestion: How will a slide in email acquisition offer impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2021 - 02/23/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The copy and and trigger timing of the pop-up has been tested in the past with little to no impact on conversion, so we hypothesized that the placement of the feature may impact conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that functioned more as a slide in feature on the bottom of the page than a forced pop-up. No copy or design of the feature was changed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant decrease in donor conversion from the slide in feature compared to the forced pop-up. With an almost 41% decrease in emails acquired, we can learn that the placement of the offer does have an impact on the number of people signing up to stay connected with Save the Children. The pop-up is almost much more disruptive - drawing attention to the feature and capturing more names than the slide in.

Continued testing is needed to really understand the motivation of this audience and what elements of friction exist in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How simplifying a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the petition for simplified ad clickers will achieve an increase in email signup rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
As a part of an open letter (petition) promotional campaign that Americans for Prosperity was running, they wanted to see if they could experiment with increasing email signup rates on the massive increases in click-through traffic received from their simplified ad approach.
As expected, far more people were clicking, but their email signup rate was dropping because the pre-click and post-click experiences weren’t directly congruent if the end user did not read the corresponding ad text to know that if they clicked, they would be asked to sign a petition.
Overall volume of emails was much higher with the simplified ad experience, but the organization wanted to see if they could capture a higher percentage of this overall increase in visitors to the open letter (petition).
Therefore, we attempted to simplify the open letter by removing some additional detail from the page, thereby shortening the letter altogether to see if we could remove friction in the email signup process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in email signup rate of -11.9%.
Our theory is that the additional context and detail that was removed from the petition actually provided less on-page context for the higher number of visitors received from the simplified ad experience — which served as a foundation to motivate people who clicked without understanding what they were being asked to do to finish the petition signing process.
Further experimentation with creating alignment between the pre-click and post-click experience of the "simplified ad experience" is advised to focus on increasing email signup rates.

experiments.title: The impact of a Yes/No Image Ad on Facebook for volunteer recruitment
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enabling immediate feedback via the image design for viewers on Facebook will achieve greater volunteer conversion rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2021
We wanted to test whether using an image that enabled viewers on Facebook to feel like they were providing immediate feedback would increase conversion. So, we designed a static image with the question of our Facebook Survey on it and a big Yes and a big No option for them to “click” on. While not serving as working buttons, we hypothesized that merely simulating a button would be enough to increase conversion rate.
We tested this against a short video ad created using Facebook’s image/video tool.
experiments.keyLearnings: The feedback ad improved conversion on every metric. Not only did we increase volunteer signups by 191%, we decreased the cost per signup 76% (from $37.95 to 8.93) and the cost per click 81% ($0.37 to $0.07). WE are testing this now on our monthly donor campaign to see if it has similar results.

experiments.title: How Using Landing Page Copy in the Body of a Prospecting Email Directly Impacts Revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using landing page copy in the body of an email increase revenue for email prospecting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2020
Heritage Foundation had been using unique email creative for their email prospecting campaigns in an effort to increase Membership sign-ups and ultimately, revenue. To see if revenue would increase, they experimented with inserting the entire copy directly from their Membership landing page into the body of the email.
Pre-Experiment Hypothesis: The Heritage Foundation Membership landing page provides the best information for why individuals should become a Heritage Foundation Member. As opposed to having to attract individuals first with an email before they see any relevant information, why not present the relevant information immediately to them in their email inbox?
experiments.keyLearnings:
*Important note: For this experiment, except for adding an intro and outro paragraph, Heritage Foundation did not alter any of their Membership landing page copy.
There was a validated increase in revenue:
With a 100% level of confidence, revenue per visitor increased 93.6%, and the average gift doubled.
Conversion rates improved:
With a 50% level of confidence, conversion rates increased by nearly 40%.
Email Click-Thru-Rates Increased:
With a 99.4% level of confidence, email click-thru-rates increased by 55.2%.
*Note: Increasing Click-Thru-Rates are particularly important for optimization and performance lift. Click-Thru-Rates are step #1 in increasing revenue in a campaign, as you are obtaining more traffic to the page.
Conclusion:
Providing relevant information up front for individuals, as opposed to shielding information behind a click, increases the overall performance of email and ad campaigns by increasing click-thru-rates, revenue, and average gift amount.

experiments.title: How using a dedicated landing page instead of a homepage affected new site signups
experiments.researchQuestion: How will using a dedicated landing page instead of a homepage affect new site signups?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/13/2020
CaringBridge launched a Facebook marketing campaign to try to attract users to create new sites. Since the homepage was the primary place that new site traffic landed, they initially directed visitors there. However, there were lots of additional links, calls to action, and other content on the homepage. They worried that these additional items might reduce the likelihood that someone would create a new CaringBridge site on that page.
So, they developed a standalone landing page on Unbounce, and created a radical redesign with the sole focus call-to-action being to start a new site. They did this because the Facebook traffic call to action was to start a site, not to engage in any of the other content on the page. Therefore, they thought it would be beneficial to focus the call to action on the intended conversion to since they knew the intent of the traffic being driven from the ad. However, they wanted to test this to ensure that it actually did improve conversion. If they targeted this landing page test towards users who had two factors: they were coming from Facebook ads, and they were not logged into CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How congruency between email and landing page value propositions affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would increasing congruency of the value propositions increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2016 - 09/07/2016
For Harvest’s September monthly appeal, they hypothesized that there was a disconnect between the email and landing page value proposition. The email was focused on the value of the monthly resource and the landing page focused on the value of giving to Harvest Ministries.
They developed a treatment that made a tighter connection between the two value propositions and made the message from the email to the landing page more congruent.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By reinforcing and bringing clarity to the value proposition from the email to the landing page, we were able to create a stronger and more cohesive message – resulting in an increase in donations by 85.7%.
It is important that a consistent message is presented between an email and a coordinating landing page. When there is a more congruent message, the force of the value proposition will be greater and more people will donate because of it.

experiments.title: How the headline on a blog post Facebook ad affects landing page views

rows.name: Headline Using Post Title and Basic CTA

experiments.researchQuestion: Which style of Facebook ad headline will drive more traffic to the blog post?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2019 - 11/08/2019
In this experiment, we were launching Facebook ads to drive traffic to a newly updated year-end fundraising blog. In developing the ads, we wondered what type of headline would be most effective at driving visits to the blog post. We have seen strong call-to-actions be effective for conversion focused ads, but wondered if that would hold up in an ad purely designed to drive traffic.
Version A used simply the post title as it might appear if someone shared the post organically. Version B used a headline that packed the specific value of the blog post into an action statement. Version C took a hybrid approach of putting the blog title first, followed by a simple call-to-action separated by a pipe.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The hybrid approach was the overwhelming winner, increasing landing page views (people that made it to the page and loaded it) by 190%. This shows us a few things...
A simple blog post title is not enough to call a Facebook viewer to take an action, even though this is the more natural appearance of an organic post.The value heavy CTA, although we have seen it be effective for driving someone to convert on a specific offer, is not nearly as powerful for driving pure traffic. In this case, our goal is to drive traffic to build an audience, not get a specific download or opt-in instantly.The hybrid approach clearly communicates the topic, and it clearly communicates the medium. The more action oriented headline could cause confusion about what the actual content is on the other end. But the hybrid approach makes it abundantly clear what is expected of the Facebook viewer: "Read the post".

experiments.title: Helpful language vs admonishment on a devotional landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more conversational and helpful copy outperform  spiritually obligatory language on our devotional landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2019 - 11/06/2019
After our test on the Defending Your Pro-Life Beliefs course page where we found that conversational language directed at helping visitors outperformed more militant “Learn to defend your pro-life beliefs” language, we wanted to try a similar approach to our successful 40-Day devotional Prayers for Life. Our control version focused on the need to be more like Jesus and prayerfully interceding for the unborn at risk of abortion. The control copy focused on how we “can’t afford” to stop praying and used heavier language to make visitors feel obligated to pray, and hopefully, download the devotional.
Our variation used language directed at providing a “daily reminder” to pray to a person’s inbox. The new copy assumed that visitors were already praying for the unborn, rather than assuming that they weren’t.
We tested this new approach on both the landing page and in the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: During the entirety of the 3-week experiment, the new language outperformed the old. We decided to use Unbounce's "smart traffic" feature rather than a straight A/B test for this experiment and the editor showed that this approach delivered an additional 40 signups versus just picking a single "winner" during the 3-week test. While the results had not yet reached 95% certainty, the consistent improvement of the new language coupled with the prior testing of similar language on the Defending your pro-life beliefs course which also performed better, caused us to choose a winner.

Our Making Life Disciples landing page is used to acquire violunteers to bring our curriculum to local churches. The page had used internal branding about Making Life Disciples and why this curriculum was important. We wondered if this extra branding was creating friction by introducing an as yet unknown quantity, Making Life Disciples, into a discussion about abortion in the church. We hypothesized that stripping out this language and focusing on the network of volunteers working to transform the church’s response to abortion would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the sub-branding of Making Life Disciples from the landing page, we significantly increased volunteer conversion. We will continue to test this learning on other landing pages in the months to come.

experiments.title: What is the impact of aligning an ad and landing page image with the subject of a pledge?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning an ad and landing page image with the subject of a pledge increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2019
In the past, we’ve found that images of fetuses worked better than babies on our pledges against abortion. However, our most recent pledge was against infanticide. I hypothesized that an image of a newborn baby would increase conversion as infanticide is the killing of a newborn, not the killing of a fetus. So I built a new variation of the landing page where the only change was the image. I also created new Facebook ads that featured the same baby image to direct to the new landing page. Facebook was setup to choose whichever ad was converting more donors and adjust spend accordingly. I set up the experiment so that anyone clicking the ad with the fetus image would be directed to the landing page with the fetus image and anyone clicking the ad with the baby image would go directly to the landing page with the baby image. This would ensure continuity of images between ad and landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did the baby image landing page increase conversion by 72.8%, the baby image ad performed significantly better than the control with a fetus image. The baby image brought in 383% more donations than the fetus image (29 to 6) during the test. This test indicates that aligning the image with the content of the ad/landing page increases conversion.

experiments.title: Impact of streamlining the layout and copy of a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Would streamlining the copy, removing the separate header, and bringing congruency between the header and form increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2019 - 04/23/2019
Our free online course, Choosing Life, had not been performing as well as we wanted. We were driving traffic to the landing page through Facebook ads linking to a pledge. After signing the pledge, visitors would see the course signup page. In reviewing the page, I noticed that the template separated the headline from the body. I also noticed that some of the text seemed unnecessary. Furthermore, the headline was not clearly linked to the CTA on the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ensuring that all the text was in the same "box" on the template ensured that visitors wouldn't gloss over the headline. By changing the headline, it was clear from the start what action we wanted them to take. Finally, by streamlining the copy, the value proposition was clearer as well.
Copy length should be long enough to articulate the value of the offer, but short enough to not lose the visitor.

experiments.title: How a radical landing page redesign affects name acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a more evocative landing page with social proof outperform a more straightforward, functional landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2015 - 02/02/2015
Online for Life developed a petition, the Pro-Life Declaration to acquire new email addresses for their house file. Initially, the petition was a flat, content-focused design that looked exactly like…a petition. We wanted to test this against a radical redesign centered around a compelling image combined with the value statement “Life is Precious”. Rather than assume that the site visitor was ready to sign the petition, we assumed that we had to keep selling them once they got on the page. In addition to the new value statement and image, we added a counter at the top to show that by signing the petition, the visitor was joining a much larger group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign delivered a 34.7% lift in the number of people who signed the Declaration — a huge boost to the campaign. This lift proved that the landing page must appeal to the donor. It also reminded us that visuals help sell the ultimate conversion, and we are not done selling once we have the user on the page.

experiments.title: How aligning the landing page copy to the Dear Reader ad still didn’t increase revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would using copy on the landing page work to better and more closely align a Heritage Foundation membership to standing with the president and increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2019 - 02/14/2019
In this iteration of continued experimentation with the “Dear Reader” ads on The Daily Signal, we wanted to experiment with a message that was customized to the content of the “Dear Reader” ads, which positions how to “learn more” about how to “stand with the president”. We have already validated that this treatment (with 100% confidence) will generate +400% increase in click traffic.
We wrote a custom message focused upon continuity between the ad copy and the landing page copy, with more detail to it, then relaunched the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment showed the same results -- that validated with a 100% level of confidence (for the second time) that we could generate +400% more clicks on the "Dear Reader" ad with the "Learn More" button, but with a 99.9% level of confidence, we found that although we had more people see the message, it decreased revenue by -85%.
Further, the treatment validated decrease donor conversion rates, as well (-52%, with a level of confidence of 99.1%).
In reviewing engagement statistics, we noticed that the treatment users bounced from the page at a rate of 95.47%. For reference, the control's bounce rate was only 75.6% (which is more than 20% lower than the treatment).
This tells us that "Dear Reader" ad clickers want to know how they can stand with the president, but they're not yet convinced that activating a Heritage Foundation membership is the best way to do it. So much so, that they immediately leave the page when they are presented with a fundraising page.
For this reason, we ended the experiment and will try another path forward.
Because the control continues to generate the highest revenue, our priority would be to look at ways to increase revenue from that membership acquisition funnel while we develop a path to capitalize upon the significantly higher volume of clickthrough traffic we can generate with the "Learn More" button text.
Our recommendation at this point is to consider adding an engagement pathway between the Heritage Foundation membership ask and the "Dear Reader" ad (ex. petition, pledge, quiz and/or survey), which will then lead the "Dear Reader" traffic through that pathway, and into a relevant ask for them to activate a membership.

experiments.title: How adding a photo to a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a photo to a landing page increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2019 - 03/03/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S.
After sending the first two emails out to a split landing page test, where they tested the presence of gift handles that explained what each suggested gift amount would be used for, they concluded that adding just one line of copy around the gift handles did not increase likelihood of giving.
The Hillsdale team wondered about including a photo of some of the students who received Constitutions onto the landing page. Rather than telling the prospective donor about what they were doing, they could show them with a photo. Would adding a photo of schoolchildren receiving the Constitution increase donations? They hypothesized that adding the photo would show proof, causing an increase in trust, and would increase emotional appeal. They hypothesized that each of these would increase donations. This experiment was tested on their non-donor file.
experiments.keyLearnings: Because this experiment only ran for one day, the experiment results shouldn’t be rolled out to the rest of the donation pages. However, this result was enough for the Hillsdale team to roll out to this landing page for the duration of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a simplified decision process and contextual landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified decision process and contextual landing page design increase sponsorship conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2018 - 07/04/2018
Compassion International receives tens of thousands of child sponsorships every year. Traffic comes to the Compassion homepage from many different sources, with many different motivations, which has made it difficult—if not near impossible to validate homepage tests to increase child sponsorships.
The search process for Compassion.com generally contained four choice options: boy/girl, country, age range, and birthday. Previous tests had shown that prioritizing one (in this case, birthday) as the primary choice could increase “sponsorship starts”, but couldn’t validate a lift after going through their four-step sponsorship process. So with the birthday selection driving more “starts”, the team decided to focus on the next page, which was a grid of children who shared the selected birthday.
There were two primary hypothesized issues with this second page: first, the grid produced an overwhelming number of choices, so many that it required its own “sorter” at the top. Second, the page gave very little context to the action that had preceded it—a birthday selection. The only indication that the children shared the birthday was a single strip of text that read like code: “Showing results for Birth Day: 2, Birth Month: January”.
The team hypothesized that if they could help the user make decisions by reducing decision friction and adding contextual relevance to the page design, they might be able to increase sponsorships.
They crafted a responsive treatment based on a landing page that had produced a lift in sponsorships from banner advertising that featured the birthday search. This new page picked a single child who shared the birthday and featured them at the top, along with contextual design clues (confetti) and a bold headline that declared that the child shared the selected birthday.
They split traffic from the homepage button “search now” to determine if this treatment, combined with the homepage birthday selector, could produce a lift in sponsorships.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with reduced decision-making and contextual design that oriented the reader, produced a 14.4% increase in conversion. This test alone resulted in an additional 1,168 sponsorships each year (a 1.7% increase in annual sponsorships)—and generating nearly $2.9 million dollars (based on Compassion's $2,500 lifetime sponsor value). 

This shows the power of simplifying the process—a single decision, instead of four, followed by a single child, instead of an endless grid. This also implies that the customer might not be as choosy as we think, we might just need to help them find and suggest the right child. It also shows the power of contextualized design—if we can help people understand that they are in the right place, then they are more likely to follow through with the intended action.

experiments.keyLearnings: While both pages performed well, there was a clear decrease in conversion for the page that included the entire eBook. Our hypothesis is that the value proposition was not made clearer by including the eBook content and the added length to the copy muddied the offer.

We then wanted to test if we could get more opt-ins by making it a single checkbox for opting in. The thought was that introducing the negative response as an option could cause more people to respond negatively.
experiments.keyLearnings: Giving visitors only one checkbox, instead of a yes or no had a significant decrease in the number of Caring Partner Opt-Ins. Our hypothesis is that the two checkboxes allowed visitors to give more extensive feedback and was not as easily ignored as the single checkbox.  It was also interesting to note that neither variation had a significant difference in the number of emails acquired, the impact was solely on the number of leads opting in.

Boys Town offers free parenting email series for people. One of these series is about teen discipline. The series have a high appeal and perform really well, but we hypothesized that we might be able to increase name conversion by using a value-focused call-to-action before a person filled out the form to get the offer. The control call-to-action said, “Get your first lesson today by telling us where we can send it.” The treatment said, “Learn how to understand your teen better and discipline more effectively. Get started by telling us where to send your free Teen Discipline series.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The value-focused headline increased name conversion by 4.7%! What this tells us is that by adding a value-focused CTA, we are able to increase motivation of the end-reader by reinforcing the value proposition. This subtle reinforcement in the copy and CTA can have a significant impact if applied across other acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How using reader-centric value proposition impacts conversion on Facebook ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Would organization-centric or reader-centric copy increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2018 - 02/28/2018
Boys Town has an email series they offer parents on how to handle tantrums with their toddler. They use paid media (Facebook) ads to offer this to people. The current Facebook ads uses organization-centric value proposition in the Facebook ad text. We hypothesized that a value proposition focused on the reader and the needs of the reader might increase conversion from the Facebook ad to the acquisition page. We tested this version against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using copy that was reader-centric rather than organization-centric, we were able to increase conversion by 28%. People are more likely to click-through from an ad to a landing page when we can connect with their needs. This approach increases the appeal of the offer resulting in higher motivation and more people wanting and potentially getting the offer.

experiments.title: How a condensed signup form affects name conversion rate on a landing page

Timeframe: 10/20/2017 - 11/27/2017
FamilyLife wanted to increase name conversion rate on their free online course, I Still Do. They hypothesized that one element of friction on the page was the length of the acquisition form, so they wanted to test a condensed version of the form. Other elements of the page were the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The condensed form increased conversion rate by 3% and average gift by 2%. The removal of friction in the process motivated more people to follow through and get signed up for the course.

experiments.title: How emotive landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive landing page copy increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2017 - 07/31/2017
Hillsdale College previously found that using an emotive headline on their American Heritage course signup page increased email acquisition by 168%. This success taught a valuable lesson about landing page headlines: It is better to orient the language around what the course does for the user, rather than a particular belief. They were curious if the same principle applied to landing page copy below the headline. They created a treatment and launched a test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This teaches us that visitors are more responsive with offers that appear to connect better with personal interests and/or beliefs (i.e. relevance).  The copy achieved this by (1) addressed the opposition and (2) directly pulled the viewer to the “good guys” side.
It also illustrates the benefit of focusing the copy on the why rather than the what of the course once the reader has committed to reading further than the headline.

experiments.title: How relevant landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will tying an online course to a current event increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2017 - 08/21/2017
Hillsdale College knew that their course “Western Heritage,” had a great value proposition. They struggled, however, to find a way to adequately describe this course and its importance on their course signup page. They wondered if tying this course material to a current event would increase their course signups. To test, they created a treatment page with landing page copy that described a recent speech given by President Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings:
These results suggest that tying an online course like “Western Heritage,” to recent events helps visitors more clearly connect with the importance and relevancy of the story of the West.
This give us an important takeaway. When we build online courses, we expect our course material to stay relevant for a long period of time. This test reminds us that it is important to keep refreshing the “entry point” of the course for new visitors with relevant hooks/angles.

experiments.title: How increasing landing page congruence affects cost per subscriber
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis was that by increasing congruence (or visual consistency) between the landing page and the ad, we could improve landing page conversion and lower the cost per subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2014 - 12/25/2014
The Hoover Institution wanted to add new subscribers to its highly-regarded military history and foreign policy newsletter Strategika. Facebook ad targeting allowed them to find new subscribers by targeting these specific interests, but after a few weeks of the campaign, cost-per-subscriber was still higher than original projections.
The landing page did a good job selling Strategika — it constructed a strong value proposition, showed plenty of credibility, and made a clear call-to-action that highlighted all of the benefits of subscription. But we hypothesized we could increase the conversion rate on the landing page by making a single simple change — making the landing page look more like the ad.
The ad had a historic picture of American soldiers storming a beachhead in World War 2. We changed the landing page to have this same background, and shortened the copy to fit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did this new page with increased congruence convert 40% more people to subscribers, because Facebook was optimizing for converisons,  it lowered the cost per subscriber by 86% — a huge savings that allowed Hoover Institution to effectively acquire nearly twice as many subscribers with their original budget.

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action to the top of a simplified landing page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding the button back in to the top of the page increase acquisition on the simplified page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2017 - 11/30/2017
Focus on the Family offers a Marriage Assessment on their website. In a previous test on the assessment landing page, we learned that a simplified page where we removed elements of friction actually decreased conversion because we removed a button at the top of the page that would take this highly motivated audience directly to the assessment. To build upon those learnings, we hypothesized that we could increase conversion by keeping and testing the simplified version again but simply just adding back in the button to the assessment at the top of the page. We split the traffic and tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By adding the button back in to the top of the page, we still weren’t able to increase the traffic going to the assessment. We saw a slight decrease in conversion – not as significant as when the button was completely removed – but still a dip.
We hypothesize that this audience is so highly motivated that they don’t actually need this assessment landing page. We propose testing removing this in-between page altogether and sending people straight to the assessment to get more people to take and complete it.

experiments.title: How congruence between ad and landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing congruence affect course signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their second course, Genesis, on Facebook. They wanted to emphasize the academic nature of the course, so they launched ads that showed a person taking the course while studying. But they hypothesized that they might be able to increase conversion by employing the principle known as congruency: aligning each element of a marketing funnel to increase response.
They pulled the image of the Sistine Chapel from the landing page and put it into the ad, and launched an A/B test to see if they were able to affect conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new ad that used the same imagery as the landing page increased conversion by 47.6%. This shows that congruence is a powerful, if often subliminal tool. While the ad image might not be something that the visitor actively thinks about, the repetition of the imagery throughout the funnel reminds them subconsciously that the elements are all linked.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of elements creates the greatest sense of urgency that allows for the increase in conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/24/2014 - 12/28/2014
CaringBridge was in the final week of their 2014 year-end fundraising campaign and expected traffic to dramatically increase to their donation pages. They wanted to maximize on-page conversion by adding elements to the page to increase urgency: reminding people of the proximity to December 31st.
We knew that adding elements like a countdown clock and a thermometer would increase urgency, but we tested to see which element (or combination of elements) would maximize landing page conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask which, in turn, lead to the increase in conversion rate. However, when combined with the thermometer element of the campaign, it negatively affected the conversion rate. This may be due to the large goal seeming out of reach for the average donor. Whatever the case, for large campaigns like this, the countdown clock will be deployed in the future to increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a radical landing page redesign affects name acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical landing page redesign increase name conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2015 - 07/17/2015
Stanford’s Graduate School of Business was promoting a free ebook to build the list for their email publication Insights by Stanford Business. They had launched with a three-column landing page designed to educate the visitor about the book by putting as much information as possible above the fold. While this campaign delivered cost-effective email acquisition, they thought that a page redesign could improve conversion and lower cost-per-name.
They devised a treatment page that “stacked” the information, starting with a headline that advertised what the visitor receives (“Get the free ebook”) rather than what the visitor wants to do (“Build a winning business”). The copy was also reduced dramatically and sharpened to intensify the force of the value proposition. Finally, the form was brought inline with the eye path and the featured contributors were brought up from below to bolster credibility.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment delivered a 10% lift in conversion — which gave us great insight into the construction of the page. Next, we will focus on copy to see if we can further build the value proposition.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.
Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn’t stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: Does a shortened landing page with added credibility language improve sign-up rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping in credibility language for new names on the “Taboo Questions” landing page will increase sign-up rates by prioritizing copy that addresses the hypothesis (users aren’t sure who Time of Grace or Pastor Mike Novotny is) without simply adding to the page’s overall length.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2026 - 03/18/2026
Time of Grace landing page, “Taboo Questions With Pastor Mike” has struggled with a low sign-up rate of 10% – 14%. With ad targeting suppressing known audience and only being directed to new names, we hypothesized that credibility may be a point of friction. “Who is Time of Grace?” “Why should I care what Pastor Mike thinks about these topics”? An initial test that simply added this credibility language resulted in an even greater decrease. However, we hypothesized that it was not the credibility language itself. Rather, this latest iteration added in credibility language while also shortening the length of the page. This way, the treatment wasn’t simply giving the user a longer page to read, but was swapping existing copy for copy that addressed our hypothesis—keeping the overall length similar, or if anything a little bit shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Adding credibility language about Time of Grace and Pastor Mike Novotny led to a significant increase in email signups for all traffic on the "Taboo Questions" landing page. In contrast, this same language "failed" in a previous experiment, providing critical insight into test execution and Time of Grace's audience. This iteration shows the language addresses a friction point that users encountered on the landing page. Its success depended on being swapped in, not just added to and lengthened. Simply increasing its length previously had a negative impact. This demonstrates that an asset cannot "have it all" or use every tool at once. Instead, it must prioritize what donors need most.

experiments.title: How creating clarity with bullet points on the landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reinforcing the article featured in the Facebook ad on the corresponding landing page for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates because clear and consistent messaging will give users greater confidence in what they’re signing up for, creating a smoother cognitive experience and reducing uncertainty.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2025 - 07/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal was to increase email acquisition for their Spring Digest 2025, a curated digital collection of thought-provoking articles and commentary.
The experiment focused on the landing page experience, specifically how well it reinforced the messaging from the Facebook ads driving traffic to the offer.
The control version of the landing page featured a set of general article highlights from the digest.
In contrast, the treatment version aligned the landing page content with the specific article referenced in the Facebook ad, including a featured political cartoon and title.
The premise for this test was rooted in message match—the idea that maintaining visual and verbal continuity from ad to landing page could increase trust, reduce friction, and improve conversions. This approach leverages psychological principles such as the Consistency Bias (people prefer information that aligns with what they’ve already seen) and Fluency Heuristics (easier-to-process content tends to be more persuasive).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Message Match Isn’t Always the Winner - While the treatment offered tighter alignment between the ad and landing page content, the control outperformed it in both email acquisition rate and donation volume. This suggests that too narrow a focus on a single article may inadvertently limit perceived value, especially if the highlighted article doesn’t resonate universally. Broad Appeal May Drive Higher Engagement - The control’s use of diverse article highlights may have created a stronger sense of breadth and value within the Spring Digest, increasing the appeal to a wider audience. Users may have felt they were signing up for a more comprehensive and enriching offer.High Average Gift in Treatment Signals Donor Intent - Though the treatment underperformed in email acquisition, the average gift amount was nearly 3x higher. This could suggest that users who did convert in the treatment were more primed or aligned with the specific content, possibly indicating higher intent or alignment with Hoover’s donor profile. However, since there was only 1 gift to treatment and 3 gifts to control this is not statistically significant. 4. Clarity vs. Curiosity - This experiment raises an interesting tension: should a landing page lead with clarity (specificity) or curiosity (variety)? The data implies that curiosity-based framing (control) can boost signups, while clarity-based framing (treatment) may filter for higher-value leads.
Future Testing
•	Segmentation Testing: Consider targeting high-intent users with treatment-style pages and broader audiences with control-style pages.
•	Message Framing Tests: Explore emotional vs. intellectual framing of article titles—does the tone of the content preview affect conversion?

experiments.title: Should asks for the content pages be premium focused?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having the ask on the content pages be similar to the homepage for the Spring Share campaign will achieve more transactions because there will be continuity between the asks on the site, which can lead to more trust and credibility.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2025
Moody Radio is running a Spring Share campaign that drives a ton of traffic to their website from on-air, paid traffic, and social.
Earlier in the campaign, we tested how an ask on the content pages should look with a premium.
For this test, we wanted to see if presenting the ask with the premium being the focus is better at converting donors, or if presenting the ask similar to how we are on the homepage would be better.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that presenting the ask on content pages differently from the homepage for the Spring Share campaign resulted in a 20.6% decrease in donations. Although the results did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95%, the trend indicates that the control (current homepage format) performed better than the treatment (focus on premium).

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the potential impact of changing the format of the ask on different parts of the website. It is clear that continuity and consistency in messaging across different pages can play a crucial role in building trust and credibility with donors. Therefore, it may be beneficial to maintain a similar approach to the ask throughout the website to ensure a unified donor experience.

Further testing is recommended to validate these findings and to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions on content pages. Adjusting the format of the ask, testing different premiums, or even considering the timing of the ask in the donor journey could all be valuable areas for future experimentation to optimize donation conversions for the Spring Share campaign on Moody Radio's website.

experiments.title: Does adding a video slide-out to a landing page increase instant donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by adding a small slide-out video to the landing page—mirroring the ad experience that likely brought users there—we could increase donor conversion rates by creating a more consistent, engaging, and compelling user journey.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/24/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The goal was to assess whether a video slide-out on the landing page of a popular content offer would improve donor conversion rates. The feature mimicked the ad experience and was only shown to desktop users.

experiments.title: How should we lay out a landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying a radical redesign to the landing page for ebook offers will achieve more email conversations because the radical redesign will remove friction and increase clarity of the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2025
For all moody ministries, we’ve been wondering if we’re presenting the landing page in the best way to optimize for email captures. After brainstorming, we decided that we wanted to try a radical redesign test to get rid of the footer, move the logo to the middle of the page, shorten the copy, and add an image of the ebook. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the radical redesign of the landing page for ebook offers resulted in a 12.8% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. This indicates that the changes made in the treatment version may have had a positive impact on attracting more email conversions.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate these findings. By continuing to test different variations of the landing page, we can gather more data to determine if the radical redesign consistently leads to higher email conversions. Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand why the treatment version performed better and make informed decisions for future optimizations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that simplifying the landing page and focusing on key elements such as the ebook image can potentially enhance the user experience and encourage more email sign-ups. This information can guide future strategies for improving email capture rates on landing pages for Moody Radio ministries.

experiments.title: How a change in layout impacts engagement on a donation landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic will achieve a higher engagement with the site because the items are easier to view with less scrolling.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2025 - 02/25/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go when they click the main donation button the PTV website.
We decided to test a grid layout with the hypothesis that the grid layout presents multiple viewing opportunities with less scrolling
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the layout to a grid for desktop web traffic resulted in a 19.7% decrease in donations for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence was at 92.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact of the grid layout on engagement with the site.

In addition, we saw:
11% decrease in time spent on the site for treatment
 	an increase from 1 to 6 Pathway Partner (recurring gifts) between control and treatment

experiments.title: How shortening the length of landing page copy impacted email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a shorter landing page, with less content and a more prominent form placement, will lead to an increase in email sign-ups by reducing friction and making it easier for visitors to find and complete the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary created an ebook on the book of Romans to engage their audience and drive sign-ups. The team wanted to test whether a shorter content offer landing page would result in more sign-ups by reducing friction for visitors, particularly by eliminating the need for them to scroll down to find the sign-up form.
The Control landing page featured a longer length, with detailed content above the fold and additional sections below the form. The Treatment landing page was shortened, focusing more succinctly on the value proposition and the sign-up form without as much content above and below.
Our hypothesis was that reducing the length of the page would reduce friction and increase sign-up conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test, with 50% of traffic seeing the longer Control page, and the other 50% seeing the shorter Treatment page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test demonstrated that a shorter landing page led to a 37.1% increase in email sign-ups, though the statistical confidence of 92.2% means the result is promising but not fully validated. Several factors may explain this outcome:
Reduced Cognitive Load & Friction: By simplifying the landing page and minimizing content above and below the form, we reduced the need for visitors to process excessive information, leading them to more easily take the desired action.
