 	Clearer Path to Action: With less content to distract from the call to action, the sign-up form became the clear focus of the page, potentially prompting more visitors to complete the form.
 	User Behavior: Shorter pages can align with the behavior of web visitors who may be less patient or more likely to convert when they feel they are not overwhelmed with excessive information.
further testing with other variations of page length and content may lead to even higher conversion rates

experiments.title: How sending “View Full Post” traffic to the single-post page impacts tribute giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending “View Full Post” traffic to a single-post page will help improve the user-experience while also increasing traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask—increasing tribute giving and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2025 - 02/17/2025
In the current experience, longer posts from an author are shortened with a hyperlink “Show More” which then expands the full post. From a UI perspective, there are limitations to this experience. In mobile especially (where a majority of CB’s traffic originates from) the full post expanding in feed can overwhelm and disorient—requiring the user to scroll all the way back up or down to resume their browsing of the feed.
We hypothesize that by changing this hyperlinked copy to “View Full Post” and instead sending the traffic to the designated Single-Post Page will eliminate this friction, while also increasing Tribute giving. The single-post page contains a tribute widget (16003) that typically converts at a high volume. We believe this could accomplish both goals at once—making the experience more user-friendly while also sending more traffic to the 16003 Tribute ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To understand the key learnings from this experiment we must first differentiate two tracked metrics, that appear very similar at first glance. The first is Tribute Donations/Revenue (From Widget). This metric tracks tribute gifts only on the single-post page—with the 16003 code. This was important to analyze because ultimately it was a large part of our hypothesis: sending more traffic to single-post page will increase gifts to the widget on this page.

However, there is a more encompassing Tribute Gift metric that was measured in this test as well. Because users can technically make a tribute gift on an author's page to various different widgets as well as the donate button in navigation—tracking this metric was equally important. It would answer a key question: are we simply directing traffic to give on a different widget, or does this lead to an overall lift in gifts and revenue as well.

With this in mind, here are some key callouts from this experiment.
The click rate was about the same between "Show More" and "View Full Post." The changed copy did not necessarily mean users were more likely to expand the post.
 	There was a significant 1376.9% increase in donations to Tribute (Widget/16003) in the treatment with a 100% level of confidence. Confirming part of our hypothesis that by increasing traffic to the single-post page, we would increase donations to the 16003 Tribute Widget.
 	However, pulling back to analyze all Tribute Gifts and Revenue shows that the treatment seems to also result in a decrease overall. This is not statistically valid at an 80% level of confidence, however, the trend did remain consistent throughout the entirety of the test run. This could indicate something very important.
overall

experiments.title: Does adding recency and social proof on a landing page impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the amount of downloads from this month for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it suggests recent ongoing popularity and drives urgency so people will feel like they don’t want to miss out. It also might feel more believable as a smaller number rather than a marketing exaggeration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/05/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on the landing page for the offer, Dueling Populisms, focusing specifically on its effect on email acquisition. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer overall VS the number of downloads this month to determine which messaging could encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Social proof language that highlights recent activity can have a positive impact on email acquisition. The treatment version, which featured the number of downloads for the current month, resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisition compared to the control version.

2. The treatment version created a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making the offer more compelling to potential donors.

3. While the results show promise, further testing is recommended to validate the effectiveness of using recent download numbers as social proof.

How to use what was learned in future experimentation:

1. Continue to test different types of social proof language to see what resonates best with potential donors. This could include not just recent activity, but also other forms of social proof such as testimonials or reviews.

2. Consider incorporating elements of urgency and exclusivity in future messaging to increase engagement and drive action.

3. Use this experiment as a foundation for further optimization of landing pages and donation offers, keeping in mind the potential impact of social proof on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How including relevant testimonials on the offer landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including participant testimonials for Jan 1 Scripture Memory Sign-up landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because sharing a positive experience from others will build more trust and confidence in the value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2024 - 12/30/2024
Each month The Navigators run a Scripture Memory Challenge via text. Before each campaign, we promote the next months challenge via Facebook ads, housefile emails invites, and text invites. This challenges have been very popular among their audience and their staff has had a lot of positive feedback about them. Therefore, we tested adding participant testimonials to the landing page for our December/January 2025 Challenge.
These testimonials were presented like a carousel that rotated through 7 different quotes every couple of seconds.
Our hypothesis was that adding social proof to the landing page in the form of testimonials would lead to more people signing up for this challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including participant testimonials on the landing page for The Navigators' Jan 1 Scripture Memory Challenge did not result in the expected increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment group saw a 4.7% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. While the results were statistically significant at a 92.3% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

This indicates that the addition of participant testimonials may not have had the intended effect of building more trust and confidence in the offer. It is important to note that further testing would be necessary to confirm these results and understand the true impact of participant testimonials on email acquisition.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of participant testimonials in different formats or with different messaging. Additionally, considering other factors that may influence email acquisition, such as the landing page design, call-to-action, or audience targeting, would also be valuable. By continuously testing and analyzing different variables, The Navigators can gain deeper insights into what strategies are most effective in driving email acquisition for their Scripture Memory Challenges.

experiments.title: How asking for a one-time or monthly gift in the email and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page effects monthly donations during Giving Tuesday
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday will achieve an increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During Giving Tuesday the Membership team designed an email and landing page test to see whether an ask for a one-time donation or monthly donation vs. an ask for a “best gift” made any difference on transactions or monthly gifts. The treatment which featured the one-time or monthly ask also led to landing page with the monthly tab first.
Here are the results breakdown:
Members: The experiment showed no statistical difference in donor acquisition between asking for a one-time or monthly gift vs. asking for their best gift. There was a 145% lift in monthly transactions with 90% LOC (5 vs. 10 gifts). There was a directional decrease in clicks on the link in the email for the treatment with a 5% decrease with 63% LOC.
Nonmembers: The test showed the treatment caused a statistically valid decrease in donor acquisition of -59% with 97% LOC. There was no added benefit in monthly acquisition, but the treatment did validate a 5% decrease in email clicks with 99.4% LOC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly or one-time donation and leading with a monthly tab on the landing page for members via an email solicitation during Giving Tuesday resulted in a significant increase in monthly gifts with no decrease in overall transactions.

Although the results showed a 145.6% increase in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was below the standard threshold of 95%. This indicates that while the treatment showed promise, further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing this approach with a larger sample size to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it could be useful to explore different variations of the treatment to optimize the ask for monthly donations and potentially further increase donation rates.

Overall, the experiment highlights the potential benefits of tailoring donation asks to members based on their giving preferences and the importance of ongoing testing and optimization to drive success in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How including the benefits a person will receive to a landing page’s call-to-action affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including the benefits a person receives to the call to action on an eBook landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will understand the value they receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running an eBook offer using Facebook ads and prospecting emails to send traffic to a landing page where people could request their free copy of their 2024 Index of U.S. Military Strength eBook. In an attempt to increase email conversion rate on this page, they previously tested adding a curiosity-creating headline, as well as a preview of the content available in the eBook. Both of these treatments, however, resulted in a decrease in email acquisition rate.
As a follow up, they wanted to test if adding the benefits a person receives in the page’s call-to-action (CTA) would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength by filling out the form below!
The treatment’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength to discover the state of the U.S. military and what it means for your future.
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic, and the experiment ran for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the benefits a person receives in the call-to-action on an eBook landing page actually led to a decrease in email conversion rate. The treatment group, which included the benefits in the CTA, saw a 10.4% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 87.3%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid but are approaching validity.

Moving forward, it is important to acknowledge that in this specific case, highlighting the benefits did not have the intended effect of increasing conversion rates. This information should be used to guide future experimentation by considering alternative strategies for optimizing the landing page, such as testing different messaging, design elements, or incentives to encourage email sign-ups. It is also crucial to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective way to drive conversions for the eBook offer.

experiments.title: Will using shorter copy on an content offer landing page increase email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers will achieve greater rates of email acquisition because there is less friction on the page to distract from completing a form..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary has a number of ebook content offers that typically perform well for their audience. We wanted to see if we could increase the email acquisition rate on these landing pages by using more focused, concise copy compared to the standard longer control copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
The use of shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers resulted in a 37.1% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. The statistical level of confidence was 92.2%, which indicates a high likelihood that the increase in email acquisition was due to the change in copy length. While the results are encouraging, further testing would be necessary to ensure the validity and reliability of the findings.
 	Further analysis of the data shows a concerning drop in the average gift and overall revenue for this offer. While these metrics did not reach a statistical level of confidence, further testing should be mindful of the potential trade-off of acquiring more subscribers who are potentially less valuable to the organization.

experiments.title: How adding visual clarity to a marriage video series landing page image impacted email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more visual clarity to the imagery on a video series landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisition because visitors will understand what’s being offered and why they should want it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/15/2024 - 05/16/2024
This organization uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. Their video series landing pages include a “screenshot” image from one of the series videos to showcase the guest speaker for the series.
We have a theory that some of the visitors coming to the landing page may not be familiar with what a video series is or how they would access it, so we decided to test whether we could use the imagery on the page to add some visual clarity.
We designed this as a 3-way test with the following variants:
Control – No change, uses the plain image of the speaker
“Devices” Treatment – Shows the video series on a phone and laptop to show how it can be watched
“Devices & Value Flag” Treatment – Shows the video series on a phone and laptop, and also adds a value impact statement above it
Our hypothesis was that the treatment images would drive higher email acquisition rates because visitors would understand what the video series offer is and why they should sign up for it.
We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature and conducted the test over 1 month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included adding more visual clarity to the imagery on the video series landing page, resulted in a 2.3% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 93.9%. This indicates that the control, which featured the plain image of the speaker, performed better.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that while the treatment did not perform as well in this instance, there may still be value in exploring ways to improve visual clarity on landing pages. Further testing with different variations of imagery or messaging could help to identify what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other elements of the landing page, such as the copy or call-to-action, to optimize for email acquisition.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment should be used to inform future testing strategies and to continue to refine the approach to optimizing video series landing pages for email acquisition.

experiments.title: Will asking a question in the header of a landing page create a better hook?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting a landing page off with a question for an acquistion offer will achieve a higher conversion rate because the question on the page will lead the user to stay on the page longer and continue to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2024 - 07/09/2024
For this test we wanted to see if by changing the headline of a landing page to be a question if we could increase conversion rate on the page.
We decided to test this approach on an older offer that gets a lot of traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that starting a landing page with a question for an acquisition offer may not always lead to a higher conversion rate. In this particular case, the treatment with the question actually resulted in a decrease in transactions for all traffic.
It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level. This means that further testing is needed to determine if starting a landing page with a question is truly effective in increasing conversion rates.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different approaches to see what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, it is important to consider factors such as messaging, design, and user experience when creating and testing landing pages to maximize conversion rates.

experiments.title: How pinning a widget above a post on a landing page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pinning the tribute widget above a new author post for JEN traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it increases visibility of the widget, but this reordering also better highlights the importance of the widget as a goal action for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2024 - 08/13/2024
When CaringBridge user makes a new post, their followers receive an email alert that takes them to directly the new journal entry on their profile. These users receive a Tribute Widget with an ask for a donation, however, this is pinned to be at the very bottom of the posts and any comments on the post in the control. If a particular post has many comments, this results in the Tribute Widget not being seen at all. We hypothesized that by pinning this tribute widget to be above the new post will resolve this potential point of friction and increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pinning the tribute widget above a new author post for JEN traffic resulted in a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 24.3% increase in Donations - Tribute (from Widget) for All Traffic with a 91.2% statistical level of confidence.

The treatment also resulted in a directional increase in revenue as well, with an 18% lift and an 85% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the placement of the tribute widget and other elements on the platform to optimize donor conversion rates. It may also be valuable to explore additional factors, such as the timing of the donation ask in relation to the user's engagement with the content, to further improve the effectiveness of fundraising efforts on the platform. Additionally, considering the potential impact on user experience and engagement is crucial when making changes to the platform layout. By continuing to test and iterate based on data-driven insights, Caringbridge can better understand user behavior and preferences, ultimately leading to more successful fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: What happens when you shorten copy on the landing page of a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the People of Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in conversions because there is less friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 03/31/2024
Moody Bible Institute is a nonprofit that has multiple sectors that roll up underneath their main MBI umbrella. One of their ministries is Today in the Word, this ministries only focus is to provide daily devotionals to their audiences through magazines, email, or on the app.
We wanted to see if we could increase their email file by marketing some of the bible studies for acquisition in Facebook. The original signup rate was lower than expected. We wanted to the impact that the change in copy length would have on the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Shortening the copy for the People of Prayer landing page did not result in the intended increase in conversions; instead, there was a 14.5% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. Inadvertently when shortening the copy, we could have reduced the value proposition on the page. Further testing will need to be done on the area to determine a good baseline.

experiments.title: How using priming language on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment page using “priming” will increase the email sign-up rate for the video series because it primes visitors to associate positive emotions and a sense of warmth with ‘The Inklings’ and the video series, making them more inclined to take action by signing up for access.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 01/26/2024
Hillsdale College is offering a free video series on “The Inklings” through paid Facebook advertising. They wanted to test how using the psychological concept “priming” would affect email conversion on the page to sign up to get the video series. On the treatment page, the priming language helps associate the video series with positive emotions and a sense of warmth like the Inklings experienced when they were discussing literature and theology.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment page using "priming" actually resulted in a decrease in email sign-up rate for the video series. This means that the hypothesis that priming visitors with positive emotions and a sense of warmth would increase their likelihood of signing up for access was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of priming on the desired outcome. While priming can be a powerful psychological tool, it is not always effective in achieving the desired results. This experiment suggests that in this particular case, priming may have had a negative effect on the decision to sign up for the video series.
Therefore, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies and messaging techniques that could potentially increase email acquisition. This could involve testing different types of language, imagery, or incentives to better appeal to the target audience. Additionally, conducting further research to gain a deeper understanding of the target audience's motivations and preferences could also inform future experimentation and optimize the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout Impacts Sign-Up Rate on Offer Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the How to Memorize Scripture landing page will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because it removes anxiety for those who might wonder if their information is secure or why The Navigators needs their email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2023 - 06/10/2023
The Navigators’ landing page for How to Memorize Scripture was experiencing a slowing in sign-up rate. We hypothesized that adding a security callout at the bottom of the form will increase sign-up rate. This test was conducted on another offer, and was expanded to How to Memorize Scripture to see the result of more testing.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment led to a 12% decrease in sign-up rate with a 94% level of confidence. Although, this is technically just under the accepted threshold for validity (95%) this in conjunction with similar results from the same test on a different offer does  indicate that perhaps this learning should be observed for Navigators' audience.
Why would a callout for security cause less people to sign-up? Perhaps in an attempt to limit anxiety around giving up an email, the callout actually introduced it. If their security hadn't been a consideration before, and the test then bring attention to it — it may have led users to overthink about "Do I really want to give my email? Is this safe?"
The takeaway here is that perhaps security wasn't a big concern for Navigators' audience. An effort to improve the sign-up rate on the page, should move on to testing other factors that might be inhibiting conversion.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a security callout at bottom of email acquisition form for users on the How to Save a World landing page will achieve an increase in acquisition rate because it relieves the stress and anxiety around how their email will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/03/2023
For Navigators’ How to Save the World landing page, we hypothesized that a simple security callout beneath the form would increase email acquisition rate. This short line of copy reinstates to the user that their information is secure and that they are only providing their email in order to receive the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a security callout at the bottom of an email acquisition form on a landing page may not have a positive impact on the email acquisition rate. The treatment group resulted in a 12.9% decrease in email acquired for all traffic with an 88.3% level of confidence, indicating that the control group was more effective. In addition, desktop visitors had a significant 730% decrease in email acquisition, suggesting that the callout may have had an adverse effect on this device.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct further testing to confirm whether the security callout is effective or not. If it does not work, other possible strategies could include using social proof or highlighting the benefits of subscribing to the newsletter. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the impact of the callout on different devices and user segments to ensure that it is not hurting the acquisition rate. Overall, ongoing experimentation and analysis are vital for improving the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a landing page impacts site creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimony for prospective site creators will achieve an increase in site creation conversion rate because social proof reduces anxiety and adds credibility to the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/11/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added social proof in the form of a testimonial on a landing page.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment omitted any kind of testimonial whereas the treatment included a real-life quote from someone who has benefited from created a site. Our hypothesis that adding social proof will reduce any kind of anxiety someone may feel when creating a site with CaringBridge. This quote adds credibility to those who may be unfamiliar with CaringBridge.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a testimonial to a confirmation page may not have the intended effect of reducing anxiety and adding credibility to an organization. The treatment resulted in a 51.0% directional decrease in website creation compared to the control, but the statistical level of confidence was only 86.6%. This means that the results are not considered valid but are approaching validity.
These results are contrary to what was expected. It's probable that the selected testimonial may not be relevant enough to the subject matter of the ebook for this funnel. It's recommended that this is retested in a different ebook funnel to draw further conclusions. Additionally, CaringBridge could anchor the benefits of creating a site by using language like "Join over XXX,XXX families who have benefited from using CaringBridge". This reference point suggests that there are many others who have used CaringBridge successfully, which may add credibility and increase the perceived value of using the platform.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials in a sidebar impacted email acquisitions on a free eBook offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility via book reviews for a free eBook offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 08/05/2022
We wanted to find out if increasing the appeal by adding social proof through credibility and evidentials would improve email acquisition rates on a free eBook offer. We took testimonial quotes from another author and from one of the organization’s trusted pastors and council members and featured them on the landing page to further reinforce to landing page visitors that this was a valuable book offer that was available for free. We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed as a 2.2% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence. The results were pretty much flat but overall we saw a slight decrease in the percentage of total emails acquired across all traffic for those who experienced the treatment version of the landing page. Our hypothesis is that visitors could have been unfamiliar with those who were quoted in the testimonials. Because the recommendations were coming from another author and from a pastor rather than a layperson, it could have been assumed that this offer was only suitable for those in positions of ministry or Christian writers—rather than a person in the general workforce or average home. This could have led some people to think the offer was not intended for them and so they did not take the next step. Some changes in visitor segments in the experiment that were interesting to note were that while direct visitors had a 16.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence, email visitors had a 14.0% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. It's possible that email visitors could have been more receptive to the treatment since they are likely to be more familiar with the style and nature of TGC book reviews if they have been receiving regular communications via email from the organization.

experiments.title: The impact on email acquisition of a content offer image placed above the fold for mobile visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the eBook image above-the-fold will increase email acquisition for mobile visitors to the landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 06/17/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life is one of Buckner’s most important online premium offers on Facebook. It has the highest number of emails acquired, yet its landing page has one of the lowest conversion rates.
When viewing the landing page on mobile devices, the image appeared all the way at the bottom of the page, below the form.
We’ve seen in testing with similar organizations that placing an image above-the-fold for mobile visitors can increase email acquisition. Since the majority of traffic to that page is mobile, we wanted to see if making the eBook image more prominent would likewise increase email for Buckner’s traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: During the experiment, we were not able to validate the test due to lower traffic to the page. However, since we came close to validation (with between a 75% and 84% level of confidence) and didn't see any negative impacts from the placement, we rolled out the top of fold image for all traffic to the page.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an ebook instant donation page impacts conversion rates and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for ebook downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/08/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive a ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding this offer to the instant donation page for the Spring Digest ebook would impact donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Providing a subscription for all 4 of the physical versions of the Hoover Digest as an enticement to increase ones' gift amount had a mixed effect. While the donor conversion rate not impacted at all (only a 0.9% difference), the average gift increased by 37%, with a 85% level of confidence. This shows that people are interested in giving more to receive the physical versions of the Digest.
Since this is a quarterly publication, we will continue testing this, as well as testing to see whether the same premium offer will work across other ebook offers for Hoover.

experiments.title: Does creating more clarity in the text of a landing page affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared passive and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
Traffic to this free online course primarily came through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a directional 16% decrease in email acquisition (level of confidence 93%). We had theorized that the extra detail regarding the course were a point of friction for landing page visitors but the data seems to indicate that RTB's audience is actually looking for these details to make their decision.
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.
Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: The Impact of Aligning a Landing Page’s headline with the CTA and adding Social Proof to the page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by aligning a landing page’s headline with the CTA and adding social proof, we will improve email acquisition conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/22/2022
Focus on the Family uses free video series offers on Facebook to acquire new email addresses and donors. In a prior test, they found that adding social proof language to a landing page improved email signups by 4% with an LOC of 89%. They decided to test this approach on their Praying the Scripture Over Your Children video series offer to see if it would improve conversion. For the treatment, they added “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!” under the image of the offer.
While preparing for the test, they also decided to align the headline of the page with the CTA. So, they changed the headline from Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids to Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids With This Free Video Series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Even though the addition of social proof improved conversion on their other offer, for this video series, it did not. That said, we don't know if the result would have been different had this test not included the additional copy on the headline. Because we changed two elements of the page at the same time, it's impossible to know if both elements decreased conversion or if they worked to cancel each other out.
For future tests we should test one element at a time to ensure a clean result. Also, even in the prior test when the social proof increased conversion, it only had a 4.3% impact on conversion. We will prioritize future tests on Focus offers that are likely to result in more significant impacts.

experiments.title: How adding social proof impacted email acquisition on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2021 - 02/10/2022
Focus on the Family reached a milestone of over 1 million sign-ups for their free video series, and we wanted to test our hypothesis that highlighting this as social proof on the landing page would increase email sign-ups on the landing page. We added a call out underneath the image to “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a directional lift of 4% on the treatment page with an 89.9% level of confidence. Highlighting the number of people who have accessed the free video series seemed to compel landing page visitors to move forward with completing the registration form. This treatment specifically worked well for mobile visitors and email visitors. Based on these promising results, we will look for additional opportunities to test adding social proof elements like this one on future offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.1% decrease in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 5.2% increase in emails acquired with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 10.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 98% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 19.0% increase in emails acquired with a 91% level of confidence.
 	Male visitors had a 37.0% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 40.0% increase in emails acquired with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 91.7% increase in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 54.5% decrease in emails acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Will offering a free membership trial leads to more membership activations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free 1-month trial for landing page visitors will achieve more membership activations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2021
As we reconfigured what membership looked like, we were trying to figure out a way to get more people to activate a membership to the NextAfter Institute. So, we thought if we offered folks a free 1-month trial, it would allow them the chance to learn what membership is about and be able to access our resources right away. We ran this experiment on our membership landing page with a 50/50 split in traffic between signing up for the typical $49/month membership vs. the 1-month free trial.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Something important to note is that metric tracking between all systems in this experiment was messy. We did our best to isolate variables, but they are not perfect.

Although the experiment did not validate, results point in the direction that the free trial is most effective in achieving more membership activations. However, something worth considering is if those folks will be retained as well as those who pay for the membership right away—more testing and analysis needs to be done to figure that out.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects email acquisition on a homepage ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition that conveys more urgency and focuses on the revenue at stake for homepage visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2020 - 12/30/2020
In this experiment, we were promoting a new ebook focused on fundraising ideas that can help increase revenue during the last week of the year. We had two main ideas as to how to message the advertisement.
In version A, we used the homepage ad itself to illustrate how fundraisers can use a similar homepage ad to promote their year-end campaign.
In version B, the messaging emphasis focused on the urgency around the biggest giving week of the year being right around the corner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn't quite hit our desired 95% level of confidence, but the result was a strong indicator that it was more relevant to emphasize the urgency around the last week of the year – leading to a 50% increase in conversions. As noted below, it was especially effective for website visitors that already had some affinity towards us and our content.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 77.7% increase in all form submissions with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 213.3% increase in all form submissions with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 66.7% increase in all form submissions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increased urgency using a countdown clock affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock near the call-to-action for landing page visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 01/06/2021
In a previous experiment, we used a sticky bar to try and create a heightened sense of urgency around our ebook outlining strategies to increase giving during the last week of the year. But the sticky bar made no significant difference in conversions.
As a next step, we wondered if using a countdown clock lower on the page ticking down to the last week of the year would be a better means of conveying urgency and lead to more downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock led to a 10.6% increase in conversion, although it didn't quite reach our desired level of confidence. However, it's a strong indicator that a countdown clock is an effective motivator to increase downloads in this context. We'll continue to use countdown clock on acquisition pages when there is a relevant deadline - in particularly around year-end and the last week of the year.

experiments.title: How framing the message around the donor’s impact affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A landing page with donor-centric language will have a higher conversion rate than one that focuses on the accomplishments as a result of Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2014 - 12/17/2014
The standard messaging points employed by Heritage have been refined over many years across multiple channels. At the start of the year end campaign, we decided to test how the information is presented to the donor. The control uses standard messaging while the new treatment would take the same information but frame it around how the accomplishments were the result of the donor, not the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The messaging points of Heritage have not changed but we have learned that we need to frame them around the donor instead of around the organization.

experiments.title: How increasing the size of the text on an offer landing page did not impact the email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will improved readability increase the email registration rate for the Religious Freedom offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 04/15/2019
When The Heritage Foundation launched a new offer, we were not achieving the email registration rate that we would normally see. So, in an attempt to boost the email registration rate, we launched a simple experiment to boost the size of the text across both desktop and mobile versions to see if readability was prohibiting us from achieving the results we wanted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 80.9% level of confidence, we found that the large text actually decreased the email registration rate by -8.3%.
We will now turn our testing towards the message itself, having eliminated something structural with the page as a variable that is limiting our effectiveness.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial affects email sign ups on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will utilizing a review on a landing page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Back to the Bible launched a new eBook 7 Secrets of Worry-Free Living and wanted to test whether including a review from the leader of a Christian men’s conference, Brian Doyle, would increase email sign ups on the landing page. All other landing page elements were identical.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we turned off the experiment before reaching a valid level of confidence, we were seeing an overall decline in conversion rate on the treatment. Future experiments will test using other various forms of social proof such as number of eBook downloads or a testimonial from a peer.

experiments.title: How removing details in an email affects traffic to the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the date and time from the email drive more traffic to the registration page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2017 - 02/15/2017
When promoting a webinar, we typically try to include the date and time in the emails that we send in order to provide as much clarity as possible to our recipients. But we wondered if putting the date and time in the email was actually disqualifying some people right away because they already knew they would be busy. We thought these elements were creating unnecessary friction at this stage in the registration process.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the date and time from the email, we could drive more click-throughs to the registration page. And once someone was on the landing page, we displayed an exit-intent pop-up offering to send a recording even if they couldn’t make the time work with their schedule.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a lift of 15.3% in click-through rate. Unfortunately, our experiment did not quite reach our desired level of confidence of 95%. It came just short at 92.4%.
After analyzing conversion rates, we saw that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment when it came to how many people actually registered.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:

1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.

2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.

3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: How the landing page layout affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which layout will produce the greatest number of donations for the year end campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2012 - 01/01/2013
As part of their year end fundraising campaign, Media Research Center wanted to find the best way to communicate their unique value proposition that would lead to increased giving. The standard donation page layout had always been a two column approach in order to get the donation form closer to the top of the page. We wanted to see if that was the best approach to communicate the case for support to the potential donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: By moving the donation form below the form, we allowed the value proposition communicated within the copy to better “sell” the gift. The single column layout was effectively making an ask before adequately explaining why the gift was needed.  The test did not have a high enough level of confidence to be proven valid so further testing would need to be done to fully validate these results.

experiments.title: How adjusting the value proposition on a landing page affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a revised value proposition on the landing page affect instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2019 - 03/31/2019
DTS was running a value proposition test on their Hebrews landing page, that focused on how the course would benefit the reader rather than focusing on the teacher of the course or the course content itself. We also adjusted the headline of the landing page. The end donation page and following workflow remained the same in both the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had little to no impact on landing page signups, but something surprising happened in this test. The instant donor conversion rate on the following page from people who signed up on the treatment landing page increased by 35% (not statistically valid, but really close). This could be directly related to the fact that we've just told them the value they're going to get out of this course and the impact it will have on their walk with Christ, rather than focusing our value proposition on course content or course teacher.
Moving forward, further testing is necessary on the value proposition of the landing page and how that flows into the donation page content. Continuing to reiterate the eternal impact of the course is key to higher donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding a photo to a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a photo to a landing page increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/28/2019 - 03/13/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. After sending the first two emails out to a split landing page test, where they tested the presence of gift handles that explained what each suggested gift amount would be used for, they concluded that adding just one line of copy around the gift handles did not increase likelihood of giving.
The Hillsdale team wondered about including a photo of some of the students who received Constitutions onto the landing page. Rather than telling the donor about what they were doing, they could show them with a photo. Would adding a photo of schoolchildren receiving the Constitution increase donations? They hypothesized that adding the photo would show proof, causing an increase in trust, and would increase emotional appeal. They hypothesized that each of these would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a 15% lift from the treatment with the photo. Because this experiment only ran for one day, the experiment results shouldn’t be rolled out to the rest of the donation pages. However, this result was enough for the Hillsdale team to roll out to this landing page for the duration of the campaign.

experiments.title: How adding privacy language to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding conversational privacy language to an acquisition page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/09/2019 - 01/31/2019
Buckner International wanted to test the effectiveness of adding privacy language below the form on their acquisition page that would increase trustworthiness and decrease friction for users submitting the form, by reiterating what they would receive from Buckner by giving us their information, as well as how their info would be handled. Copy on the page remained the same, as did the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: The privacy language test did not reach statistical validity, but it showed a slight trend towards a decrease in conversion rate. It's possible that the privacy language had the opposite of the intended affect and increased people's wariness to submit the form.More testing in this area will be necessary to validate learnings.

experiments.title: How the length of copy in an email vs the landing page affects sales lead conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shifting the narrative from the landing page to the email lead to greater sales leads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2018
In this experiment, our goal was to generate sales qualified leads to set up a call related to new mystery donor audit. The control had a brief email explaining the topic, and landing page that went into full detail.
We have seen in the past (particularly with fundraising emails) that emails are often the best place to spend more time explaining a narrative. So for our treatment, we wondered if we could increase conversions by shifting a large portion of the copy from the landing page to the email. The copy is almost identical between the two versions, with a few minor adjustments in the treatment to make the email and landing page flow together naturally.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email and landing page saw a 215% increase in conversion rate at a 93% level of confidence. While we ideally aim for a 95% level of confidence, the impact on sales leads seems apparent and strong enough for us to move forward with treatment decisively.
This further confirms a hypothesis that highly personal emails are more effective at explaining a narrative and story, and that the common “best practice” of writing short email copy to boost clicks is not a reliable way to increase the ultimate goal of conversion.

experiments.title: How simplifying the imagery on a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a simpler background decrease friction on a landing page and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2018 - 10/30/2018
Hoover was running their Dueling Populisms content offer, and wanted to test the effectiveness of a simpler landing page on conversion rate. To test this, they removed the background image on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test did not have any statistically significant impact on conversion, but saw a trend towards a 12% decrease.  More rigorous testing is likely needed to validate learnings on the page.

experiments.title: How adding credibility and clarity to the landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page produces the the greatest conversion rate to customers for their conferences?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2014 - 03/27/2014
The Acquire The Fire site is a WordPress multi-page installation that gave the user plenty of options: get resources, find out more about the ministry, see the latest list of tour dates, and access to the store (which sold curriculum, merchandise, and other non-ticket items). Sometimes, access to all of multiple pages is a good thing — it encourages users to spend more time on the site and learn more about the ministry. However, we hypothesized that this could lead to what we call “unsupervised thinking” — by allowing users to wander all over the site, we give them more opportunities to get distracted from the ultimate goal. We needed to be able to guide them down the “path of righteousness” that leads to conversion.
We wanted to create an experiment that helps determine what motivates their customers in order to increase their overall conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After just under a month of testing, this landing page delivered a 35% lift in visitor conversion! Additionally, the name acquisition offers grew Teen Mania’s email list size by 13%. This page was a resounding success, and taught us several things about Teen Mania customers:
1. Credibility matters. There are a lot of youth evangelism events out there, but few of them have the endorsements, coverage, and credibility that Acquire The Fire has. This needs to be up front and repeated throughout the value proposition.
2. Buying tickets is a big decision. Our customers are generally buying tickets for a large group and want to make a good decision with their budget dollars. The more that we can show them what they will experience and reinforce that with quotes and testimonials from people who have experienced an Acquire The Fire event, the more we can reduce anxiety about the event itself.
3. Not all visitors are ready to buy. We know anecdotally that it can take time for youth pastors to get budget approval, parental approval, or youth signups. Because of these delays, not everyone who comes to this site is able to buy tickets. We need ways to engage these visitors and give them something of value in exchange for their email address (and the opportunity to market to them later).

experiments.title: How reduced email-specific landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will reducing the length of the value proposition on a email-specific landing page increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2017 - 08/16/2017
Hillsdale College discovered that creating channel-specific landing pages allowed them to launch tests and document test results with even greater accuracy. By splitting up the incoming traffic based on channel, they hoped they could match the experience closer to the motivation of the user. They hypothesized that individuals who received and read through an email offer about their online course, “Theology 101” may have a high enough motivation that they didn’t need to read a long value proposition on the landing page they clicked to visit. They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale found that the treatment with the reduced value proposition actually decreased email acquisition by 26.2%. This would indicate that the highly motivated user still needs a little nudging even after clicking through the email offer.
The results of this test almost reached validity. However, since they did not officially reach the 95% confidence threshold, we cannot say with certainty that this reduction in negatively impacted conversion.
Despite falling short of validity, this test does provide a helpful learning. It’s clear that the reduction in copy was nearing significance. For that reason, we have launched additional tests similar to this one on a variety of channel-specific landing pages to track how copy length impacts email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How a testing into a new landing page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation landing page will produce the greatest amount of donors and dollars?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2015 - 06/08/2015
Hillsdale College had recently launched a new microsite to handle all of their online giving. The new microsite had a variation of their standard design but made it easier to create contextualized donation forms and took into account certain principles we learned from previous experiments such as removing banners and navigation, adding value proposition language, and an easier to use donation form.
One of their more traffic donation forms was a general donation form for their online classes.  Each class has its own donation form but, to handle general website traffic, we wanted one that could be applicable to all courses.  Before making the switch to the new microsite, we wanted to validate that the new page would achieve the lift in conversion rate and revenue that we were expecting.
experiments.keyLearnings: The learnings achieved through previous experiments held true with this new landing page and help achieve a 35.8% increase in donation conversion rate. The experiment was a good example of leveraging the learnings from multiple experiments to craft a new, more effective landing page.

experiments.title: How changing copy on a button for a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing button copy to be clearer for potential monthly donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 04/29/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to clearly communicate the action and improve conversion rates. The results showed that the CTA change improved user clarity and increased conversion rates. The results showed whether this change improved conversion rates, though specific outcome data is not provided here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It is important to note that the donation sample size (17 total) was very small as we were aiming for monthly donors. This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to set appropriate expectations for the user.

Key Results:

-       +76% non-statistically significant lift (75%) in overall conversion rate.

-       +99% non-statistically significant lift (89%) in overall RPV.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have changed the CTA copy on the landing page to DONATE TODAY.

 

Desktop performance:

Control: 2.3% conversion rate and $0.76 RPV.

Treatment: 3.9% conversion rate, which is a 69% increase with a 44% level of confidence. RPV increased by 29% with a 60% level of confidence.

 

Mobile performance:

Control: 1.5% conversion rate and $0.28 RPV.

Treatment: 2.8% conversion rate, which was an 81% increase with a 69% level of confidence. RPV also increased by 167% with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to be the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept, would lead to potential confusion and friction about the message. Therefore, leading the users to the non-tribute page that had more missional language, would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2026 - 01/27/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and was tested on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the donate button links to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors led to a 7.8% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donor conversion rate, potentially reducing confusion and friction for users.
In future experimentation, it is important to note that while the results of this experiment suggest a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended 95%. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution and not used for making definitive decisions.
However, the insights gained from this experiment can guide future testing strategies. It is worth exploring further variations in the donation button links to see if similar positive results can be achieved with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, considering the significant increases in both donations and revenue from the non-tribute donation page, incorporating more missional language and context into donation pages could be a valuable strategy to enhance donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: Does Identity-Based Messaging Outperform Recipient-Based Messaging on a Campaign Landing Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing appeal through the lens of the donor’s identity for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because identity-based messaging increases personal relevance and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/29/2025
This test ran on Bible League Canada’s Transforming India landing page, which aimed to send Bibles to waiting believers. We wanted to explore whether donor identity-based messaging could outperform the standard recipient-based messaging.
The control version emphasized the hardship and urgency experienced by Christians in India, featuring a clear call to action. The treatment led with a direct appeal to the donor’s faith and identity. The narrative remained the same, but the message positioned the donor as an active participant in Bible League’s mission.
This A/B test ran for the month of October and was split 50/50 amongst website traffic. Visitors came from a mix of meta ads, email appeals, and organic website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

This test did not produce a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. Both versions generated nearly identical numbers of donations, with a 0.8% relative decrease in revenue per visitor. The average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, but still not enough to come to a strong conclusion. The biggest difference was the decrease in recurring gifts (5 vs 1), but this wasn't at a statistically-significant level of confidence.

Framing the landing page message around donor identity did not outperform a more traditional, recipient-focused messaging. This may suggest that:
There was not a clear enough difference between the control and treatment.
 	The visitors didn't have much of a preference between the two versions.
 	The donors' faith identity was already assumed through earlier steps in the journey
Implications for Future Experimentation:
Testing the identity-based language earlier in the donor journey
 	Combining identity with stronger urgency or social proof to create a more layered motivation
 	Identity framing language in a colder traffic segment where shared beliefs aren't assumed

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Moody Radio acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 08/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Moody Radio’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which simplified the opt-in copy and removed the form frame for Moody Radio's acquisition landing page, did not lead to an increase in email opt-ins compared to the control. The treatment actually resulted in a 5.0% decrease in Opt-in checkbox clicked for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 60.2%. Therefore, the hypothesis that reducing friction and decision fatigue would streamline the process for users and increase opt-ins was not supported by the data.

On the positive side, the experiment did show a significant increase in Revenue by 216% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while the simplified messaging did not lead to more opt-ins, it may have had a positive impact on revenue generation.

Moving forward, the insights gained from this experiment suggest that when making changes to acquisition landing pages, it is essential to carefully consider the potential impact on user behavior and to thoroughly test any hypothesis before implementing changes. Additionally, when interpreting results, it is important to focus on statistical significance levels of at least 95% to ensure the validity of the findings. Finally, while an increase in revenue is positive, it should be balanced with other key metrics such as opt-in rates to fully evaluate the effectiveness of a new approach.

experiments.title: How changing the placement of an eBook a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because By presenting the offer right away, we reduce friction and maximize clarity, especially for users in scanning mode.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Dueling Populisms”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Further Results:

Transactions:
Control: 38 donations
 	Treatment: 37 donations
 	A 2.63% decrease
Control: $1,434
 	Treatment: $1,399
 	A 2.44% decrease
The treatment showed a modest but meaningful increase (+4.4%) in email acquisition by moving the ebook offer to the top of the page. However, this result was not statistically significant. More testing needs to be done to confirm that above-the-fold visibility enhances user engagement for lead-generation offers, particularly when screen space is limited.
While email acquisition increased slightly, there was no significant difference in downstream revenue or transactions. This suggests that while placement can improve micro-conversions (like lead capture), it doesn’t automatically translate into macro-conversions (like donations or purchases). Additional nurturing may be needed.
This experiment reinforces that even small changes to content hierarchy — without altering the offer itself — can lead to improved performance. This is especially important for high-traffic, high-value landing pages like this one.
Test the size or prominence of the CTA button
 	Try shorter copy on just the mobile audience
 	Trigger a popup if the user has gone idle for a bit... to see if you can remind them to signup.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page redesign impact email signups for a video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
This organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition for the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.
 	The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the control and treatment pages.
 	Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: How the placement of an ebook image on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive. because  eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2025 - 06/30/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
