We created a treatment version of their Cherish your Spouse video series landing page where the bullet points stated what subscribers would now be able to do, because of these short videos. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 2 weeks. We believe that this new language would increase the perceived value of the videos, leading to more signups on the page and a higher downstream donor conversion on the subsequent donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After three weeks, we were unable to show any statistically significant impact, positive or negative, from the treatment. Revenue showed the biggest impact, but it too wasn't statistically valid. After testing impact bullet points on three offers, we had one with a clear increase in donor conversion, one with a clear decrease, and this experiment with no significant impact. This shows that the success of emphasizing the value/impact of an offer vs emphasizing the content of the offer depends on the impact that is emphasized. For this offer, emphasizing that the videos will enable you to "feel the closeness that comes from cherishing each other and experience greater marital intimacy" weren't compelling enough impacts to significantly impact conversion.

More testing is needed here offer by offer to identify the best value impacts to emphasize in the landing page copy.

experiments.title: How a value-driven headline on an acquisition offer landing page impacts email conversion, donor conversion, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating reader-focused value proposition language for visitors to the Cherish video series landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life. because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/10/2023
Focus on the Family is a Christian ministry that serves families worldwide. Every year, hundreds of thousands of people sign up for their free video series’ to learn more about strengthening marriages, families, and faith.
One of their most effective series is Cherish Your Spouse. In reviewing the landing page for this offer, we felt that the headline lacked a strong value for the visitor. We hypothesized that if we changed the headline to clarify the impact of the series on subscribers, it would increase the appeal of the offer and signups on the page, ultimately leading to more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for three weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant impact on email signups from the new headline. Downstream donor conversion actually trended down for the treatment page but we were not able to validate that decrease either. Future tests will need to change more than the headline to have a higher likelihood of resulting in a statistically valid result. Our next test will be to change the body copy to emphasize what subscribers will experience as a result of watching these videos.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the Course on Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because it removes anxiety around giving up an email. because  it removes anxiety around giving up an email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/25/2023
The IMB’s Course on Prayer offer was experiencing a low sign-up rate on the landing page. We hypothesized that eliminating anxiety with a simple security callout at the bottom of the form would reassure users by clarifying what their information will be used for. We believe a reminder to the user of why their email is being requested (to receive their course lessons) will increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment should not be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Ultimately, a very small lift with a low level of confidence shows that this treatment did not cause much of a difference for users arriving at this landing page. More testing is required, but this also prompts us to explore what other factors could be causing anxiety/friction to email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to have a larger sample size and a higher level of confidence before making any decisions based on the results. It may also be beneficial to test alternative forms of security callouts or messaging to determine which resonates best with visitors.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition language on a content offer landing page impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because they know what they will be helping achieve by donating..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. As part of our efforts to drive new name and new donor acquisition through content offers, we wanted to run a test on landing page copy to see whether being more concise with what a donation would get would impact conversion rates. This test ran across all acquisition offers on all channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to potential donors may not necessarily result in an increase in conversions. The treatment resulted in a 6.2% decrease in donations with a 40.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better. However, since the statistical level of confidence falls below 85%, we cannot consider these results valid.
For future experiments, we can continue to test which specific points of clarity will help increase donor conversions. Since Hoover is a think tank, the immediate connection between a donation and a tangible impact is a harder case to make; continued language testing will help clarify that.

experiments.title: How addressing a felt need in landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the felt need for prospective ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate  because relevancy to the individual’s needs strengthens the value proposition for the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/13/2023
IMB was experiencing a relatively low sign up rate on an offer landing page. To address this, we developed a treatment for this landing page that would better address a person’s felt need. The control for the experiment starts the page with a declarative statement to take action. The treatment invites the person to think about why they would want to sign up for this online course and provides ways that this offer will fulfill those needs. Our hypothesis is that the language that unpacks the “why” would result in more emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of prospective customers can lead to a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment that focused on the "why" behind signing up for the online course instead of just encouraging action saw a 4.4% increase in email acquisitions compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.3%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid for decision making.
Additionally, downstream we saw that donor conversion rate was negatively affected by the copy (though also not a valid result).
It's likely that this low impact is because the felt need is already addressed at the ad level (where most traffic to this page is coming from). Therefore, people weren't more motivated by this language on this landing page.

experiments.title: How including student testimonials on a course landing page impacted signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page will increase course sign ups because it establishes more credibility for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2023 - 04/01/2023
In an effort to increase the number of course sign ups on the our Genesis course sign up landing page, we wanted to know how including testimonials of previous students on the course sign up page would impact the number of individuals who enrolled in our Genesis course.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment variants. The control version modeled the standard landing page format for all courses, while the treatment version included a text box of two different quotes regarding this course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment aimed to determine the impact of including student testimonials on the Genesis course signup landing page to increase course signups. Results showed that the treatment resulted in a 4.9% decrease in course signups with a statistical level of confidence of 63.7%. Although the control variant won, the results cannot be considered valid since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of setting a higher statistical level of confidence for more valid results, considering the impact on specific audience segments, and the need for further experimentation on the use of testimonials on course signup landing pages. Future experimentation could focus on testing different variations of testimonials, targeting specific audience segments, and testing other elements on the landing page that could impact course signups.
We chose to end this experiment before the 30 day mark due to results consistently being in the negatives.
The key learning from this experiment is that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page may not always be effective in increasing course sign ups, and further testing will be necessary.

experiments.title: How an alternate motive conveyed in the course landing page headline impacted course sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the headline on personal application and self-improvement for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because visitors will be more interested in how the course can improve their life in faith rather than deepening their knowledge of a book of the bible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2023 - 03/26/2023
The Dallas Theological Seminary’s James course landing page is producing a 19.1% sign-up rate and $7.57 gross cost per name – both metrics are missing their benchmark. In an attempt to improve these results, we’ve developed a landing page test that tests what the user hopes to get out of the course.
Looking at the current headline, we wonder if it effectively speaks to the user’s desire. Do they want to deepen their knowledge of a book of the bible or do they want to take the overall message of the course and apply it to their life in faith? Are they looking for knowledge or self-improvement?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version that emphasized action and how the course could improve the visitor's life in faith resulted in a 3.5% increase in course signups, albeit with a statistical level of confidence of only 61.9%. This means that the findings cannot be deemed significant or conclusive for future decision-making.
Moving further down the funnel, the treatment lifted donation conversion by 6.2% but with a 16.6% level of confidence. And revenue experienced a 16% decrease through the treatment with a 70% level of confidence. None of the metrics validated through this test.
The key learning from this experiment is that a headline that emphasizes personal application and self-improvement could increase course signup rates, but the data overall is not consistent or significant enough to determine an optimal approach through this headline test. The collective audience in the test did not lean favorably in either direction when it comes to desirability or motivation to sign up for the course. The next test we run on this page should have more significant changes to trigger more conversions and a more significant level of confidence.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: The impact on donor conversion of adding language about protecting subscriber privacy to a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reassuring subscribers that their data will be kept private will reduce mental friction and increase downstream donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2022 - 09/26/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They use free content offers to acquire donors on Facebook. One of their most popular offers is 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Children in Your Life . They wanted to see if they could increase donor conversion by adding a sentence about subscriber privacy to the form.  in 2017, they tested this approach and found no increase in email conversion, however tests with other organizations found that this language would could increase downstream donor conversion.
They cloned the landing page and added this just below the form: “We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to deliver your devotion.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for two months there was no significant impact on downstream donor conversion by adding the privacy language to the landing page. When looking at email conversion there was also no significant difference between the control and treatment pages. While other organizations have found with their visitors that this language can increase donor conversion, the audience for this offer is not motivated either way by this additional value proposition.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a premium landing page impacted donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop langauge for premium-resource driven donors will achieve a change in conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Their featured offer in May was Invincible: Conquering the Mountains That Separate You from the Blessed Life.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this specific offer, adding in value proposition language to the landing page for the book led to a decrease in both conversion rates and revenue per donor of 14.2% and 8.3% respectively. Despite an increase in average gift, the overall result was a decline in revenue for the experiment variant page.
Our hypothesis is that the majority of traffic to these book offer pages is driven directly from TV and radio programs promoting the book; those that reach that page want to purchase the book and don't have that much of an interest in value proposition.
However, it will be interesting to keep track of the 'value prop' donors long term, as our hypothesis is that by buying into the value prop of WHY they should donate to PTV instead of another ministry, they will become better long-term donors, and not just those looking to buy one book and that is the extent of their support.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity into a headline affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline of a content offer will result in a higher number of email conversions
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
I Am Second is committed to helping people be authentic and genuine—both with God and with others. For this experiment, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of emails they collect on a content offer landing page. They hypothesized that a headline that gave more clarity into what the subscriber would receive would yield a higher number of emails than the control that was good, but lacked full clarity into what the reader would actually gain by downloading the resource.
experiments.keyLearnings: As hypothesized, the headline clarity resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisitions. Although greater than the control, due to the experiment not getting at least a 95% LoC or greater, we cannot say with full certainty that the treatment had a true measurable effect. In addition, we normally only like to make one change to an experiment to isolate results...and this particular experiment changed both the headline and added an additional supporting sub-headine. While one could argue that the sub-headline further aligned with the headline, providing even more clarity...it cannot be proven as this test had too many variable changed. For future tests, it would be better only to change one element, or if time/urgency is a factor, do a three-way test that has a control, and two variants to see if both of these changes actually add to the increase. It is also important to note that this organization rarely gets enough traffic actually to validate tests with true statistical significance, but even still there are learnings to be had...as shown above.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity and recency copy to a headline on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into a landing page headline, and aligning the message with recent events will result in higher email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 08/27/2022
e3 Partners equips believers to take ownership of the Great Commission. They send believers into local and global communities to evangelize the lost, and they partner with believers to establish new churches across the globe. For this experiment, e3 wanted to test adding clarity and recency copy to their headline on an email acquisition landing page. The control headline had been used for a while and had good results, but after seeing other experiments that tested tried and true content offer copy, they decided to try it on their previously good performing content offer – prayers for the overwhelmed. Since this test ran during a period of societal uncertainty with recent tragedies in the news, inflation fears, and world unrest, we felt it was a prime opportunity to adjust the headline copy to reflect these worldwide events, while still remaining generic enough to stay evergreen.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not have enough traffic to validate the experiment, we did see a 206% increase in conversions for the treatment with a 69% level of confidence. This was a very small sample size, but we did not see a negative effect on any of the channels. Due to this, we decided to role this out oas the new control. The next steps in the experiment might be to test some tactical elements, like testimonials or social proof that aligns with the recency copy.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving experience from 3 steps to 2 steps increases donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the steps to donate for ebook downloaders will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2022 - 06/27/2022
Since changing giving platforms, the Navigators giving experience is now a 3 step process after requesting an ebook on a landing page. After a site visitor fills out the form to get an offer, they’re taken to a landing page with the value prop language and button on the bottom to give. When people click on the button they’re taken to a generic giving page with a gift array. They are then given the option to “add to basket” and are taken to a page to fill out their credit card information. Our hypothesis is that if we can reduce this experience down to 2 steps, site visitors will experience less friction and we’ll see a higher donor conversion rate.
To test this concept we ran this experiment through a specific funnel/offer: “Fight on my knees” ebook. Traffic to this funnel is mainly driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 65% lift in donor conversion rate but we only saw a level of confidence of 53.9% which means this experiment did not validate. Even after running for over a month, this funnel did not get enough traffic in order to acquire a large enough sample size. It is recommended that this either retested in a different funnel that has more traffic or we test using a 1 page donation process if at all possible.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and more clarity to a landing page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the headline and adding social proof in the form of a testimonial would result in a higher number of email conversions for people that land on this campaign landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 06/28/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof in the form of a testimonial, and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around a 27% for the treatment.

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 28%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof in the form of a testimonial were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How aligning the messaging of a landing page to recent news events affects the conversion rate of page that has historically performed well
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding recency copy to the headline and body copy for an email sign up page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign had been steady for a long time, as this offer has been part of the Focus on the Family ecosystem for quite some time, but there is always room for improvement.
Due to recent tragic events that transpired at the time of this experiment, we felt we could align the messaging of this offer to the headlines of the day…without specifically calling out the tragedy itself. We wanted the messaging to remain evergreen, even if the treatment won out. We decided to test if adding recency copy to the headline and body copy would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a 38% lift in email conversions for the treatment, with a 52% level of confidence.
With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 52%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the recency copy that aligned with the news headlines of the time would have continued to outperform the control. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions during the short timeframe, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
The copy points, while aligned with the recent headlines, still remain evergreen enough to keep live, and could continue to see gains in conversions if we make this treatment the new control.

experiments.title: How aligning the graphic elements from an ad with the graphic elements on a landing page affect conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that matching the creative assets from the ad running on social media to the graphics on the landing page will result in higher conversions for visitors to the email landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs).
It is common for this team to update the ad creative in Facebook if we see that the ad conversion rates could use improvement. It is not as common to also have the graphics from the campaign match exactly the images on the landing page. While the change in creative helped the ad conversion rates at the “top of the funnel”, we noticed the signup conversion rates on this campaign (in the “middle of the funnel”) began to decline after making the creative switch. We felt this was an opportunity to test creative congruence between the new ad creative and the existing landing page.
For this particular experiment, in the treatment, we changed out creative assets on the email sign up page to match that of the new ad creative running on Facebook, and we made a slight tweak to the headline copy to add more clarity. The control was left as is.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw very little difference between the control and the treatment. In fact, the control (with a different graphic treatment between the ad and the landing page) was slightly ahead on email conversions (by just 3).

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and graphic treatment). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 10%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the graphic treatment caused the small decrease in conversions. We can say that we gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages. In the end, there were really no positive or adverse effects to making the creative match.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity to a landing page headline affects sign up conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline for readers that land on this page will achieve a higher number of email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw a decrease of around a 27% for the treatment, with a 27% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and increased emphasis on the call to action text - not shown in the screenshot).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 27%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the increased emphasis on the CTA were the cause of the drop in conversions. We can say that we saw a decrease in conversions and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and headline clarity affect email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and headline clarity  for readers of a landing page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof under a landing page image and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around 2% for the treatment with a 20% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 20%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned landing page impacted email acquisition for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity and credibility through imagery for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in online course subscribers.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/21/2022
On a past eBook offer, we had seen a highly visual landing page increase email conversion so we wanted to test the same hypothesis on an online course offer. We developed a treatment that highlighted an image of Tim Keller, one of the instructors featured in the course, to add some clarity and credibility to the offer. We split traffic between the plain-text control vs. the radically redesigned layout of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw there was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rate with just a 23.3% level of confidence so the test is not valid and the results were flat. One interesting thing to note is when we looked downstream one step further into the funnel, donations experienced a 68.2% decrease with a 86% level of confidence in the treatment, indicating a directional decline. We believe this is because the highly designed landing page doesn't have enough synergy with the plain-text donation page that follows it, making the control landing page more congruent with the experience for each step. Next, we will test adding the Faith & Work course graphic on the plain-text page to see if adding a visual element will help indicate to visitors they are taking the correct next step to get the offer and increase email acquisition.

experiments.title: How a landing page layout redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 04/15/2022
Through previous testing, we had found a winning version of the landing page but we identified a few changes we could implement to further optimize and test to achieve a higher conversion rate on the page. We developed a treatment that moved the eBook image to the right side of the page, relocated the supporting value proposition text/”reasons to download” from the bottom of the page into bullet points above the form, and added this copy leading into the form: “Tell us where to send your free copy now:”
experiments.keyLearnings: We ran the experiment for one month and only achieved a 23.2% level of confidence that there was a 0.67% decrease in email acquisition on the page so the results are not valid. However, we did observe a valid increase in acquisition with some specific segments in the experiment that are worth noting: Email visitors had a 52.2% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. 45-54 aged visitors had an 18.3% increase in email acquisitions with a 96% level of confidence, and 55-64 aged visitors had a 34.6% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. Overall, what we learned was that putting the form lower on the page by increasing the text ahead of the call to action could have been causing an adverse effect on conversion for some visitors. For future testing, we will shorten the copy ahead of the form to see if the length of the copy is interrupting the experience and causing friction, and distracting from the intended call to action.

experiments.title: How the location of the email capture impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for name and email prior to starting the survey for email subscribers will achieve increased survey completion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/18/2021
American Cornerstone Institute was launching a survey on the state of education in America. This was part of a larger campaign to promote their Little Patriots educational curriculum. The first phase included sending this survey to their email house file.
We had a theory that the position of the name and email capture may influence the likelihood of someone to complete the survey. This is something that is normally reserved to the end of the survey but, we believed that this might actually cause greater frustration and be viewed as a bait and switch. We decided to test this hypothesis with this initial launch.
experiments.keyLearnings: After collecting over 800 submissions, we found that there was no statistically significant difference when asking for the email address prior to starting a survey or at the end. This unlocks the possibility of capturing the email address prior to form submission so that we can send "abandon cart" emails for people who begin the survey but don't finish.

experiments.title: How changing the value prop to focus more on the speaker affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy around Ted Cruz for those that visit the page will achieve a increase in conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2021 - 05/01/2021
This experiment was run on the registration page of their WWCB that was promoted on both Facebook and in email prospecting. The control copy focused more on the WWCB event itself with special guest Ted Cruz. With the Treatment copy, we focused more on Ted Cruz and specifically what he was going to be talking about on the call.
The thought behind this is people would be more interested and it would have more pull if we talked about what Ted Cruz would be discussing during the WWCB rather than talking about the event itself which happens every week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this did not validate we did see a 15% increase in conversion rate. The control saw a 3.1% conversion rate while the treatment saw a 3.5% instant conversion rate.

By focusing on Ted Cruz specifically and adding a bullet point list of what he was going to cover and talk about during the WWCB event we saw it lead to an increase in conversion rate and revenue as a whole. We will test this value prop again for future registrations to see if we continue to see similar results and if this will validate.

 

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
25-34 visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a landing page with a seasonal offer impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2021 - 04/08/2021
In the weeks leading up to Easter, a sticky bar on the homepage was pointing visitors to a landing page where they could sign up for a free resource offer with Easter-focused family activities. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the landing page to see if we could increase overall email acquisition on this offer. We developed a treatment that removed the traditional footer with links to alternate pages on the site to avoid distraction from the intended call to action, which was to submit the email acquisition form and sign up for the free PDF.
We also revised the header on the page from “Adventures in Odyssey: Easter Faith Building Activities!” to “Get access to your free copy now and help your family celebrate Easter together with this free PDF of fun ideas for activities and devotionals to prompt meaningful conversations!” in an effort to make the offer less organization-focused and more focused on the page visitor.
The third element we changed for the treatment version of the page was enhancing the overall value proposition and added in language that helped address the why behind someone might benefit from the contents of the free guide rather than only sharing what was inside. We spoke directly to the felt needs of the audience and the emotions they might be experiencing as a parent and how this resource could alleviate some of the stress that often accompanies the anticipation of the Easter holiday.
Finally, we changed the CTA on the form from “To get your FREE download of Easter Faith Building Activities, fill out the form below” to “Tell us where to send your FREE copy of Adventure in Odyssey’s Easter Faith Building Activities:” and switched the button copy from “Download Here” to “Send my free copy »” to make this portion feel like less work for the user. We set up the control and the treatment to run as an A/B test and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we didn't have enough run-time to acquire the number of visitors to the page needed to validate results and we only reached a 35% level of confidence on the experiment. However, what we saw was a few observations on specific visitor segments that are outlined below. Even though we did not reach statistical significance overall, we can use these learnings to guide the development of language for future tests that target display/tablet visitors and speak more relevantly to females and those who are 65+. We could also use this knowledge to our advantage by keeping this audience in mind for introducing social proof on a landing page by adding a testimonial quote from a 65+ female who found the resource valuable. We can also test each of these items from the radical redesign individually on other free offers in the future to have more insight into which changes led to these increases and decreases per segment.
Desktop visitors had a 31.7% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Display visitors had a 42.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 34.9% decrease in outbound link click with a 94% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 33.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 28.5% decrease in outbound link click with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 32.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 39.5% increase in outbound link click with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 72.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had an 86.2% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 1,211.5% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 34.0% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How including author information affects video series sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Including information on the author/speaker will increase signups and email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2021 - 05/03/2021
Focus on the Family was releasing a new video series and they wanted to see what the impact of including information on the speaker would be on email conversion. The thought was that the broad audience that lands on the sign up page may not recognize the name of the speaker and so knowing more about them would convince the readers to sign up. This was a 50/50 split test and the primary traffic was from social media.
(N.B. The client set up the variable page and because of the nature of the test set up, the variable shows as the control.)
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means including information for the speaker is not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that the placing the information below the decision point of the submission button made the information a second thought and so did not have an impact.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that the placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift through feelings and outcomes impact conversion on a sponsorship page
experiments.researchQuestion: Focusing on the feeling and affects of the problem and showing the change that a donor’s gift will make will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their main sponsorship page, where the audience has the opportunity to see the different children who need a sponsor. It is important to note that this is not the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor a particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings: The outcome of this test showed that this communication tactic does not affect sponsorship conversion by itself. Our hypothesis is this is not a page where people make the decision to sponsor, but to see the different children that they could sponsor. Since this page is not a decision point, a treatment on this page will not have enough conversion to validate with this level of traffic. The next step is to focus on the child specific pages.

experiments.title: How including author information affects video series sign up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Including information on the author/speaker will increase signups and email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2021 - 05/03/2021
Focus on the Family was releasing a new video series and they wanted to see what the impact of including information on the speaker would be on email conversion. The thought was that the broad audience that lands on the sign up page may not recognize the name of the speaker and so knowing more about them would convince the readers to sign up. This was a 50/50 split test and the primary traffic was from social media.
(N.B. The client set up the variable page and because of the nature of the test set up, the variable shows as treatment.)
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means including information for the speaker is not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that the placing the information below the decision point of the submission button made the information a second thought and so did not have an impact.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a premium offer impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that expanding the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2019 - 12/16/2019
For the month of December, a special premium was being offered with a membership purchase. When evaluating the landing page copy, we wanted to develop a treatment that would increase conversions to the membership page. The control jumped right into the details about the premium and membership without giving a clear motivation for considering a Club Membership. We expanded the value proposition with a paragraph to enhance the value-add before launching into the and details of the Club benefits and premium information.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we only had a short window to direct traffic to the treatment landing page. That being said, the experiment did not receive enough traffic to validate fully, but we did find that mobile visitors had a 20.1% increase in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence. We'll continue to find opportunities to introduce similar language on other landing pages that have a longer life span and see if we can observe a valid lift on conversions in the future on other premium offers.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 60.6% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 23.6% decrease in outbound link click with an 90% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 131.4% increase in outbound link click with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How aligning the image and value proposition for a content offer affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the image with the offer and increasing the clarity of the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2021 - 06/12/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey resource through Facebook ads. Their cost-per-name was above what they projected, and they wanted to see how they could optimize their results. A previous test had shown that an image that was more specific to the offer greatly increased conversion rate and CPA, so they wanted to apply that to the landing page as well. Additionally, they wanted to test the content on the landing page to see if increasing clarity of the offer and removing vertical space could increase conversion.
They created two treatments and ran an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither landing page made a significant difference in conversion rate. There was directional evidence that the new image provided a slight bump to conversion rate, but it wasn't near significance.
The 29% conversion rate on the landing page is still low compared to other Navigators offers, but this test suggests that the issue is upstream from the landing page—perhaps in the targeting or in the advertising.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the order of login options for mobile visitors will achieve an increase in user signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2021 - 04/03/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
The desktop version of this test showed a decrease in signups—however, the mobile showed promise. So they decided to isolate the mobile test to keep it running longer and cut their losses on desktop signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, this experiment failed to produce a winning treatment with any difference. While there was a small directional lift, it was not close to statistical validity. The team moved on to higher priority tests to drive more new users.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that this placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How emotional impact language on the tribute widget affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding emotional impact language for personal home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2021 - 05/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute widget was a primary source of donations for people who visited their journals. Over the years, they had optimized the copy to maximize conversion.
However, as they reviewed the latest control, they wondered if they could better convey the emotional impact that a journal provides for someone who is going through a health journey, and if that would translate into more gifts or more revenue.
They created a treatment that made two tweaks to the copy. First, they changed the headline to include the impact, as well as the result. Instead of just “Help [FirstName] Stay Connected To Family and Friends”, they added the effect: “Help [FirstName] Experience the Comfort of Staying Connected To Family and Friends”. Then, in the body copy, they inserted language that referred to the potential feeling of loss the author might feel without Caringbridge: “A $30 donation to CaringBridge means that [FirstName] won’t lose this special connection to family and friends.
Then, they split traffic to see the impact of this new emotional impact language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, there was no impact on conversion rate. This suggests a few potential reasons why:
The reader might have already understood the emotional impact, and the specific callout only brought that to the surface—it wasn't missing. Any impact the emotional language might have had could have been blunted by the additional copy. The copy was ultimately not that different from the original.
Either way, this experiment failed to move the needle, and more radical approaches must be taken to improve conversion.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: How a form on the right side of the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: A right side form could harm conversion rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 06/04/2021
Focus’s web team wanted to roll out a new landing page format with the form on the right side of the page instead of the bottom. We wanted to assure that this did no harm to email acquisition. So we tested this on a new offer they were launching called Preparing Your Kids for the Teen Years.
experiments.keyLearnings: The majority of the traffic and conversions were on a mobile device. This test showed no results because on a mobile device it is almost exactly the same user experience. So we are going to retest this format on a high traffic page to only desktop users.

experiments.title: How adding an “About Us” section to the landing page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of an “about us” section to tell the organization’s history and beliefs to the landing page impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2020 - 10/03/2020
As a part of promoting a new content offer, The Fund for American Studies wondered whether telling more about their organization’s history would help boost email signups, but most importantly, whether or not it would increase the donor conversion rate after someone had signed up to request the resource they were offering.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 61% level of confidence, we observed a -23.7% decrease in donor conversion rate. This was subsequently driving a similar decrease in revenue, as well.
We did observe roughly a directional increase of +8.5% in email signup rates, but we decided to turn this off and focus experimentation elsewhere.
Further experimentation may be required, as we did notice an uptick in revenue from returning visitors with the treatment, but decreases in new visitors. This may be driven by new visitors largely being motivated by the offer, instead of the organization. Perhaps adding this description to the donation page may be a better place within the funnel for experimentation next.

experiments.title: How a new webinar landing page design affects registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a time/date bar on the landing page affect webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
One of the most common replies we get to webinar notification emails is people trying to clarify what time the webinar is actually at. Despite our best efforts to mention the time and time zone in every piece of communication we send, there still seemed to always be some level of confusion or clarity needed.
To accommodate for this, we added a time/date bar and widened the column of the template in the treatment version.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no change in registration rate as a result of this experiment. In this case, we see that as a success. We were able to make the time/date of the webinar much clearer and more apparent without diluting or distracting from the primary value proposition.

experiments.title: How more direct copy affected donations on a landing page

rows.name: Variant 1 -- copy test, more direct copy

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct headline that tells people what they just did (and more direct body copy) increase the number of donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2019 - 08/26/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation sent chase emails to their file for a direct mail letter about ending the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare). They then versioned those chase emails to send to the rest of their file. They wanted to test the donation page copy to see if a more direct headline and explanation of what TPPF is doing to combat Obamacare would increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: This copy experiment led to a 27.1% decrease in donations. However, there are several factors in the data that make these experiment results inconclusive. The sample size is too small, there is only a one gift difference, and the level of confidence is too low to come close to validating. Given this information, the experiment results cannot be used for future communications to the TPPF donor file.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a premium added to a landing page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2018 - 06/01/2018
Compassion International wanted to test the impact of a premium on sponsorship conversion. They had a partnership with Chip and Joanna Gaines that allowed them to offer a book as a premium as a way to thank the donor for their sponsorship.
They added this premium to the sponsorship pages specifically around their remarketing campaigns driven from Facebook and launched an A/B test to determine the impact for conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the premium offer to the donation page produced a slight lift (8%) with no statistical confidence. This lack of variance seemed to indicate that by the time the prospect had reached the page, they had already made up their mind to give and weren’t affected by the premium.

The Compassion team decided to launch another test after this that moved the premium offer upstream in the communication chain to see if the premium would attract more traffic from the Facebook ad itself, and would result in a higher downstream conversion rate.

experiments.title: How the landing page design can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another. Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as "approaching validity." This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How creating unique landing pages for every email in a campaign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will creating unique landing pages to tie to each email theme increase the donors to a money bomb campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2016 - 04/12/2016
During a SBA List money bomb, we wanted to see if they could increase the number of donations by creating unique landing pages for each email theme. Each email focused on a specific theme taken from their all-encompassing general value proposition. Besides wanting to increase the number of donors through reinforcing the message and theme from email to landing page, SBA List also wanted to see which theme of their value proposition, donors would respond to most. We tested these unique landing pages against a general, all-encompassing value proposition landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren’t able to validate the test, initial results showed that unique landing pages for each email theme did not help increase donations. The general value proposition landing page was able to convert donors at a higher rate. These initial findings tell us that SBA List donors aren’t more motivated by one theme over another and instead are motivated by all that SBA List does. These learnings were implemented for the remainder of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a change in tone of the landing page affects the performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will yield the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find the best possible messaging tone to drive an increase in donations and revenue. A previous experiment using member benefits had not produced any significant lift so we decided to take a more radical approach at test the tone used on the page.
Our hypothesis was that landing page that more clearly laid out the problem and emphasized how the potential donor (instead of the organization) could help solve it would convert better. We also removed the 3rd party credibility indicators since, after further consideration, those may have confused the message.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither landing page was able to produce a significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue. This would suggest that the tone of the message was not influencing the campaign as much as the core message itself. For future campaigns, it would be best to take a more radical approach and test the entire messaging platform before getting to the final days of the campaign.

experiments.title: How the stories told on a landing page affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Given the altruistic nature of volunteers, focusing on the impact they will have on others will appeal more to visitors than the impact the volunteering will have on them.  As a result, this new treatment will increase the number of names acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2012 - 10/03/2012
The Global Volunteer Network was looking for a way to acquire more applications for their volunteer program.  The existing landing page focused on the experience the volunteers would go through and how it would transform their lives. We wanted to discover what motivated volunteers to sign up so we created a new treatment that talked about the impact they would have on others instead of what volunteering would do for them.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not reach the required sample size or a statistical level of confidence in the time that this test ran.  While it would appear that talking about the impact they would have on others increased conversions, the lift was not significant enough to be valid.  We would recommend further testing of the idea before rolling it out across the site.

experiments.title: How adding gift handles to a landing page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a suggested giving amount increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2019 - 03/02/2019
Hillsdale College recently mailed out thousands of letters to school administrators and teachers across America. In these letters, they offered to send a free pocket-sized copy of the U.S. Constitution for every single child in every classroom. Hillsdale received an astonishing number of replies requesting copies of the Constitution. To fund the shipment to all of these schools, Hillsdale launched a four-day email campaign asking donors to send a donation to help send pocket-sized Constitutions to schoolchildren across the U.S. They wanted to know if donations would increase on the landing page with the addition of a brief explanation of how many pocket Constitutions could be sent to children with a certain donation amount. They thought that if they suggested a giving amount next to how many pocket Constitutions could be sent with that amount, it would help the donor make a decision that they would otherwise have to make on their own. They hypothesized that telling people the impact a gift amount would make would inspire people to give generously, thus increasing donations.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Hillsdale team saw a 7.2% drop in conversion with the gift handle treatment. This was after sending all traffic from the second email of the campaign to the treatment landing page, which suggests that there would be an even larger decrease in conversion had the traffic been split evenly in that second email. After the test had run for three days, the Hillsdale team decided to end the test in favor of launching a new test against the control: the addition of a photo in the landing page. This new experiment can be found here.

experiments.title: How a simplified landing page and added imagery affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a radically redesigned landing page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2018 - 12/03/2018
Heartlight Ministries is running a free online course called Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, and they wanted to test a new copy approach as well as adding a photo of Mark to reinforce expertise and better connect with the reader. This test was a radical redesign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page did not get any valid results based on the low sample size, but the treatment showed a 26% increase in conversion rate and was therefore rolled out 100% to the page.

experiments.title: How more visual and design elements on a landing page affect downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding more design and visual elements lead to more downloads?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2019
NextAfter produces a number of reports, studies, and eBooks each year and has a ‘template’ for the download landing page which is very simple and copy heavy.
