With The Canadian Online Fundraising Scorecard there were some strong visuals and infographics within the report so we wondered if we shared more of the visual components on the landing page and gave the landing page a bit more of an aesthetic that matched the report itself if people would be more willing to download the report.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was a very slight increase in downloads on the more designed version so it is worth looking at. The treatment had a signup option right away as opposed to the control where you have to scroll/read before you provide your information which means you may choose to download, or not, before you experience the offer or value so we could keep more of the conversational flow similar while adding more visual elements to see if that makes a difference. The visitor also has a pretty high motivation when they hit the page so perhaps the quick download added value. Again, worth looking at in future experiments.

experiments.title: How simplifying a landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does simplifying the copy on a page bring clarity to the value proposition and increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2018 - 07/05/2018
Buckner International was offering a free parenting series course and wanted to try to increase conversions on the landing page. They hypothesized that simplifying the landing page by taking out some of the copy and clarifying the value proposition would increase conversion. They also took out many of the Buckner references and focused more on the parent the content was speaking to.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the experiment produced a 5% directional lift, there was no statistically valid learnings from this experiment. Since the page was already pretty highly converting once people got to the page, we recommend testing other parts of the funnel to significantly alter conversion rate and cost.

experiments.title: How adding a premium offer to a landing page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a premium added to a landing page affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2018 - 06/01/2018
Compassion International wanted to test the impact of a premium on sponsorship conversion. They had a partnership with Chip and Joanna Gaines that allowed them to offer a book as a premium as a way to thank the donor for their sponsorship.
They added this premium to the sponsorship pages specifically around their remarketing campaigns driven from Facebook and launched an A/B test to determine the impact for conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the premium offer to the donation page produced a slight lift (8%) with no statistical confidence. This lack of variance seemed to indicate that by the time the prospect had reached the page, they had already made up their mind to give and weren’t affected by the premium.

The Compassion team decided to launch another test after this that moved the premium offer upstream in the communication chain to see if the premium would attract more traffic from the Facebook ad itself, and would result in a higher downstream conversion rate.

experiments.title: Does Adding the Word “Free” to a Landing Page Subhead Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding “free” language to the offer page subhead increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/17/2018
Our landing pages for The Hoover Institution’s ebook offers typically focused on simply explaining the benefits of the offer content rather than emphasizing that they are getting it the content for free. Since the typical Hoover donor is usually an older, intellectual, middle-to-upper class demographic, our assumption was that they would likely view a “free” offer as having less value than otherwise. However, we decided to test this hypothesis just in case our assumptions were incorrect.
experiments.keyLearnings: As there was virtually no statistical difference, we concluded that adding "free" language to the subhead was unnecessary to the offer's success. It was not necessarily hurting the value of the offer, but it was also not compelling enough to increase our conversion rate. In this case, we would rather stick with simply relying on the value proposition of the offer to sell itself.

experiments.title: How adding a third party testimonial to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a testimonial from a well-known figure to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
Heartlight Ministries launched an offer called When Your Teen is Struggling, and wanted to test whether including a testimonial from a well-known Christian author, Joe White, would increase conversion on the landing page. The rest of the copy and elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The decrease of 4% on the treatment page was not statistically valid, but it’s possible that the placement of the testimonial distracted people’s eyes from the line of copy, or that the author of the quote was not influential enough to have a strong effect on conversion rate.
This concept would need to be tested again to be a statistically valid learning, and might be more impactful if multiple statements were used.

experiments.title: How navigation impact donor conversion rate on landing pages
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing navigational elements increase instant donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2017 - 07/18/2017
With the launch of the Oblates Celebrating Mercy online course, we wanted to test out some to the more tactical elements of their donation page. Specifically, we had the hypothesis that the navigational elements that were a part of the standard template were causing significant friction to the donation process. We decided to test removing the elements to see if that increased instant donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month an a half of running, we found that removing the navigation did not have any statistically significant impact on donor conversion. When digging deeper into these results, one of the elements that jumped out at us was the overall low instant donor conversion. Based upon the low rate for both the control and treatment, it would seem that we needed to shift our focus away from reducing friction and begin testing into the right value proposition.

experiments.title: How emotion-driven copy affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive copy increase donation revenue on a tribute donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2017 - 08/31/2017
CaringBridge had previously run an experiment on the journal pages of their site that produced a big lift  in revenue when “emotive” language was shown. They wanted to run an additional test to see if the same sort of emotive language would produce an increase in revenue on the tribute donation page. Since the the winning copy from the previous experiment wouldn’t deploy for a few weeks, they had a chance to test the copy on the tribute page independently. They hypothesized that if they could also get a lift on the donation page, then they might be able to have a compound lift from the two areas of improvement.
They created a treatment donation page that utilized the “love and support” language and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page failed to produce any significant difference from the control donation page. This shows that while the emotive language might be a great motivator to start the gift process, it doesn’t have a significant effect at finishing the donation process. It also may show a relationship between the emotive language in the donate box and the standard control language on the donation page—these two copy variations may be working in tandem to produce a higher revenue total.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy policy to a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding privacy language to a landing page increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/22/2017
Buckner International was running an email acquisition campaign with their 7 Scriptures offer. They wanted to see if adding privacy language around the conversion form would increase conversion rate due to a higher perceived sense of security for downloaders. All other elements of the landing page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that conversion rate stayed pretty constant between the two landing pages, with the privacy policy page showing little to no difference in conversion rate. It’s possible that a privacy policy does affect people’s perception of the security of the offer, but not having one does not deter people from downloading either.

experiments.title: How more concise value proposition affects email conversion rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more concise value proposition increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2017 - 09/25/2017
Buckner International was running a test on an acquisition offer that offered free scriptures to pray for your kids. They wanted to test if a shortened, more concise value proposition on the landing page would increase conversion rate. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test did not validate, and we saw only a minor decrease in conversion rate from the shortened copy. This could be tested further with a more radical test to figure out if people are deterred by length of copy, or more encouraged to download with a more built-out value proposition for the offer.

experiments.title: How a supporting image affects email acquisition rate on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing a supporting image increase email acquisition rate on a landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/19/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom recently launched a campaign to rally support around Jack Phillips–one of the clients they are defending at the Supreme Court. They found that adding additional copy to their facebook ads did not increase or decrease clickthrough rate. They wondered: Will removing the image of Jack Phillips on the email acquisition page increase the overall email acquisition rate? They created a treatment version (without the image of Jack) and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Alliance Defending Freedom found that the the treatment version actually had no statistically significant effect on email acquisition. In other words, we saw no difference in conversion on the page that lacked the supporting image. This is an interesting learning for all of us to consider, as we build new campaign pages. This test suggests that the presence (or lack thereof) of a smaller image doesn’t have a dramatic impact on visitor motivation.
However, it’s also important to note that the conversion rate for this page was much higher than the average acquisition page (converting over 70% of all visitors). Motivation may have been so high, in fact, that it overpowered any page elements that would have otherwise affected page users. Thus, Alliance Defending Freedom will continue to test the image element in order to be certain of its impact.

experiments.title: How a new landing page design impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the new redesigned page and donation form impact donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2016 - 11/10/2016
The Heritage.org website is in the process of a redesign. As part of this transition, it was suggested that the existing donation page design be migrated to the new format. This would include the transition to a new look and feel for the donation form itself. Before making this change, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on the performance of the donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for the length of a money bomb campaign, we determined that the new design had no statistically significant impact on the performance of the campaign. While this is reassuring, it has led to two more questions:
How does the donation form design specifically impact conversion? We want to isolate this factor to ensure the more modern design is not having a negative impact on the older donor base.
Once the new site launches, will this new design actually increase conversion by maintaining visual congruence.
Both of these questions will be tested in upcoming campaigns.

experiments.title: How the landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another.
Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as “approaching validity.” This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How the landing page imagery affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the use of imagery help boost donor conversion on the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2015 - 08/31/2015
In a previous experiment, we had validated the messaging approach to use for a money bomb campaign. Because it involved a physical building, we felt this campaign would lend itself better to images to help convey the message. This is a different approach than we normally take with money bombs so we wanted to be sure to validate the impact these images had on the overall campaign performance
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the images to the page created no significant lift to either donor conversion or average gift. They made the page more visually appealing but this didn’t impact the donors’ likelihood to give a gift.
